Patterns›Array / String›Problem 1 of 150
1

Merge Sorted Array

Easy

Problem. You are given two sorted integer arrays, nums1 and nums2. nums1 has length m + n: the first m slots hold its real values and the last n slots are zero-padding placeholders. nums2 holds n values. Merge nums2 into nums1 so that nums1 becomes one sorted array. You must do it in place (modify nums1 directly; you do not return anything).

Example. nums1 = [1,2,3,0,0,0], m = 3, nums2 = [2,5,6], n = 3 → nums1 = [1,2,2,3,5,6]. Trace: the real values are 1,2,3; the three zeros are space for nums2's 2,5,6. After merging the six values in sorted order we get 1,2,2,3,5,6.

Brute Force. The simplest idea: copy all n values from nums2 into the trailing slots of nums1, then sort the whole thing with the standard library. This ignores the fact that both arrays are already sorted, but it definitely works.

class Solution {
public:
    void merge(vector<int>& nums1, int m, vector<int>& nums2, int n) {
        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
            nums1[m + i] = nums2[i];
        }
        sort(nums1.begin(), nums1.end());
    }
};

Time: O((m+n) log(m+n)), Space: O(1). The copy is linear but the sort dominates; sorting m+n elements costs log-linear time. No extra array is allocated.

Optimized. The key insight is the two-pointer technique: instead of two pointers moving forward, we fill nums1 from the back. The last n slots are empty, so we can safely write the largest remaining value there without overwriting anything we still need. We compare the current largest of nums1's real part and nums2, and drop the bigger one into the current back slot. A "pointer" here just means an index variable that we walk through the array.

class Solution {
public:
    void merge(vector<int>& nums1, int m, vector<int>& nums2, int n) {
        int firstIndex = m - 1;
        int secondIndex = n - 1;
        int writeIndex = m + n - 1;
        while (secondIndex >= 0) {
            if (firstIndex >= 0 && nums1[firstIndex] > nums2[secondIndex]) {
                nums1[writeIndex] = nums1[firstIndex];
                firstIndex--;
            } else {
                nums1[writeIndex] = nums2[secondIndex];
                secondIndex--;
            }
            writeIndex--;
        }
    }
};

Time: O(m+n), Space: O(1). Each value is written exactly once as the write pointer sweeps from the back. Nothing extra is allocated.

Interview Notes.

  • Filling from the back is the trick that makes in-place merging safe — going front-to-back would clobber unprocessed nums1 values.
  • The loop only needs to continue while secondIndex >= 0; any leftover nums1 values are already in their correct sorted positions.
  • Edge cases: n = 0 (nothing to merge), or m = 0 (just copy nums2 over).
  • A common follow-up is "what if you couldn't modify nums1?" — then you'd allocate a fresh result array and merge forward, O(m+n) space.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Array / String›Problem 2 of 150
2

Remove Element

Easy

Problem. Given an array nums and a value val, remove all occurrences of val in place. Return the count k of remaining elements; the first k slots of nums must hold those elements (order doesn't matter, leftover slots are ignored).

Example. nums = [3,2,2,3], val = 3 → return 2, with nums starting [2,2,...]. Trace: drop both 3s, keep the two 2s up front.

Brute Force. Build a separate list of every element that isn't val, then copy it back. Simple but uses extra memory.

class Solution {
public:
    int removeElement(vector<int>& nums, int val) {
        vector<int> kept;
        for (int number : nums) {
            if (number != val) kept.push_back(number);
        }
        for (int i = 0; i < (int)kept.size(); i++) {
            nums[i] = kept[i];
        }
        return kept.size();
    }
};

Time: O(n), Space: O(n). One pass to filter, one to copy; the temporary kept vector is the extra space.

Optimized. Use a slow/fast two-pointer scheme. The fast pointer scans every element; the slow pointer marks where the next kept element should land. Whenever the fast pointer sees something that isn't val, we copy it to the slow position and advance slow. Slow ends up equal to the count of kept elements.

class Solution {
public:
    int removeElement(vector<int>& nums, int val) {
        int writeIndex = 0;
        for (int readIndex = 0; readIndex < (int)nums.size(); readIndex++) {
            if (nums[readIndex] != val) {
                nums[writeIndex] = nums[readIndex];
                writeIndex++;
            }
        }
        return writeIndex;
    }
};

Time: O(n), Space: O(1). Single pass, only two index variables.

Interview Notes.

  • This slow/fast write-pointer pattern recurs constantly for "remove/compact in place" problems (see the next two).
  • Since order doesn't matter, an even faster variant swaps the matched element with the last element and shrinks the array — fewer writes when val is rare.
  • Edge case: empty array returns 0; array entirely equal to val returns 0.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Array / String›Problem 3 of 150
3

Remove Duplicates from Sorted Array

Easy

Problem. Given a sorted array nums, remove duplicates in place so each unique value appears once. Return the count k of unique values; the first k slots must hold them in sorted order.

Example. nums = [0,0,1,1,1,2,2,3,3,4] → return 5, nums starts [0,1,2,3,4]. Trace: keep the first occurrence of each value, skip repeats.

Brute Force. Because it's sorted, duplicates are adjacent, but a naive approach is to use a set to track seen values and rebuild.

class Solution {
public:
    int removeDuplicates(vector<int>& nums) {
        set<int> seen(nums.begin(), nums.end());
        int writeIndex = 0;
        for (int value : seen) {
            nums[writeIndex] = value;
            writeIndex++;
        }
        return writeIndex;
    }
};

Time: O(n log n), Space: O(n). Building the set costs log-linear time and stores up to n values.

Optimized. Since the array is already sorted, equal values sit next to each other, so we only ever compare with the previous kept value. Use the slow/fast pointer pattern: the slow pointer points at the last unique value written; whenever the fast pointer finds a value different from nums[slow], we advance slow and write it there.

class Solution {
public:
    int removeDuplicates(vector<int>& nums) {
        if (nums.empty()) return 0;
        int writeIndex = 0;
        for (int readIndex = 1; readIndex < (int)nums.size(); readIndex++) {
            if (nums[readIndex] != nums[writeIndex]) {
                writeIndex++;
                nums[writeIndex] = nums[readIndex];
            }
        }
        return writeIndex + 1;
    }
};

Time: O(n), Space: O(1). One pass, two indices. Return writeIndex + 1 because writeIndex is the last index used, not a count.

Interview Notes.

  • The whole optimization hinges on "sorted" — if unsorted, you'd need a hash set (the brute force).
  • Off-by-one trap: the count is writeIndex + 1, since indices start at 0.
  • Edge case: empty array — handle before touching nums[0].
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Array / String›Problem 4 of 150
4

Remove Duplicates from Sorted Array II

Medium

Problem. Same as above, but each unique value may appear at most twice. Return the new length k and put the kept values in the first k slots, in order.

Example. nums = [1,1,1,2,2,3] → return 5, nums starts [1,1,2,2,3]. Trace: keep two 1s, drop the third; keep both 2s; keep 3.

Brute Force. Count occurrences (a sorted run is contiguous) and emit each value up to twice into a temporary array.

class Solution {
public:
    int removeDuplicates(vector<int>& nums) {
        vector<int> kept;
        int runStart = 0;
        while (runStart < (int)nums.size()) {
            int runEnd = runStart;
            while (runEnd < (int)nums.size() && nums[runEnd] == nums[runStart]) {
                runEnd++;
            }
            int allowed = min(2, runEnd - runStart);
            for (int i = 0; i < allowed; i++) kept.push_back(nums[runStart]);
            runStart = runEnd;
        }
        for (int i = 0; i < (int)kept.size(); i++) nums[i] = kept[i];
        return kept.size();
    }
};

Time: O(n), Space: O(n). One pass over runs, but the kept vector is extra space.

Optimized. Generalize the slow/fast pointer with a neat trick: a new element is allowed if the slot two positions back (nums[writeIndex - 2]) is different from it. If they were equal, that value already appears twice in our output, so we skip. This naturally enforces "at most twice" and even extends to "at most k" by comparing writeIndex - k.

class Solution {
public:
    int removeDuplicates(vector<int>& nums) {
        int writeIndex = 0;
        for (int number : nums) {
            if (writeIndex < 2 || number != nums[writeIndex - 2]) {
                nums[writeIndex] = number;
                writeIndex++;
            }
        }
        return writeIndex;
    }
};

Time: O(n), Space: O(1). Single pass, in place.

Interview Notes.

  • The writeIndex < 2 guard lets the first two elements through unconditionally.
  • This generalizes: replace 2 with any k to allow each value k times — a classic follow-up.
  • It's correct only because the input is sorted; comparing against writeIndex - 2 assumes equal values are adjacent.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Array / String›Problem 5 of 150
5

Majority Element

Easy

Problem. Given an array nums of size n, find the majority element — the value that appears more than n/2 times. You may assume it always exists.

Example. nums = [2,2,1,1,1,2,2] → 2. Trace: 2 appears 4 times out of 7, and 4 > 3.

Brute Force. Count each value with a hash map (a structure mapping keys to values, here value → count), then return whichever count exceeds n/2.

class Solution {
public:
    int majorityElement(vector<int>& nums) {
        unordered_map<int, int> counts;
        int total = nums.size();
        for (int number : nums) {
            counts[number]++;
            if (counts[number] > total / 2) return number;
        }
        return -1;
    }
};

Time: O(n), Space: O(n). One pass, but the map can store up to n distinct keys.

Optimized. Use the Boyer-Moore voting algorithm. Intuition: pair up each majority element with a different element and they cancel out; since the majority appears more than half the time, it survives. We keep a candidate and a count. When count hits 0 we adopt the current value as the new candidate; we increment count on a match and decrement on a mismatch.

class Solution {
public:
    int majorityElement(vector<int>& nums) {
        int candidate = nums[0];
        int count = 0;
        for (int number : nums) {
            if (count == 0) candidate = number;
            if (number == candidate) count++;
            else count--;
        }
        return candidate;
    }
};

Time: O(n), Space: O(1). Single pass, two variables.

Interview Notes.

  • Boyer-Moore only guarantees correctness when a strict majority (> n/2) is promised; otherwise add a verification pass to confirm the candidate.
  • Common follow-up: "find all elements appearing more than n/3 times" — generalizes to two candidates and two counters.
  • Alternative O(1)-space trick: sort and return nums[n/2], but that's O(n log n) time.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Array / String›Problem 6 of 150
6

Rotate Array

Medium

Problem. Rotate array nums to the right by k steps, in place. Elements that fall off the end wrap to the front. k can be larger than the array length.

Example. nums = [1,2,3,4,5,6,7], k = 3 → [5,6,7,1,2,3,4]. Trace: the last 3 elements 5,6,7 move to the front; the rest shift right.

Brute Force. Rotate one step at a time, k times: move the last element to the front and shift everything else right.

class Solution {
public:
    void rotate(vector<int>& nums, int k) {
        int length = nums.size();
        k %= length;
        for (int step = 0; step < k; step++) {
            int last = nums[length - 1];
            for (int i = length - 1; i > 0; i--) {
                nums[i] = nums[i - 1];
            }
            nums[0] = last;
        }
    }
};

Time: O(n·k), Space: O(1). Each of the k rotations shifts all n elements.

Optimized. Use the reversal trick. Reversing the whole array puts the last k elements at the front but in reverse order; then reversing the first k and the remaining n−k separately fixes the order within each block. Always take k % length first because rotating by the full length is a no-op.

class Solution {
public:
    void rotate(vector<int>& nums, int k) {
        int length = nums.size();
        k %= length;
        reverse(nums.begin(), nums.end());
        reverse(nums.begin(), nums.begin() + k);
        reverse(nums.begin() + k, nums.end());
    }
};

Time: O(n), Space: O(1). Three reversals, each linear, no extra array.

Interview Notes.

  • Forgetting k %= length is the most common bug, causing out-of-bounds when k > length.
  • An alternative O(n) solution copies into a new array using (i + k) % length as the target index — simpler to reason about but O(n) space.
  • Walk through the three-reverse steps on paper in the interview; it's not obvious until you see it.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Array / String›Problem 7 of 150
7

Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock

Easy

Problem. Given daily prices, you may buy on one day and sell on a later day, one transaction only. Return the maximum profit, or 0 if no profit is possible.

Example. prices = [7,1,5,3,6,4] → 5. Trace: buy at 1 (day 2), sell at 6 (day 5), profit 5.

Brute Force. Try every pair of buy day and later sell day, track the best difference.

class Solution {
public:
    int maxProfit(vector<int>& prices) {
        int maxProfit = 0;
        for (int buyDay = 0; buyDay < (int)prices.size(); buyDay++) {
            for (int sellDay = buyDay + 1; sellDay < (int)prices.size(); sellDay++) {
                maxProfit = max(maxProfit, prices[sellDay] - prices[buyDay]);
            }
        }
        return maxProfit;
    }
};

Time: O(n²), Space: O(1). Every pair of days is examined.

Optimized. Single pass tracking the minimum price seen so far. For each day, the best profit if we sell today is today's price − cheapest day before now. Keep a running minimum and a running best profit.

class Solution {
public:
    int maxProfit(vector<int>& prices) {
        int lowestPrice = INT_MAX;
        int maxProfit = 0;
        for (int price : prices) {
            lowestPrice = min(lowestPrice, price);
            maxProfit = max(maxProfit, price - lowestPrice);
        }
        return maxProfit;
    }
};

Time: O(n), Space: O(1). One pass, two variables.

Interview Notes.

  • The insight: you never need to know which earlier day was cheapest, just its price.
  • Edge case: strictly decreasing prices yield 0 (you simply don't trade).
  • Follow-ups expand into Problem 8 (unlimited transactions) and harder "at most k transactions" / "with cooldown" variants.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Array / String›Problem 8 of 150
8

Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock II

Medium

Problem. Same setup, but you may complete as many transactions as you like (buy then sell, repeatedly), holding at most one share at a time. Maximize total profit.

Example. prices = [7,1,5,3,6,4] → 7. Trace: buy 1 sell 5 (+4), buy 3 sell 6 (+3), total 7.

Brute Force. Recursively, at each day choose to buy, sell, or skip, and take the best. This explores an exponential decision tree.

class Solution {
public:
    int maxProfit(vector<int>& prices) {
        return explore(prices, 0, false);
    }
private:
    int explore(vector<int>& prices, int day, bool holding) {
        if (day == (int)prices.size()) return 0;
        int skip = explore(prices, day + 1, holding);
        int act;
        if (holding) act = prices[day] + explore(prices, day + 1, false);
        else act = -prices[day] + explore(prices, day + 1, true);
        return max(skip, act);
    }
};

Time: O(2^n), Space: O(n). Two branches per day, recursion depth n.

Optimized. A clean greedy observation: total profit equals the sum of every upward step. Whenever tomorrow's price is higher than today's, pocket that difference — it's the same as buying at every local valley and selling at every local peak.

class Solution {
public:
    int maxProfit(vector<int>& prices) {
        int totalProfit = 0;
        for (int day = 1; day < (int)prices.size(); day++) {
            if (prices[day] > prices[day - 1]) {
                totalProfit += prices[day] - prices[day - 1];
            }
        }
        return totalProfit;
    }
};

Time: O(n), Space: O(1). Single pass summing positive deltas.

Interview Notes.

  • Why greedy works: a profitable multi-day rise a < c can be decomposed into consecutive steps, so summing each up-step never undercounts.
  • A frequent probe: "what if there's a transaction fee?" — then you can't naively grab every step; you switch to a DP tracking hold/cash states.
  • Edge case: arrays of length 0 or 1 give 0.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Array / String›Problem 9 of 150
9

Jump Game

Medium

Problem. Given nums where nums[i] is the maximum jump length from index i, starting at index 0, determine whether you can reach the last index.

Example. nums = [2,3,1,1,4] → true. Trace: 0→1 (jump 1) →4 (jump 3), reaches the end. nums = [3,2,1,0,4] → false (stuck at index 3).

Brute Force. Recursively try every jump length from each reachable index until you either reach the end or exhaust options.

class Solution {
public:
    bool canJump(vector<int>& nums) {
        return reach(nums, 0);
    }
private:
    bool reach(vector<int>& nums, int position) {
        if (position >= (int)nums.size() - 1) return true;
        for (int jump = 1; jump <= nums[position]; jump++) {
            if (reach(nums, position + jump)) return true;
        }
        return false;
    }
};

Time: O(2^n) worst case, Space: O(n) recursion depth. It re-explores overlapping subpaths.

Optimized. Greedy: sweep left to right tracking farthestReach, the furthest index reachable so far. At each index, if it lies beyond farthestReach we're stuck and return false; otherwise extend the reach. If reach ever covers the last index, success.

class Solution {
public:
    bool canJump(vector<int>& nums) {
        int farthestReach = 0;
        for (int i = 0; i < (int)nums.size(); i++) {
            if (i > farthestReach) return false;
            farthestReach = max(farthestReach, i + nums[i]);
        }
        return true;
    }
};

Time: O(n), Space: O(1). One pass, one variable.

Interview Notes.

  • The greedy works because reachability is monotonic: if you can reach index i, you can reach every index up to farthestReach.
  • Edge case: a 0 at index 0 with length-1 array is still reachable (you're already there).
  • Natural follow-up is Problem 10: not "can you reach" but "minimum jumps to reach."
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Array / String›Problem 10 of 150
10

Jump Game II

Medium

Problem. Same jump rules, but you're guaranteed the last index is reachable. Return the minimum number of jumps to get there.

Example. nums = [2,3,1,1,4] → 2. Trace: jump from 0 to index 1 (value 3), then from index 1 straight to index 4. Two jumps.

Brute Force. Recursively compute the minimum jumps from each position, taking the best over all reachable next positions.

class Solution {
public:
    int jump(vector<int>& nums) {
        return minJumps(nums, 0);
    }
private:
    int minJumps(vector<int>& nums, int position) {
        if (position >= (int)nums.size() - 1) return 0;
        int best = INT_MAX;
        for (int step = 1; step <= nums[position]; step++) {
            int sub = minJumps(nums, position + step);
            if (sub != INT_MAX) best = min(best, sub + 1);
        }
        return best;
    }
};

Time: O(2^n), Space: O(n). Exponential branching without memoization.

Optimized. A greedy BFS-by-levels view: think of jumps as expanding rings. Track the currentEnd (farthest index reachable with the jumps taken so far) and farthest (the best you could reach by jumping from anything in the current ring). When the scan index passes currentEnd, you must take another jump and the new boundary becomes farthest.

class Solution {
public:
    int jump(vector<int>& nums) {
        int jumps = 0;
        int currentEnd = 0;
        int farthest = 0;
        for (int i = 0; i < (int)nums.size() - 1; i++) {
            farthest = max(farthest, i + nums[i]);
            if (i == currentEnd) {
                jumps++;
                currentEnd = farthest;
            }
        }
        return jumps;
    }
};

Time: O(n), Space: O(1). One pass; we loop to n−1 so we don't count a phantom jump at the destination.

Interview Notes.

  • The loop stops at n−1: once you're at the last index you don't jump again.
  • This is essentially breadth-first search where each "level" is one more jump, but flattened into a single linear scan.
  • A common probe: explain why farthest is allowed to overshoot currentEnd before you "commit" the jump.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Array / String›Problem 11 of 150
11

H-Index

Medium

Problem. Given citations[i] = number of citations of the i-th paper, return the researcher's h-index: the largest h such that at least h papers each have at least h citations.

Example. citations = [3,0,6,1,5] → 3. Trace: there are 3 papers with ≥ 3 citations (3, 6, 5), but not 4 papers with ≥ 4, so h = 3.

Brute Force. Try every candidate h from 0 up to n, and for each count how many papers have at least h citations; keep the largest valid h.

class Solution {
public:
    int hIndex(vector<int>& citations) {
        int n = citations.size();
        int bestH = 0;
        for (int h = 0; h <= n; h++) {
            int papersWithAtLeastH = 0;
            for (int citationCount : citations) {
                if (citationCount >= h) papersWithAtLeastH++;
            }
            if (papersWithAtLeastH >= h) bestH = h;
        }
        return bestH;
    }
};

Time: O(n²), Space: O(1). For each of n+1 candidate values we scan all n papers.

Optimized. Counting / bucket approach. A paper with more than n citations can't push the h-index above n, so cap each count at n and tally how many papers fall in each bucket. Then sweep buckets from high to low, accumulating paper counts; the first h where the running total reaches h is the answer.

class Solution {
public:
    int hIndex(vector<int>& citations) {
        int n = citations.size();
        vector<int> bucket(n + 1, 0);
        for (int citationCount : citations) {
            bucket[min(citationCount, n)]++;
        }
        int runningPapers = 0;
        for (int h = n; h >= 0; h--) {
            runningPapers += bucket[h];
            if (runningPapers >= h) return h;
        }
        return 0;
    }
};

Time: O(n), Space: O(n). One pass to fill buckets, one to sweep; the bucket array is the extra space.

Interview Notes.

  • Capping at n is the key trick — citations beyond n are wasted because there are only n papers.
  • A simpler O(n log n) alternative: sort descending and find the last index i where citations[i] >= i + 1.
  • "H-Index II" gives a sorted input and expects O(log n) via binary search.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Array / String›Problem 12 of 150
12

Insert Delete GetRandom O(1)

Medium

Problem. Design a set-like structure RandomizedSet supporting insert(val), remove(val), and getRandom() — each in average O(1) time. getRandom must return any current element with equal probability.

Example. insert(1) → true; insert(2) → true; remove(1) → true; getRandom() → always 2 (only element left).

Brute Force. Back it with just a vector. Insert is O(1) amortized and getRandom is O(1), but remove must search the vector and erase, which is O(n).

class RandomizedSet {
    vector<int> values;
public:
    bool insert(int val) {
        if (find(values.begin(), values.end(), val) != values.end()) return false;
        values.push_back(val);
        return true;
    }
    bool remove(int val) {
        auto position = find(values.begin(), values.end(), val);
        if (position == values.end()) return false;
        values.erase(position);
        return true;
    }
    int getRandom() {
        return values[rand() % values.size()];
    }
};

Time: insert O(n), remove O(n), getRandom O(1). Space: O(n). The linear scans and shifting erase break the O(1) requirement.

Optimized. Combine a vector (for O(1) random access) with a hash map from value → its index in the vector. The trick for O(1) removal: swap the element to delete with the last element, pop the back, and update the moved element's index in the map. No shifting needed.

class RandomizedSet {
    vector<int> values;
    unordered_map<int, int> indexOf;
public:
    bool insert(int val) {
        if (indexOf.count(val)) return false;
        indexOf[val] = values.size();
        values.push_back(val);
        return true;
    }
    bool remove(int val) {
        if (!indexOf.count(val)) return false;
        int removeIndex = indexOf[val];
        int lastValue = values.back();
        values[removeIndex] = lastValue;
        indexOf[lastValue] = removeIndex;
        values.pop_back();
        indexOf.erase(val);
        return true;
    }
    int getRandom() {
        return values[rand() % values.size()];
    }
};

Time: insert/remove/getRandom all average O(1), Space: O(n). The map gives O(1) existence checks and index lookups; swap-with-last avoids shifting.

Interview Notes.

  • The swap-with-last idiom is the heart of the design — interviewers want to see you realize a vector can't delete from the middle cheaply.
  • Be careful when the element to remove is the last element; the swap is harmless but make sure you erase from the map correctly.
  • Follow-up "allow duplicates" (RandomizedCollection) replaces the map value with a set of indices.
  • getRandom needs contiguous storage so rand() % size gives uniform probability — that's why a plain hash set wouldn't suffice.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Array / String›Problem 13 of 150
13

Product of Array Except Self

Medium

Problem. Given nums, return an array answer where answer[i] is the product of every element except nums[i]. You must not use division, and aim for O(n) time.

Example. nums = [1,2,3,4] → [24,12,8,6]. Trace: answer[0] = 2·3·4 = 24, answer[1] = 1·3·4 = 12, and so on.

Brute Force. For each index, multiply all the other elements with an inner loop.

class Solution {
public:
    vector<int> productExceptSelf(vector<int>& nums) {
        int n = nums.size();
        vector<int> answer(n, 1);
        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
            for (int j = 0; j < n; j++) {
                if (i != j) answer[i] *= nums[j];
            }
        }
        return answer;
    }
};

Time: O(n²), Space: O(1) extra (besides the output). Every index scans the whole array.

Optimized. Use prefix and suffix products. A prefix product is the running product of everything to the left of an index; the suffix product is everything to the right. Their product is exactly "all except self." First pass fills answer[i] with the prefix; second pass multiplies in the suffix using a single running variable, so no extra arrays beyond the output.

class Solution {
public:
    vector<int> productExceptSelf(vector<int>& nums) {
        int n = nums.size();
        vector<int> answer(n, 1);
        int prefix = 1;
        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
            answer[i] = prefix;
            prefix *= nums[i];
        }
        int suffix = 1;
        for (int i = n - 1; i >= 0; i--) {
            answer[i] *= suffix;
            suffix *= nums[i];
        }
        return answer;
    }
};

Time: O(n), Space: O(1) extra (the output array doesn't count). Two passes, two scalar running products.

Interview Notes.

  • "No division" is the constraint that rules out the trivial total-product/nums[i] answer — and division also breaks on zeros.
  • Storing the prefix directly in answer then folding suffixes in keeps extra space at O(1).
  • A classic probe: "how would you handle one zero? two zeros?" with division — good to discuss why prefix/suffix sidesteps the whole issue.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Array / String›Problem 14 of 150
14

Gas Station

Medium

Problem. There are n gas stations on a circle. gas[i] is fuel available at station i, cost[i] is fuel needed to drive from i to i+1. Starting with an empty tank, return the starting station index from which you can complete the full circle, or -1 if impossible. The answer is unique if it exists.

Example. gas = [1,2,3,4,5], cost = [3,4,5,1,2] → 3. Trace: starting at station 3 you accumulate enough buffer (gain +3, +3) before the deficits to never go negative.

Brute Force. Try each station as a start; simulate the full loop and check the tank never goes negative.

class Solution {
public:
    int canCompleteCircuit(vector<int>& gas, vector<int>& cost) {
        int n = gas.size();
        for (int start = 0; start < n; start++) {
            int tank = 0;
            bool ok = true;
            for (int step = 0; step < n; step++) {
                int station = (start + step) % n;
                tank += gas[station] - cost[station];
                if (tank < 0) { ok = false; break; }
            }
            if (ok) return start;
        }
        return -1;
    }
};

Time: O(n²), Space: O(1). Each candidate start triggers a full loop.

Optimized. Two greedy facts. First, a solution exists iff total gas ≥ total cost. Second, if the running tank drops below 0 at some station, no start between the previous candidate start and that station can work — so the next viable start is the station right after. One pass finds it.

class Solution {
public:
    int canCompleteCircuit(vector<int>& gas, vector<int>& cost) {
        int totalBalance = 0;
        int tank = 0;
        int startIndex = 0;
        for (int i = 0; i < (int)gas.size(); i++) {
            int gain = gas[i] - cost[i];
            totalBalance += gain;
            tank += gain;
            if (tank < 0) {
                startIndex = i + 1;
                tank = 0;
            }
        }
        return totalBalance >= 0 ? startIndex : -1;
    }
};

Time: O(n), Space: O(1). Single pass.

Interview Notes.

  • The crucial lemma: if you can't reach station j starting from i, you can't reach j from any station between i and j either (they'd have even less or equal fuel). Be ready to justify it.
  • totalBalance >= 0 is both necessary and sufficient given uniqueness — that's why a single negative-reset pass lands on the right answer.
  • Edge case: all stations break even (gas == cost) → start 0 works.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Array / String›Problem 15 of 150
15

Candy

Hard

Problem. n children stand in a line, each with a ratings value. Distribute candies so each child gets at least one, and any child with a higher rating than an immediate neighbor gets more candies than that neighbor. Return the minimum total candies.

Example. ratings = [1,0,2] → 5. Trace: child 0 gets 2, child 1 gets 1, child 2 gets 2 → 2+1+2 = 5.

Brute Force. Give everyone 1 candy, then repeatedly sweep the line; whenever a child violates the rule against a neighbor, bump their candy up by 1. Repeat until a full sweep makes no change.

class Solution {
public:
    int candy(vector<int>& ratings) {
        int n = ratings.size();
        vector<int> candies(n, 1);
        bool changed = true;
        while (changed) {
            changed = false;
            for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
                if (i > 0 && ratings[i] > ratings[i - 1] && candies[i] <= candies[i - 1]) {
                    candies[i] = candies[i - 1] + 1;
                    changed = true;
                }
                if (i < n - 1 && ratings[i] > ratings[i + 1] && candies[i] <= candies[i + 1]) {
                    candies[i] = candies[i + 1] + 1;
                    changed = true;
                }
            }
        }
        int total = 0;
        for (int amount : candies) total += amount;
        return total;
    }
};

Time: O(n²) worst case, Space: O(n). A long increasing run can require many sweeps to settle.

Optimized. Two-pass greedy. Left-to-right: if a child rates higher than the left neighbor, give them one more than that neighbor. Right-to-left: if a child rates higher than the right neighbor, ensure they have at least one more than the right neighbor (take the max so the left-pass result isn't lost). Summing the per-child maximums satisfies both directions simultaneously.

class Solution {
public:
    int candy(vector<int>& ratings) {
        int n = ratings.size();
        vector<int> candies(n, 1);
        for (int i = 1; i < n; i++) {
            if (ratings[i] > ratings[i - 1]) {
                candies[i] = candies[i - 1] + 1;
            }
        }
        for (int i = n - 2; i >= 0; i--) {
            if (ratings[i] > ratings[i + 1]) {
                candies[i] = max(candies[i], candies[i + 1] + 1);
            }
        }
        int total = 0;
        for (int amount : candies) total += amount;
        return total;
    }
};

Time: O(n), Space: O(n). Two linear passes plus a sum.

Interview Notes.

  • The max in the second pass is essential — without it you'd overwrite a larger candy count earned in the left pass.
  • There's a clever O(1)-space solution counting up-slopes, down-slopes, and the peak; interviewers love it as a stretch follow-up.
  • Edge cases: a single child (answer 1), all equal ratings (answer n).
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Array / String›Problem 16 of 150
16

Trapping Rain Water

Hard

Problem. Given non-negative height values representing an elevation map (each bar width 1), compute how much rainwater is trapped between the bars after raining.

Example. height = [0,1,0,2,1,0,1,3,2,1,2,1] → 6. Trace: water pools in the dips; summing the trapped depth at every index gives 6 units.

Brute Force. Water above any index is bounded by the shorter of the tallest bar to its left and the tallest bar to its right, minus its own height. Compute those two maxima per index with inner scans.

class Solution {
public:
    int trap(vector<int>& height) {
        int n = height.size();
        int water = 0;
        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
            int leftMax = 0;
            for (int j = 0; j <= i; j++) leftMax = max(leftMax, height[j]);
            int rightMax = 0;
            for (int j = i; j < n; j++) rightMax = max(rightMax, height[j]);
            water += min(leftMax, rightMax) - height[i];
        }
        return water;
    }
};

Time: O(n²), Space: O(1). Each index recomputes both maxima from scratch.

Optimized. Two pointers from both ends. Keep leftMax and rightMax. Whichever side's wall is shorter is the limiting boundary, so we can safely settle water for that side and move that pointer inward. The shorter side guarantees water level is decided by its own running max.

class Solution {
public:
    int trap(vector<int>& height) {
        int left = 0;
        int right = height.size() - 1;
        int leftMax = 0;
        int rightMax = 0;
        int water = 0;
        while (left < right) {
            if (height[left] < height[right]) {
                if (height[left] >= leftMax) leftMax = height[left];
                else water += leftMax - height[left];
                left++;
            } else {
                if (height[right] >= rightMax) rightMax = height[right];
                else water += rightMax - height[right];
                right--;
            }
        }
        return water;
    }
};

Time: O(n), Space: O(1). Single pass with two converging pointers.

Interview Notes.

  • The correctness argument: when height[left] < height[right], the left side's trapping is bounded entirely by leftMax because there's some taller bar on the right — so we don't even need to know rightMax exactly.
  • A common intermediate solution precomputes leftMax[] and rightMax[] arrays — O(n) time but O(n) space; the two-pointer version trims it to O(1).
  • A stack-based approach also works, filling water layer by layer; mention it as an alternative.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Array / String›Problem 17 of 150
17

Roman to Integer

Easy

Problem. Convert a Roman numeral string to an integer. Symbols: I=1, V=5, X=10, L=50, C=100, D=500, M=1000. Usually values add left to right, but when a smaller symbol precedes a larger one (like IV=4, IX=9, CM=900) it is subtracted.

Example. "MCMXCIV" → 1994. Trace: M(1000) + CM(900) + XC(90) + IV(4) = 1994.

Brute Force. Scan left to right; for each symbol, peek at the next one. If the current value is smaller than the next, this is a subtractive pair so subtract it; otherwise add it.

class Solution {
public:
    int romanToInt(string s) {
        unordered_map<char, int> value = {
            {'I',1},{'V',5},{'X',10},{'L',50},{'C',100},{'D',500},{'M',1000}
        };
        int total = 0;
        for (int i = 0; i < (int)s.size(); i++) {
            if (i + 1 < (int)s.size() && value[s[i]] < value[s[i + 1]]) {
                total -= value[s[i]];
            } else {
                total += value[s[i]];
            }
        }
        return total;
    }
};

Time: O(n), Space: O(1). One pass over the string; the map is fixed-size. (This is already efficient — Roman parsing has no quadratic naive trap worth showing separately.)

Optimized. The same single-pass rule is the optimal approach: if a symbol's value is less than the next symbol's value, subtract it; otherwise add it. The code above already does this in O(n) time and O(1) space, so no further improvement is needed.

class Solution {
public:
    int romanToInt(string s) {
        unordered_map<char, int> value = {
            {'I',1},{'V',5},{'X',10},{'L',50},{'C',100},{'D',500},{'M',1000}
        };
        int total = 0;
        int previous = 0;
        for (int i = (int)s.size() - 1; i >= 0; i--) {
            int current = value[s[i]];
            if (current < previous) total -= current;
            else total += current;
            previous = current;
        }
        return total;
    }
};

Time: O(n), Space: O(1). Scanning right to left lets us compare against the symbol we just processed without a look-ahead bounds check.

Interview Notes.

  • The right-to-left scan is a tidy variant: you compare to the previous (the one to the right) and avoid an index-bounds check on the look-ahead.
  • Only six subtractive combinations are valid (IV, IX, XL, XC, CD, CM); inputs are guaranteed well-formed in this problem.
  • Edge: single-character numerals work trivially since there's nothing larger after them.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Array / String›Problem 18 of 150
18

Integer to Roman

Medium

Problem. Convert an integer (1 to 3999) to its Roman numeral string, using the standard subtractive forms (e.g. 4 = IV, 900 = CM).

Example. 1994 → "MCMXCIV". Trace: 1000→M, 900→CM, 90→XC, 4→IV.

Brute Force. Handle each decimal place (thousands, hundreds, tens, ones) with separate logic or lookup tables per digit. It works but the code is verbose and error-prone.

class Solution {
public:
    string intToRoman(int num) {
        string thousands[] = {"","M","MM","MMM"};
        string hundreds[] = {"","C","CC","CCC","CD","D","DC","DCC","DCCC","CM"};
        string tens[] = {"","X","XX","XXX","XL","L","LX","LXX","LXXX","XC"};
        string ones[] = {"","I","II","III","IV","V","VI","VII","VIII","IX"};
        return thousands[num / 1000] + hundreds[(num / 100) % 10]
             + tens[(num / 10) % 10] + ones[num % 10];
    }
};

Time: O(1), Space: O(1). Fixed number of digit positions; constant work. (Concise but relies on four hand-built tables.)

Optimized. Greedy with a value→symbol table sorted high to low, including the subtractive pairs (900/CM, 400/CD, etc.). Repeatedly subtract the largest value that fits and append its symbol. This naturally produces the shortest valid numeral.

class Solution {
public:
    string intToRoman(int num) {
        vector<pair<int, string>> table = {
            {1000,"M"},{900,"CM"},{500,"D"},{400,"CD"},
            {100,"C"},{90,"XC"},{50,"L"},{40,"XL"},
            {10,"X"},{9,"IX"},{5,"V"},{4,"IV"},{1,"I"}
        };
        string result;
        for (auto& entry : table) {
            int value = entry.first;
            const string& symbol = entry.second;
            while (num >= value) {
                result += symbol;
                num -= value;
            }
        }
        return result;
    }
};

Time: O(1), Space: O(1). The table is fixed-size and num is bounded by 3999, so the loop count is bounded by a constant.

Interview Notes.

  • Putting the subtractive pairs into the table is the elegant move — it removes all the special-casing.
  • The greedy "largest first" never gets stuck because Roman values are designed so each symbol covers its range cleanly.
  • Interviewers may ask you to reverse it (Problem 17) or to handle values above 3999 (needs overlines — usually out of scope).
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Array / String›Problem 19 of 150
19

Length of Last Word

Easy

Problem. Given a string s of words separated by spaces, return the length of the last word. Trailing spaces are possible and a "word" is a maximal run of non-space characters.

Example. " fly me to the moon " → 4. Trace: ignoring trailing spaces, the last word is "moon", length 4.

Brute Force. Split the string into words (e.g. with a stream), then return the length of the last one.

class Solution {
public:
    int lengthOfLastWord(string s) {
        stringstream stream(s);
        string word;
        string lastWord;
        while (stream >> word) {
            lastWord = word;
        }
        return lastWord.size();
    }
};

Time: O(n), Space: O(n). One pass, but it stores each word; the final word can be up to length n.

Optimized. Scan from the end. Skip any trailing spaces first, then count characters until you hit the next space or the start of the string. No extra storage.

class Solution {
public:
    int lengthOfLastWord(string s) {
        int i = s.size() - 1;
        while (i >= 0 && s[i] == ' ') i--;
        int length = 0;
        while (i >= 0 && s[i] != ' ') {
            length++;
            i--;
        }
        return length;
    }
};

Time: O(n), Space: O(1). At most one backward pass, two scalar variables.

Interview Notes.

  • Scanning from the back is the clean trick — you never need to find all word boundaries, just the last one.
  • Handle trailing spaces explicitly (skip them before counting), the classic bug here.
  • Edge cases: a string of only spaces gives 0; a single word with no spaces returns its full length.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Array / String›Problem 20 of 150
20

Longest Common Prefix

Easy

Problem. Given an array of strings, return the longest string prefix common to all of them. If there is none, return the empty string.

Example. ["flower","flow","flight"] → "fl". Trace: all three start with "fl", but the third breaks at the next character.

Brute Force. Take the first string as a candidate prefix; for each other string, shorten the candidate until it's a prefix of that string.

class Solution {
public:
    string longestCommonPrefix(vector<string>& strs) {
        if (strs.empty()) return "";
        string prefix = strs[0];
        for (int i = 1; i < (int)strs.size(); i++) {
            while (strs[i].find(prefix) != 0) {
                prefix = prefix.substr(0, prefix.size() - 1);
                if (prefix.empty()) return "";
            }
        }
        return prefix;
    }
};

Time: O(total characters), Space: O(1) extra. Each find checks a prefix against a string; total work is bounded by the sum of string lengths.

Optimized. Vertical scanning: compare character position 0 across all strings, then position 1, and so on. The first position where any string ends or characters disagree marks the end of the common prefix. This stops as early as possible.

class Solution {
public:
    string longestCommonPrefix(vector<string>& strs) {
        if (strs.empty()) return "";
        for (int position = 0; position < (int)strs[0].size(); position++) {
            char current = strs[0][position];
            for (int i = 1; i < (int)strs.size(); i++) {
                if (position >= (int)strs[i].size() || strs[i][position] != current) {
                    return strs[0].substr(0, position);
                }
            }
        }
        return strs[0];
    }
};

Time: O(total characters) worst case but stops at the first mismatch, Space: O(1) extra.

Interview Notes.

  • Vertical scanning short-circuits the moment a column disagrees — best when the common prefix is short.
  • Edge cases: empty input array, an empty string in the array (forces an immediate empty result).
  • A divide-and-conquer or binary-search-on-prefix-length variant exists; mention it if asked for alternatives.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Array / String›Problem 21 of 150
21

Reverse Words in a String

Medium

Problem. Given a string s, reverse the order of the words. Words are runs of non-space characters; collapse multiple spaces and strip leading/trailing spaces so the result has single spaces between words.

Example. " the sky is blue " → "blue is sky the". Trace: words are the, sky, is, blue; reverse the order and join with single spaces.

Brute Force. Tokenize into words with a stream (which conveniently skips extra spaces), then build the result back to front.

class Solution {
public:
    string reverseWords(string s) {
        stringstream stream(s);
        string word;
        vector<string> words;
        while (stream >> word) words.push_back(word);
        string result;
        for (int i = (int)words.size() - 1; i >= 0; i--) {
            result += words[i];
            if (i > 0) result += " ";
        }
        return result;
    }
};

Time: O(n), Space: O(n). One pass to collect words, one to join; the words vector holds them all.

Optimized. An in-place technique (O(1) extra beyond the string): reverse the entire string, then reverse each individual word back, and finally compact out the extra spaces with a write pointer. After the first reversal the words are in the right order but each is spelled backward; reversing each word fixes that.

class Solution {
public:
    string reverseWords(string s) {
        reverse(s.begin(), s.end());
        int writeIndex = 0;
        int i = 0;
        int n = s.size();
        while (i < n) {
            if (s[i] != ' ') {
                if (writeIndex != 0) s[writeIndex++] = ' ';
                int wordStart = writeIndex;
                while (i < n && s[i] != ' ') s[writeIndex++] = s[i++];
                reverse(s.begin() + wordStart, s.begin() + writeIndex);
            } else {
                i++;
            }
        }
        s.resize(writeIndex);
        return s;
    }
};

Time: O(n), Space: O(1) extra (operates on the string in place). The reversals and compaction are all linear.

Interview Notes.

  • The "reverse whole, reverse each word" pattern is a staple for word-level rearrangement.
  • Space handling is the tricky part: you only emit a separator before a word when something has already been written.
  • In C++ the stream solution is short and interview-acceptable; the in-place version shines when asked for O(1) extra space or in languages with mutable strings (the C char[] version).
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Array / String›Problem 22 of 150
22

Zigzag Conversion

Medium

Problem. Write a string in a zigzag pattern across a given number of numRows, then read it row by row. For example with 3 rows you go down the rows, then diagonally up, repeating. Return the row-by-row concatenation.

Example. s = "PAYPALISHIRING", numRows = 3 → "PAHNAPLSIIGYIR". Trace: row 0 = P,A,H,N; row 1 = A,P,L,S,I,I,G; row 2 = Y,I,R; concatenated gives the answer.

Brute Force. Build an actual 2D grid (matrix of characters), place each character at its computed row/column following the zigzag walk, then read non-empty cells row by row.

class Solution {
public:
    string convert(string s, int numRows) {
        if (numRows == 1) return s;
        int columns = s.size();
        vector<vector<char>> grid(numRows, vector<char>(columns, 0));
        int row = 0, col = 0;
        bool goingDown = true;
        for (char ch : s) {
            grid[row][col] = ch;
            if (goingDown) {
                if (row == numRows - 1) { goingDown = false; col++; row--; }
                else row++;
            } else {
                if (row == 0) { goingDown = true; row++; }
                else { col++; row--; }
            }
        }
        string result;
        for (int r = 0; r < numRows; r++)
            for (int c = 0; c < columns; c++)
                if (grid[r][c] != 0) result += grid[r][c];
        return result;
    }
};

Time: O(numRows · n), Space: O(numRows · n). The grid is mostly empty yet allocated fully.

Optimized. Skip the grid: keep one string per row. Walk through s once, appending each character to its current row's string, and flip direction whenever you hit the top or bottom row. Concatenate the row strings at the end.

class Solution {
public:
    string convert(string s, int numRows) {
        if (numRows == 1) return s;
        vector<string> rows(min(numRows, (int)s.size()));
        int currentRow = 0;
        bool goingDown = false;
        for (char ch : s) {
            rows[currentRow] += ch;
            if (currentRow == 0 || currentRow == numRows - 1) {
                goingDown = !goingDown;
            }
            currentRow += goingDown ? 1 : -1;
        }
        string result;
        for (const string& rowText : rows) result += rowText;
        return result;
    }
};

Time: O(n), Space: O(n). Each character is placed once; the row strings together hold exactly n characters.

Interview Notes.

  • The direction flip happens at both boundaries (top and bottom rows) — that's what makes it zigzag rather than just wrapping.
  • Always special-case numRows == 1: there's no zigzag and the formula would divide oddly.
  • A purely mathematical O(1)-space approach computes each row's indices with a fixed period of 2·numRows − 2; mention it if pushed on space.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Array / String›Problem 23 of 150
23

Find the Index of the First Occurrence in a String

Easy

Problem. Given strings haystack and needle, return the index of the first occurrence of needle in haystack, or -1 if it isn't present. (This is the classic substring-search / strstr problem.)

Example. haystack = "sadbutsad", needle = "sad" → 0. Trace: "sad" matches starting at index 0 (it also occurs at index 6, but we want the first).

Brute Force. Try every starting position in haystack and check character by character whether needle matches there.

class Solution {
public:
    int strStr(string haystack, string needle) {
        int n = haystack.size();
        int m = needle.size();
        for (int start = 0; start + m <= n; start++) {
            int offset = 0;
            while (offset < m && haystack[start + offset] == needle[offset]) {
                offset++;
            }
            if (offset == m) return start;
        }
        return -1;
    }
};

Time: O(n·m), Space: O(1). Each of up to n starts can re-check up to m characters.

Optimized. The KMP (Knuth–Morris–Pratt) algorithm achieves O(n+m). It precomputes a "longest proper prefix that is also a suffix" (LPS) array for needle, which tells us how far to jump on a mismatch without re-checking characters we already matched. The text pointer never moves backward.

class Solution {
public:
    int strStr(string haystack, string needle) {
        int m = needle.size();
        vector<int> lps(m, 0);
        int prefixLength = 0;
        for (int i = 1; i < m; ) {
            if (needle[i] == needle[prefixLength]) {
                prefixLength++;
                lps[i] = prefixLength;
                i++;
            } else if (prefixLength > 0) {
                prefixLength = lps[prefixLength - 1];
            } else {
                lps[i] = 0;
                i++;
            }
        }
        int textIndex = 0;
        int patternIndex = 0;
        while (textIndex < (int)haystack.size()) {
            if (haystack[textIndex] == needle[patternIndex]) {
                textIndex++;
                patternIndex++;
                if (patternIndex == m) return textIndex - m;
            } else if (patternIndex > 0) {
                patternIndex = lps[patternIndex - 1];
            } else {
                textIndex++;
            }
        }
        return -1;
    }
};

Time: O(n+m), Space: O(m). The LPS table is built in O(m); the scan is linear because the text index only moves forward.

Interview Notes.

  • For most interviews the O(n·m) brute force is accepted; KMP is the impressive follow-up when asked to beat it.
  • The LPS array is the conceptual heart: it encodes how much of a partial match we can reuse after a mismatch.
  • Edge cases: empty needle conventionally returns 0; needle longer than haystack returns -1 (the loop bound handles it).
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Array / String›Problem 24 of 150
24

Text Justification

Hard

Problem. Given an array of words and a maxWidth, format the text so each line is exactly maxWidth characters, fully justified. Pack as many words per line as fit (with at least one space between words). Distribute extra spaces as evenly as possible, with leftover spaces going to the leftmost gaps. The last line (and any line with a single word) is left-justified — words separated by a single space, padded with trailing spaces.

Example. words = ["This","is","an","example"], maxWidth = 16 → line "This is an" then a left-justified last line "example ". Trace: three words fit on line 1 (4+2+2 = 8 chars of text, 8 spaces to spread across 2 gaps → 4 each).

Brute Force. There isn't a meaningfully different "naive" algorithm here — the natural approach is already linear in total characters. We present a straightforward greedy line-packer, then the same logic with the space-distribution carefully handled. First, a version that simply joins words with single spaces and pads (not fully justified) to illustrate the line-grouping step.

class Solution {
public:
    vector<string> fullJustify(vector<string>& words, int maxWidth) {
        vector<string> result;
        int i = 0;
        int n = words.size();
        while (i < n) {
            int lineStart = i;
            int lineLength = words[i].size();
            i++;
            while (i < n && lineLength + 1 + (int)words[i].size() <= maxWidth) {
                lineLength += 1 + words[i].size();
                i++;
            }
            string line = words[lineStart];
            for (int k = lineStart + 1; k < i; k++) {
                line += " " + words[k];
            }
            line.resize(maxWidth, ' ');
            result.push_back(line);
        }
        return result;
    }
};

Time: O(total characters), Space: O(total characters) for the output. This groups words correctly but doesn't spread interior spaces — it's the scaffolding.

Optimized. Greedy line-packing plus careful space distribution. Greedily fit words onto a line until the next word won't fit. For a line with gaps = wordsOnLine − 1 gaps and extraSpaces = maxWidth − totalWordLength, give each gap extraSpaces / gaps spaces and hand one extra to the first extraSpaces % gaps gaps. The last line and any single-word line are left-justified with single spaces, then padded on the right.

class Solution {
public:
    vector<string> fullJustify(vector<string>& words, int maxWidth) {
        vector<string> result;
        int i = 0;
        int n = words.size();
        while (i < n) {
            int lineStart = i;
            int lettersCount = words[i].size();
            i++;
            while (i < n && lettersCount + 1 + (int)words[i].size() <= maxWidth) {
                lettersCount += 1 + words[i].size();
                i++;
            }
            int wordsOnLine = i - lineStart;
            int gaps = wordsOnLine - 1;
            bool isLastLine = (i == n);
            string line;
            if (isLastLine || gaps == 0) {
                line = words[lineStart];
                for (int k = lineStart + 1; k < i; k++) {
                    line += " " + words[k];
                }
                line.resize(maxWidth, ' ');
            } else {
                int totalLetters = 0;
                for (int k = lineStart; k < i; k++) totalLetters += words[k].size();
                int totalSpaces = maxWidth - totalLetters;
                int baseSpaces = totalSpaces / gaps;
                int leftover = totalSpaces % gaps;
                line = words[lineStart];
                for (int k = lineStart + 1; k < i; k++) {
                    int spacesHere = baseSpaces + (k - lineStart <= leftover ? 1 : 0);
                    line += string(spacesHere, ' ') + words[k];
                }
            }
            result.push_back(line);
        }
        return result;
    }
};

Time: O(total characters), Space: O(total characters) for output. Each word and space is written once.

Interview Notes.

  • This is a simulation problem — correctness comes from handling every rule precisely; there's little algorithmic cleverness, so don't over-think the complexity.
  • The three special cases that trip people up: the last line, a single-word line (no gaps to distribute into), and leftover spaces favoring the left gaps.
  • Walk the interviewer through your line-packing condition lineLength + 1 + nextWord <= maxWidth — the +1 accounts for the mandatory space before the next word.
  • Watch integer division: baseSpaces is the even share, leftover (the remainder) is sprinkled onto the leftmost gaps one at a time.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Two Pointers›Problem 25 of 150
25

Valid Palindrome

Easy

Problem. Given a string, decide whether it reads the same forward and backward, but only considering alphanumeric characters (letters and digits) and ignoring case. So , . spaces and other punctuation are skipped entirely, and A is treated the same as a.

Example. Input "A man, a plan, a canal: Panama" → Output true. Stripping non-alphanumerics and lowercasing gives "amanaplanacanalpanama", which is the same reversed.

Brute Force. Build a cleaned string containing only the lowercased alphanumeric characters, then build its reverse, then compare the two strings for equality.

class Solution {
public:
    bool isPalindrome(string inputString) {
        string cleaned;
        for (char currentChar : inputString) {
            if (isalnum(static_cast<unsigned char>(currentChar))) {
                cleaned += tolower(static_cast<unsigned char>(currentChar));
            }
        }
        string reversed(cleaned.rbegin(), cleaned.rend());
        return cleaned == reversed;
    }
};

Time: O(n), where n is the length of the string — we scan it once to clean, once to reverse, once to compare. Space: O(n), because we allocate two extra strings (cleaned and reversed) proportional to the input size.

Optimized. The key insight is converging pointers: place one pointer at the left end and one at the right end, skip over any character that is not alphanumeric, and compare the two characters (case-folded). If they ever differ, it is not a palindrome; otherwise step both pointers inward until they meet. This avoids building any extra string.

class Solution {
public:
    bool isPalindrome(string inputString) {
        int left = 0;
        int right = static_cast<int>(inputString.size()) - 1;
        while (left < right) {
            while (left < right && !isalnum(static_cast<unsigned char>(inputString[left]))) {
                left++;
            }
            while (left < right && !isalnum(static_cast<unsigned char>(inputString[right]))) {
                right--;
            }
            if (tolower(static_cast<unsigned char>(inputString[left])) !=
                tolower(static_cast<unsigned char>(inputString[right]))) {
                return false;
            }
            left++;
            right--;
        }
        return true;
    }
};

Time: O(n) — each pointer moves inward at most n times total across the whole run, so the combined movement is linear. Space: O(1), because we only use two integer indices and no extra storage that grows with the input.

Interview Notes.

  • isalnum and tolower come from <cctype>; casting the argument to unsigned char first avoids undefined behavior on negative char values — a real gotcha interviewers notice.
  • An empty string or a string of only punctuation is considered a valid palindrome (the loop never finds a mismatch).
  • Follow-up variation: "valid palindrome with at most one deletion allowed" — that needs a small branch when a mismatch is found, trying to skip either the left or the right character.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Two Pointers›Problem 26 of 150
26

Is Subsequence

Easy

Problem. Given two strings s and t, return true if s is a subsequence of t. A subsequence means you can delete zero or more characters from t without reordering the rest, and what remains equals s. So the characters of s must appear inside t in the same relative order, but not necessarily next to each other.

Example. Input s = "abc", t = "ahbgdc" → Output true. We find a, then b, then c left-to-right inside t. Input s = "axc", t = "ahbgdc" → false, because there is no x to match.

Brute Force. For each character of s, scan t from the current position forward looking for that character, and remember where you found it so the next search starts after it. This is essentially the same scan as the optimized version but written without recognizing the pattern — there is no genuinely worse correct approach here, so the "brute" version is a straightforward nested search.

class Solution {
public:
    bool isSubsequence(string s, string t) {
        int searchStart = 0;
        for (char neededChar : s) {
            bool found = false;
            for (int textIndex = searchStart; textIndex < static_cast<int>(t.size()); textIndex++) {
                if (t[textIndex] == neededChar) {
                    searchStart = textIndex + 1;
                    found = true;
                    break;
                }
            }
            if (!found) {
                return false;
            }
        }
        return true;
    }
};

Time: O(n) where n is the length of t, because searchStart only ever advances forward and the inner loop resumes from it. Space: O(1).

Optimized. The clean pattern is two pointers moving in the same direction at different speeds: one pointer (sourceIndex) over s, one (textIndex) over t. Always advance textIndex; only advance sourceIndex when the current characters match. If sourceIndex reaches the end of s, every character was matched in order.

class Solution {
public:
    bool isSubsequence(string s, string t) {
        int sourceIndex = 0;
        int textIndex = 0;
        while (sourceIndex < static_cast<int>(s.size()) &&
               textIndex < static_cast<int>(t.size())) {
            if (s[sourceIndex] == t[textIndex]) {
                sourceIndex++;
            }
            textIndex++;
        }
        return sourceIndex == static_cast<int>(s.size());
    }
};

Time: O(n) — a single pass over t. Space: O(1), just two indices.

Interview Notes.

  • Edge case: an empty s is always a subsequence of anything, and the loop correctly returns true immediately.
  • Classic follow-up: "what if you get a stream of billions of different s values to check against the same t?" Then preprocess t once into a map from each character to a sorted list of its positions, and binary-search for the next position greater than the current one — turning each query into O(|s| log |t|).
  • This "advance one pointer only on a match" idea is the same skeleton used in merging sorted lists.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Two Pointers›Problem 27 of 150
27

Two Sum II - Input Array Is Sorted

Medium

Problem. Given a 1-indexed array of integers sorted in non-decreasing order and a target, find the two distinct positions whose values add up exactly to target. You are guaranteed exactly one solution exists, and you must return the two indices as a length-2 vector, with the smaller index first. The sortedness is the crucial detail.

Example. Input numbers = [2,7,11,15], target = 9 → Output [1,2], because numbers[0] + numbers[1] = 2 + 7 = 9, and adding 1 to each 0-based index gives the 1-based answer [1,2].

Brute Force. Try every pair of indices and check whether their values sum to the target.

class Solution {
public:
    vector<int> twoSum(vector<int>& numbers, int target) {
        int size = static_cast<int>(numbers.size());
        for (int firstIndex = 0; firstIndex < size; firstIndex++) {
            for (int secondIndex = firstIndex + 1; secondIndex < size; secondIndex++) {
                if (numbers[firstIndex] + numbers[secondIndex] == target) {
                    return {firstIndex + 1, secondIndex + 1};
                }
            }
        }
        return {};
    }
};

Time: O(n²) — for each of n elements we may scan all the others. Space: O(1). This ignores the fact that the array is sorted, which is wasteful.

Optimized. Use converging pointers driven by the sorted order. Start left at the front and right at the back and look at their sum. If the sum is too small, the only way to grow it is to move left rightward to a bigger value; if the sum is too big, move right leftward to a smaller value; if it equals the target, you are done. Because the array is sorted, each move provably cannot skip the answer.

class Solution {
public:
    vector<int> twoSum(vector<int>& numbers, int target) {
        int left = 0;
        int right = static_cast<int>(numbers.size()) - 1;
        while (left < right) {
            int currentSum = numbers[left] + numbers[right];
            if (currentSum == target) {
                return {left + 1, right + 1};
            } else if (currentSum < target) {
                left++;
            } else {
                right--;
            }
        }
        return {};
    }
};

Time: O(n) — the two pointers only move toward each other, so together they take at most n steps. Space: O(1).

Interview Notes.

  • Why moving a pointer is safe: if numbers[left] + numbers[right] is already too big, then left paired with anything >= right is also too big, so right can never be part of a solution again — discard it. The symmetric argument holds for moving left.
  • The "exactly one solution" guarantee means we never have to handle the no-answer or multiple-answer cases, but writing a final return {} keeps the compiler happy.
  • Contrast with the original "Two Sum" (unsorted): there the optimal tool is a hash map (O(n) time, O(n) space), because two pointers require sorted input.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Two Pointers›Problem 28 of 150
28

Container With Most Water

Medium

Problem. You are given an array height where height[i] is the height of a vertical line drawn at position i on the x-axis. Picking any two lines, together with the x-axis they form a container. Find the two lines that hold the most water, and return that maximum area. The water level is limited by the shorter of the two lines, and the width is the distance between their indices.

Example. Input height = [1,8,6,2,5,4,8,3,7] → Output 49. The best pair is index 1 (height 8) and index 8 (height 7): width = 8 - 1 = 7, limiting height = min(8,7) = 7, area = 7 * 7 = 49.

Brute Force. Try every pair of lines, compute the area each forms, and keep the largest.

class Solution {
public:
    int maxArea(vector<int>& height) {
        int size = static_cast<int>(height.size());
        int best = 0;
        for (int leftLine = 0; leftLine < size; leftLine++) {
            for (int rightLine = leftLine + 1; rightLine < size; rightLine++) {
                int width = rightLine - leftLine;
                int limitingHeight = min(height[leftLine], height[rightLine]);
                best = max(best, width * limitingHeight);
            }
        }
        return best;
    }
};

Time: O(n²) — we evaluate every pair. Space: O(1).

Optimized. Use converging pointers with a greedy move rule. Start with the widest possible container (left at the front, right at the back). The area is bounded by the shorter line, so moving the taller line inward can only reduce width without ever raising the limiting height — pointless. Instead, always move the pointer at the shorter line inward, hoping to find a taller line that more than compensates for the lost width.

class Solution {
public:
    int maxArea(vector<int>& height) {
        int left = 0;
        int right = static_cast<int>(height.size()) - 1;
        int best = 0;
        while (left < right) {
            int width = right - left;
            int limitingHeight = min(height[left], height[right]);
            best = max(best, width * limitingHeight);
            if (height[left] < height[right]) {
                left++;
            } else {
                right--;
            }
        }
        return best;
    }
};

Time: O(n) — one pointer moves inward on every iteration, so we do at most n steps. Space: O(1).

Interview Notes.

  • The subtle part is justifying the greedy move: discarding the shorter line is safe because any container using that shorter line with a narrower width can only be smaller (less width, same-or-lower cap), so we lose nothing by abandoning it.
  • If both lines are equal height, moving either one is fine — the code moves right, and the result is identical.
  • This is purely a max-area problem; it is not the same as "Trapping Rain Water," which sums water across all bars and uses a different (often two-pointer-with-running-max) approach. Interviewers sometimes ask both back to back.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Two Pointers›Problem 29 of 150
29

3Sum

Medium

Problem. Given an integer array nums, return all unique triplets [a, b, c] such that a + b + c == 0 and the three are at distinct positions. The result must not contain duplicate triplets (e.g. [-1,0,1] should appear only once even if multiple index combinations produce it).

Example. Input nums = [-1,0,1,2,-1,-4] → Output [[-1,-1,2],[-1,0,1]]. The values -1, -1, 2 sum to 0, and -1, 0, 1 sum to 0; no other distinct-value triplet works.

Brute Force. Try every triple of indices and check whether the values sum to zero, using a set of sorted triplets to remove duplicates.

class Solution {
public:
    vector<vector<int>> threeSum(vector<int>& nums) {
        int size = static_cast<int>(nums.size());
        set<vector<int>> uniqueTriplets;
        for (int firstIndex = 0; firstIndex < size; firstIndex++) {
            for (int secondIndex = firstIndex + 1; secondIndex < size; secondIndex++) {
                for (int thirdIndex = secondIndex + 1; thirdIndex < size; thirdIndex++) {
                    if (nums[firstIndex] + nums[secondIndex] + nums[thirdIndex] == 0) {
                        vector<int> triplet = {nums[firstIndex], nums[secondIndex], nums[thirdIndex]};
                        sort(triplet.begin(), triplet.end());
                        uniqueTriplets.insert(triplet);
                    }
                }
            }
        }
        return vector<vector<int>>(uniqueTriplets.begin(), uniqueTriplets.end());
    }
};

Time: O(n³) for the three nested loops, plus log-factor overhead inserting into the set. Space: O(number of triplets) for the set. This is too slow for large inputs.

Optimized. The pattern is sort first, then fix one element and run Two Sum II on the rest. After sorting, loop a pointer anchor over each value; for each anchor, use converging left/right pointers on the remaining sub-array to find pairs summing to -nums[anchor]. Sorting also makes duplicate-skipping easy: equal values sit next to each other, so we step past repeats for the anchor and for both inner pointers.

class Solution {
public:
    vector<vector<int>> threeSum(vector<int>& nums) {
        sort(nums.begin(), nums.end());
        int size = static_cast<int>(nums.size());
        vector<vector<int>> result;
        for (int anchor = 0; anchor < size - 2; anchor++) {
            if (anchor > 0 && nums[anchor] == nums[anchor - 1]) {
                continue;
            }
            int left = anchor + 1;
            int right = size - 1;
            while (left < right) {
                int currentSum = nums[anchor] + nums[left] + nums[right];
                if (currentSum == 0) {
                    result.push_back({nums[anchor], nums[left], nums[right]});
                    while (left < right && nums[left] == nums[left + 1]) {
                        left++;
                    }
                    while (left < right && nums[right] == nums[right - 1]) {
                        right--;
                    }
                    left++;
                    right--;
                } else if (currentSum < 0) {
                    left++;
                } else {
                    right--;
                }
            }
        }
        return result;
    }
};

Time: O(n²) — sorting is O(n log n), then for each of n anchors the inner two-pointer scan is O(n), giving O(n²) overall, which dominates. Space: O(1) extra beyond the output, ignoring the sort's internal cost and the result itself.

Interview Notes.

  • Sorting is the enabling trick: it both lets you use converging pointers (sum direction is meaningful) and groups duplicates so they are trivial to skip.
  • The three duplicate-skip checks are the most error-prone part. Skip duplicate anchors before the inner loop; skip duplicate left/right values only after recording a valid triplet.
  • An early break when nums[anchor] > 0 is a valid micro-optimization: once the smallest of three sorted values is positive, no triplet can sum to zero.
  • Natural generalizations: "3Sum Closest" (track the sum nearest the target instead of exactly zero) and "4Sum" (add one more outer loop and recurse the same idea), both built on this same sorted-plus-two-pointer scaffold.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Sliding Window›Problem 30 of 150
30

Minimum Size Subarray Sum

Medium

Problem. Given an array of positive integers nums and a positive integer target, find the minimal length of a contiguous subarray whose sum is greater than or equal to target. Return 0 if no such subarray exists. Constraints: 1 <= nums.length <= 10^5, values are positive (this positivity is what makes the window technique valid).

Example. target = 7, nums = [2,3,1,2,4,3] → output 2. The subarray [4,3] sums to 7 with length 2; no shorter subarray reaches 7.

Brute Force. Try every possible start index, and from each start extend the end forward accumulating the sum until it reaches target, recording the smallest length found.

class Solution {
public:
    int minSubArrayLen(int target, vector<int>& nums) {
        int arrayLength = nums.size();
        int shortest = INT_MAX;
        for (int start = 0; start < arrayLength; ++start) {
            int runningSum = 0;
            for (int end = start; end < arrayLength; ++end) {
                runningSum += nums[end];
                if (runningSum >= target) {
                    shortest = min(shortest, end - start + 1);
                    break;
                }
            }
        }
        return shortest == INT_MAX ? 0 : shortest;
    }
};

Time: O(n²), Space: O(1). Two nested loops over the array give the quadratic time; only a few scalar variables are used.

Optimized. Use a variable-size sliding window. Expand the right edge to add elements into windowSum. Whenever windowSum >= target, the window is valid, so record its length and then shrink from the left to find the smallest valid window ending at the current right edge. Because all values are positive, shrinking always decreases the sum, so the logic is sound.

class Solution {
public:
    int minSubArrayLen(int target, vector<int>& nums) {
        int arrayLength = nums.size();
        int shortest = INT_MAX;
        int windowSum = 0;
        int left = 0;
        for (int right = 0; right < arrayLength; ++right) {
            windowSum += nums[right];
            while (windowSum >= target) {
                shortest = min(shortest, right - left + 1);
                windowSum -= nums[left];
                ++left;
            }
        }
        return shortest == INT_MAX ? 0 : shortest;
    }
};

Time: O(n), Space: O(1). Each index is added once by right and removed at most once by left, so the total work is linear; only counters are stored.

Interview Notes.

  • The technique relies on all values being positive — with negatives, shrinking could increase the sum and the window logic breaks (you would need a prefix-sum plus a monotonic deque instead).
  • The inner while does not make this quadratic: left only ever moves forward across the whole array once.
  • Initialize the answer to INT_MAX as a sentinel meaning "no valid window yet," then translate it to 0 at the end.
  • A common follow-up asks for the O(n log n) prefix-sum + binary-search solution; mention you know it exists.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Sliding Window›Problem 31 of 150
31

Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters

Medium

Problem. Given a string inputString, find the length of the longest substring that contains no repeated characters. Constraints: 0 <= inputString.length <= 5 * 10^4; characters may be letters, digits, symbols, or spaces.

Example. inputString = "abcabcbb" → output 3. The answer is "abc"; as soon as the second a appears, the window can no longer hold the original abc start.

Brute Force. Check every substring, and for each one test whether all its characters are unique using a set; track the longest unique substring length.

class Solution {
public:
    int lengthOfLongestSubstring(string inputString) {
        int stringLength = inputString.size();
        int longest = 0;
        for (int start = 0; start < stringLength; ++start) {
            unordered_set<char> seen;
            for (int end = start; end < stringLength; ++end) {
                if (seen.count(inputString[end])) break;
                seen.insert(inputString[end]);
                longest = max(longest, end - start + 1);
            }
        }
        return longest;
    }
};

Time: O(n²), Space: O(min(n, alphabetSize)). The nested loops give quadratic time; the set holds at most one window's worth of distinct characters.

Optimized. Use a sliding window plus a hash map recording the last index at which each character was seen. Expand right one character at a time. If the current character was already seen at a position at or after left, jump left to just past that previous occurrence — this discards the duplicate without rescanning. Update the answer with each valid window length.

class Solution {
public:
    int lengthOfLongestSubstring(string inputString) {
        unordered_map<char, int> lastIndex;
        int longest = 0;
        int left = 0;
        for (int right = 0; right < static_cast<int>(inputString.size()); ++right) {
            char current = inputString[right];
            if (lastIndex.count(current) && lastIndex[current] >= left) {
                left = lastIndex[current] + 1;
            }
            lastIndex[current] = right;
            longest = max(longest, right - left + 1);
        }
        return longest;
    }
};
