                sign = -1;
            } else if (currentChar == '(') {
                ++position;
                result += sign * evaluate(expression, position);
            } else if (currentChar == ')') {
                ++position;
                return result;
            }
            ++position;
        }
        return result;
    }
};

Time: O(n) — each character is visited once even across recursion. Space: O(n) for the recursion stack on deeply nested input. (This recursive form is actually efficient; it is labelled "brute" only as the more obvious first solution.)

Optimized. Use an explicit stack to handle parentheses iteratively, avoiding recursion depth limits. Keep a running result, a current sign (+1 or -1), and a number buffer. On (, push the current result and sign, then reset them to start a fresh sub-expression. On ), finalize the inner result, then pop the saved sign and outer result to fold the inner value back in.

class Solution {
public:
    int calculate(string expression) {
        stack<int> savedState;
        int result = 0;
        int sign = 1;
        int currentNumber = 0;
        for (char currentChar : expression) {
            if (isdigit(currentChar)) {
                currentNumber = currentNumber * 10 + (currentChar - '0');
            } else if (currentChar == '+') {
                result += sign * currentNumber;
                currentNumber = 0;
                sign = 1;
            } else if (currentChar == '-') {
                result += sign * currentNumber;
                currentNumber = 0;
                sign = -1;
            } else if (currentChar == '(') {
                savedState.push(result);
                savedState.push(sign);
                result = 0;
                sign = 1;
            } else if (currentChar == ')') {
                result += sign * currentNumber;
                currentNumber = 0;
                int savedSign = savedState.top(); savedState.pop();
                int savedResult = savedState.top(); savedState.pop();
                result = savedResult + savedSign * result;
            }
        }
        return result + sign * currentNumber;
    }
};

Time: O(n) — one pass over the string. Space: O(n) — the stack grows with parenthesis nesting depth.

Interview Notes.

  • The mental model: a ( opens a new accumulation context; you stash the outer result and the sign that multiplies the whole upcoming group, then start fresh at zero.
  • Multi-digit parsing means you only commit currentNumber into result when you hit an operator or ) — don't forget the final commit after the loop ends.
  • Spaces are simply skipped because they match none of the branches; no explicit handling needed.
  • This is the no-multiply/divide variant. Its sibling Basic Calculator II adds * and / (handled with a running-stack-of-terms approach), and Basic Calculator III combines both — mention the family if asked to generalize.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Linked List›Problem 57 of 150
57

Linked List Cycle

Easy

Problem. Given the head of a linked list, return true if the list contains a cycle (some node's next eventually points back to an earlier node, so a walk never ends) and false otherwise. Example. List 3 -> 2 -> 0 -> -4, where -4->next points back to the node 2. Walking from the head we loop forever, so the answer is true. Brute Force. Walk the list and remember every node address we have already visited in a hash set. If we ever reach a node already in the set, there is a cycle; if we hit nullptr, there is not.

bool hasCycle(ListNode* head) {
    unordered_set<ListNode*> visited;
    ListNode* current = head;
    while (current != nullptr) {
        if (visited.count(current)) {
            return true;
        }
        visited.insert(current);
        current = current->next;
    }
    return false;
}

Time: O(n), Space: O(n). We touch each node once, but the set can grow to hold every node.

Optimized. The key insight is Floyd's cycle detection (the "tortoise and hare"). Move a slow pointer one step and a fast pointer two steps per iteration. If there is no cycle, fast reaches the end. If there is a cycle, fast keeps lapping the loop and must eventually land on the same node as slow.

bool hasCycle(ListNode* head) {
    ListNode* slow = head;
    ListNode* fast = head;
    while (fast != nullptr && fast->next != nullptr) {
        slow = slow->next;
        fast = fast->next->next;
        if (slow == fast) {
            return true;
        }
    }
    return false;
}

Time: O(n), Space: O(1). Each pointer travels at most a linear number of steps, and we store only two pointers.

Interview Notes.

  • Always guard both fast != nullptr and fast->next != nullptr before doing fast->next->next, or you will dereference null.
  • The pointers are compared by address (slow == fast), not by value.
  • A common follow-up (LeetCode 142) asks for the node where the cycle begins: reset one pointer to head and advance both one step at a time until they meet again.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Linked List›Problem 58 of 150
58

Add Two Numbers

Medium

Problem. Two non-empty lists represent non-negative integers with digits stored in reverse order (ones digit first). Add the numbers and return the sum as a list in the same reverse order. Example. 2 -> 4 -> 3 is 342 and 5 -> 6 -> 4 is 465. Their sum is 807, returned as 7 -> 0 -> 8. Brute Force. The tempting naive route is to walk both lists building the full integers, add them, then split the result back into digits. This breaks immediately because the numbers can have hundreds of digits and overflow any built-in integer type, so it is not a viable solution here. We still need digit-by-digit addition; the version below is the straightforward one.

ListNode* addTwoNumbers(ListNode* firstList, ListNode* secondList) {
    ListNode dummy;
    ListNode* current = &dummy;
    int carry = 0;
    while (firstList != nullptr || secondList != nullptr || carry != 0) {
        int sum = carry;
        if (firstList != nullptr) {
            sum += firstList->val;
            firstList = firstList->next;
        }
        if (secondList != nullptr) {
            sum += secondList->val;
            secondList = secondList->next;
        }
        carry = sum / 10;
        current->next = new ListNode(sum % 10);
        current = current->next;
    }
    return dummy.next;
}

Time: O(max(m, n)), Space: O(max(m, n)). We process one digit per step and allocate one output node per digit.

Optimized. The code above is already optimal; the pattern that makes it clean is the dummy head. We build the result behind a throwaway dummy node so we never special-case the first digit, and we return dummy.next. The single loop condition || carry != 0 elegantly handles a final carry (e.g. 5 + 5 = 10 producing a new leading digit).

Time: O(max(m, n)), Space: O(max(m, n)). Same as above; there is no asymptotically better approach because every output digit must be created.

Interview Notes.

  • The carry is 0 or 1 here, but writing carry = sum / 10 generalizes cleanly.
  • Declaring ListNode dummy; on the stack avoids a leak from a new dummy you would otherwise have to delete.
  • Watch the loop condition: forgetting carry != 0 drops the final carry digit.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Linked List›Problem 59 of 150
59

Merge Two Sorted Lists

Easy

Problem. Given the heads of two lists sorted in non-decreasing order, splice them into one sorted list and return its head. The result should reuse the existing nodes. Example. 1 -> 2 -> 4 and 1 -> 3 -> 4 merge into 1 -> 1 -> 2 -> 3 -> 4 -> 4. Brute Force. Collect every value from both lists into a vector, sort it, then build a fresh list from the sorted values.

ListNode* mergeTwoLists(ListNode* firstList, ListNode* secondList) {
    vector<int> values;
    for (ListNode* current = firstList; current != nullptr; current = current->next) {
        values.push_back(current->val);
    }
    for (ListNode* current = secondList; current != nullptr; current = current->next) {
        values.push_back(current->val);
    }
    sort(values.begin(), values.end());
    ListNode dummy;
    ListNode* tail = &dummy;
    for (int value : values) {
        tail->next = new ListNode(value);
        tail = tail->next;
    }
    return dummy.next;
}

Time: O((m + n) log(m + n)), Space: O(m + n). The sort dominates, and we allocate a whole new list.

Optimized. The key insight is that both inputs are already sorted, so we can merge with two pointers using a dummy head. Repeatedly compare the front nodes of the two lists, append the smaller one to the result, and advance that list. When one list runs out, attach the remainder of the other.

ListNode* mergeTwoLists(ListNode* firstList, ListNode* secondList) {
    ListNode dummy;
    ListNode* tail = &dummy;
    while (firstList != nullptr && secondList != nullptr) {
        if (firstList->val <= secondList->val) {
            tail->next = firstList;
            firstList = firstList->next;
        } else {
            tail->next = secondList;
            secondList = secondList->next;
        }
        tail = tail->next;
    }
    tail->next = (firstList != nullptr) ? firstList : secondList;
    return dummy.next;
}

Time: O(m + n), Space: O(1). We visit each node once and allocate nothing new (only the stack dummy).

Interview Notes.

  • Use <= rather than < to keep the merge stable (preserves relative order of equal values).
  • The final line stitching on the leftover tail is what makes the dummy-head version short; don't loop those nodes one at a time.
  • This merge step is the core of merge sort on a linked list, a common follow-up.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Linked List›Problem 60 of 150
60

Copy List with Random Pointer

Medium

Problem. Each node has the usual next plus a random pointer that may point to any node in the list or to nullptr. Return a deep copy: a brand-new list whose nodes mirror both the next and random structure, sharing no nodes with the original. Example. If node A's random points to node C, then in the copy, A's clone's random must point to C's clone (not to the original C). Brute Force. Make a first pass creating each clone and storing a mapping from each original node to its clone in a hash map. Make a second pass to set every clone's next and random by looking up the originals' targets in the map.

class Node {
public:
    int val;
    Node* next;
    Node* random;
    Node(int value) : val(value), next(nullptr), random(nullptr) {}
};

Node* copyRandomList(Node* head) {
    unordered_map<Node*, Node*> originalToClone;
    Node* current = head;
    while (current != nullptr) {
        originalToClone[current] = new Node(current->val);
        current = current->next;
    }
    current = head;
    while (current != nullptr) {
        originalToClone[current]->next = originalToClone[current->next];
        originalToClone[current]->random = originalToClone[current->random];
        current = current->next;
    }
    return originalToClone[head];
}

Time: O(n), Space: O(n). Two linear passes plus a map holding every node. (Looking up originalToClone[nullptr] returns a value-initialized nullptr, which is exactly what we want for end-of-list and null randoms.)

Optimized. The clever trick removes the map by interleaving cloned nodes. First weave each clone directly after its original (A -> A' -> B -> B' ...). Now each clone sits right after the node whose random it must mirror, so clone->random = original->random->next. Finally unzip the two lists apart.

Node* copyRandomList(Node* head) {
    if (head == nullptr) {
        return nullptr;
    }
    Node* current = head;
    while (current != nullptr) {
        Node* clone = new Node(current->val);
        clone->next = current->next;
        current->next = clone;
        current = clone->next;
    }
    current = head;
    while (current != nullptr) {
        if (current->random != nullptr) {
            current->next->random = current->random->next;
        }
        current = current->next->next;
    }
    Node* cloneHead = head->next;
    current = head;
    while (current != nullptr) {
        Node* clone = current->next;
        current->next = clone->next;
        if (clone->next != nullptr) {
            clone->next = clone->next->next;
        }
        current = current->next;
    }
    return cloneHead;
}

Time: O(n), Space: O(1) extra. Three linear passes, no auxiliary map (output nodes don't count as extra).

Interview Notes.

  • The hash-map version is perfectly acceptable in an interview and far easier to get right under pressure; mention the interleaving trick as the O(1)-space optimization.
  • In the unzip pass you must restore the original list's next pointers, or you corrupt the input.
  • Always handle the empty list up front, since head->next would otherwise crash.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Linked List›Problem 61 of 150
61

Reverse Linked List II

Medium

Problem. Given the head and two 1-based positions left <= right, reverse only the nodes from position left to position right, leaving the rest intact. Return the new head. Example. 1 -> 2 -> 3 -> 4 -> 5 with left = 2, right = 4 becomes 1 -> 4 -> 3 -> 2 -> 5. Brute Force. Copy the values of the segment into a vector, reverse the vector, and write the values back into the same nodes. This avoids pointer surgery entirely.

ListNode* reverseBetween(ListNode* head, int left, int right) {
    vector<int> segment;
    ListNode* current = head;
    int position = 1;
    while (current != nullptr && position < left) {
        current = current->next;
        position++;
    }
    ListNode* segmentStart = current;
    while (current != nullptr && position <= right) {
        segment.push_back(current->val);
        current = current->next;
        position++;
    }
    current = segmentStart;
    for (int index = segment.size() - 1; index >= 0; index--) {
        current->val = segment[index];
        current = current->next;
    }
    return head;
}

Time: O(n), Space: O(right - left + 1). Linear walk plus a vector sized to the segment.

Optimized. The pattern is dummy head + in-place reversal by splicing. Place a dummy before head so left = 1 is not special. Walk a beforeSegment pointer to the node just before position left. Then repeatedly take the node after the current first segment node and splice it to the front of the segment (head-insertion), which reverses the segment in O(1) extra space.

ListNode* reverseBetween(ListNode* head, int left, int right) {
    ListNode dummy(0, head);
    ListNode* beforeSegment = &dummy;
    for (int step = 1; step < left; step++) {
        beforeSegment = beforeSegment->next;
    }
    ListNode* segmentHead = beforeSegment->next;
    for (int step = 0; step < right - left; step++) {
        ListNode* movedNode = segmentHead->next;
        segmentHead->next = movedNode->next;
        movedNode->next = beforeSegment->next;
        beforeSegment->next = movedNode;
    }
    return dummy.next;
}

Time: O(n), Space: O(1). One walk to the segment plus a constant-work splice per reversed node.

Interview Notes.

  • segmentHead stays fixed as the node that ends up last in the reversed segment; only movedNode changes each iteration.
  • The loop runs right - left times, not right - left + 1, because head-insertion of the others is enough.
  • Returning dummy.next (not head) is essential when left == 1, since the original head moves.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Linked List›Problem 62 of 150
62

Reverse Nodes in k-Group

Hard

Problem. Reverse the nodes of the list k at a time and return the modified list. If the number of remaining nodes is fewer than k, leave them as they are. You may not just swap values. Example. 1 -> 2 -> 3 -> 4 -> 5 with k = 2 becomes 2 -> 1 -> 4 -> 3 -> 5 (the trailing 5 is a leftover group of size 1). Brute Force. Since values may not be swapped, the conceptually simplest version walks group by group: for each group of k, detach it, reverse it with a standard reversal loop, and reconnect. We implement this with a helper that reverses a fixed count of nodes.

ListNode* reverseKGroup(ListNode* head, int k) {
    ListNode* groupStart = head;
    for (int count = 0; count < k; count++) {
        if (groupStart == nullptr) {
            return head;
        }
        groupStart = groupStart->next;
    }
    ListNode* previous = reverseKGroup(groupStart, k);
    ListNode* current = head;
    for (int count = 0; count < k; count++) {
        ListNode* nextNode = current->next;
        current->next = previous;
        previous = current;
        current = nextNode;
    }
    return previous;
}

Time: O(n), Space: O(n / k). Each node is reversed once, but the recursion stacks one frame per group.

Optimized. The iterative version uses a dummy head and a group-counting pointer to avoid recursion's stack space. We keep a groupPrev pointer before each group, check that a full group of k nodes exists by advancing a probe, and if so reverse that group in place and relink it.

ListNode* reverseKGroup(ListNode* head, int k) {
    ListNode dummy(0, head);
    ListNode* groupPrev = &dummy;
    while (true) {
        ListNode* probe = groupPrev;
        for (int count = 0; count < k && probe != nullptr; count++) {
            probe = probe->next;
        }
        if (probe == nullptr) {
            break;
        }
        ListNode* groupStart = groupPrev->next;
        ListNode* current = groupStart->next;
        for (int count = 0; count < k - 1; count++) {
            groupStart->next = current->next;
            current->next = groupPrev->next;
            groupPrev->next = current;
            current = groupStart->next;
        }
        groupPrev = groupStart;
    }
    return dummy.next;
}

Time: O(n), Space: O(1). Each node is moved a constant number of times and we keep only a few pointers.

Interview Notes.

  • The probe loop confirming a full group is what implements "leave a short tail alone".
  • After reversing, groupStart is the node that became the group's tail, so it is the correct next groupPrev.
  • The recursive version is easier to write under pressure; state the O(n/k) stack cost when you offer it.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Linked List›Problem 63 of 150
63

Remove Nth Node From End of List

Medium

Problem. Remove the n-th node counting from the end of the list and return the head. It is guaranteed n is valid. Example. 1 -> 2 -> 3 -> 4 -> 5 with n = 2 removes the 4, giving 1 -> 2 -> 3 -> 5. Brute Force. Walk once to count the length L, then walk again to the node just before position L - n (1-based from the front) and unlink the target.

ListNode* removeNthFromEnd(ListNode* head, int n) {
    int length = 0;
    for (ListNode* current = head; current != nullptr; current = current->next) {
        length++;
    }
    ListNode dummy(0, head);
    ListNode* beforeTarget = &dummy;
    for (int step = 0; step < length - n; step++) {
        beforeTarget = beforeTarget->next;
    }
    ListNode* target = beforeTarget->next;
    beforeTarget->next = target->next;
    delete target;
    return dummy.next;
}

Time: O(n) (two passes, still linear), Space: O(1). Two walks of the list, constant extra storage.

Optimized. The pattern is a two-pointer gap plus a dummy head, doing it in one pass. Advance a fast pointer n nodes ahead, then move fast and a slow pointer (starting at the dummy) together until fast reaches the last node. Now slow sits exactly before the node to remove.

ListNode* removeNthFromEnd(ListNode* head, int n) {
    ListNode dummy(0, head);
    ListNode* fast = &dummy;
    ListNode* slow = &dummy;
    for (int step = 0; step < n; step++) {
        fast = fast->next;
    }
    while (fast->next != nullptr) {
        fast = fast->next;
        slow = slow->next;
    }
    ListNode* target = slow->next;
    slow->next = target->next;
    delete target;
    return dummy.next;
}

Time: O(n), Space: O(1). A single linear pass with two pointers.

Interview Notes.

  • The dummy head is what lets you remove the actual head (n == length) without a special branch.
  • The gap between fast and slow is held at n, so when fast hits the tail, slow is one before the target.
  • Remember to delete the removed node to avoid a leak when the interviewer cares about memory.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Linked List›Problem 64 of 150
64

Remove Duplicates from Sorted List II

Medium

Problem. Given a sorted list, delete all nodes that have a duplicate value, leaving only values that appeared exactly once. Return the head. Example. 1 -> 2 -> 3 -> 3 -> 4 -> 4 -> 5 becomes 1 -> 2 -> 5 (both 3s and both 4s are removed entirely). Brute Force. Count occurrences of each value with a hash map in one pass, then build a new list of values whose count is exactly one. Because the input is sorted we could instead use the original order; here we keep it simple with a map.

ListNode* deleteDuplicates(ListNode* head) {
    unordered_map<int, int> counts;
    for (ListNode* current = head; current != nullptr; current = current->next) {
        counts[current->val]++;
    }
    ListNode dummy;
    ListNode* tail = &dummy;
    for (ListNode* current = head; current != nullptr; current = current->next) {
        if (counts[current->val] == 1) {
            tail->next = new ListNode(current->val);
            tail = tail->next;
        }
    }
    return dummy.next;
}

Time: O(n), Space: O(n). One pass to count, one to rebuild, plus a map and a new list.

Optimized. Because the list is sorted, equal values are adjacent, so we can do it in place with a dummy head. Keep previous at the last node known to be unique. When the node after previous starts a run of equal values, skip the entire run; otherwise advance previous.

ListNode* deleteDuplicates(ListNode* head) {
    ListNode dummy(0, head);
    ListNode* previous = &dummy;
    ListNode* current = head;
    while (current != nullptr) {
        if (current->next != nullptr && current->val == current->next->val) {
            int duplicateValue = current->val;
            while (current != nullptr && current->val == duplicateValue) {
                current = current->next;
            }
            previous->next = current;
        } else {
            previous = current;
            current = current->next;
        }
    }
    return dummy.next;
}

Time: O(n), Space: O(1). One pass, only re-linking pointers.

Interview Notes.

  • Note the difference from the easier version (LeetCode 83) which keeps one copy; here a duplicated value is removed entirely.
  • Only advance previous when the current node is confirmed unique, otherwise it would point into the deleted run.
  • previous->next = current after skipping bridges over the whole duplicate block in one assignment.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Linked List›Problem 65 of 150
65

Rotate List

Medium

Problem. Rotate the list to the right by k places, where k may be larger than the list length. Return the new head. Example. 1 -> 2 -> 3 -> 4 -> 5 with k = 2 becomes 4 -> 5 -> 1 -> 2 -> 3 (the last two nodes move to the front). Brute Force. Rotate one step at a time, k times: each step finds the last node, moves it to the front. With k reduced modulo length first, this is correct but does redundant full walks.

ListNode* rotateRight(ListNode* head, int k) {
    if (head == nullptr || head->next == nullptr) {
        return head;
    }
    int length = 0;
    for (ListNode* current = head; current != nullptr; current = current->next) {
        length++;
    }
    k = k % length;
    for (int rotation = 0; rotation < k; rotation++) {
        ListNode* beforeLast = head;
        while (beforeLast->next->next != nullptr) {
            beforeLast = beforeLast->next;
        }
        ListNode* lastNode = beforeLast->next;
        beforeLast->next = nullptr;
        lastNode->next = head;
        head = lastNode;
    }
    return head;
}

Time: O(n * k) (up to O(n^2) before the modulo, O(n * (k mod n)) after), Space: O(1). Each of k rotations re-walks the list.

Optimized. The insight is to close the list into a ring, then cut it at the right place. Find the length and the tail, connect the tail to the head making a cycle, then walk length - (k % length) steps from the head to the new tail, and break the ring there.

ListNode* rotateRight(ListNode* head, int k) {
    if (head == nullptr || head->next == nullptr) {
        return head;
    }
    int length = 1;
    ListNode* tail = head;
    while (tail->next != nullptr) {
        tail = tail->next;
        length++;
    }
    int shift = k % length;
    if (shift == 0) {
        return head;
    }
    tail->next = head;
    int stepsToNewTail = length - shift;
    ListNode* newTail = head;
    for (int step = 1; step < stepsToNewTail; step++) {
        newTail = newTail->next;
    }
    ListNode* newHead = newTail->next;
    newTail->next = nullptr;
    return newHead;
}

Time: O(n), Space: O(1). One pass to measure, one partial pass to the cut point.

Interview Notes.

  • Always reduce k modulo the length first; k can be far larger than the list.
  • The new head is the node at index length - (k % length) from the front.
  • Forgetting to set the new tail's next to nullptr leaves a cycle, an easy bug to make.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Linked List›Problem 66 of 150
66

Partition List

Medium

Problem. Given a list and a value partitionValue, reorder it so all nodes with value less than partitionValue come before all nodes greater than or equal to it. The relative order within each group must be preserved. Example. 1 -> 4 -> 3 -> 2 -> 5 -> 2 with partitionValue = 3 becomes 1 -> 2 -> 2 -> 4 -> 3 -> 5. Brute Force. Make two passes: first copy the values less than the pivot into a result list in order, then append the values greater-or-equal in order. Building new nodes makes the bookkeeping trivial.

ListNode* partition(ListNode* head, int partitionValue) {
    ListNode dummy;
    ListNode* tail = &dummy;
    for (ListNode* current = head; current != nullptr; current = current->next) {
        if (current->val < partitionValue) {
            tail->next = new ListNode(current->val);
            tail = tail->next;
        }
    }
    for (ListNode* current = head; current != nullptr; current = current->next) {
        if (current->val >= partitionValue) {
            tail->next = new ListNode(current->val);
            tail = tail->next;
        }
    }
    return dummy.next;
}

Time: O(n), Space: O(n). Two passes and a fully new list.

Optimized. The clean pattern is two dummy heads, one per bucket. Walk once, appending each original node to either the "less" list or the "greater-or-equal" list, then join the two lists. This reuses the existing nodes and keeps relative order automatically.

ListNode* partition(ListNode* head, int partitionValue) {
    ListNode lessDummy;
    ListNode greaterDummy;
    ListNode* lessTail = &lessDummy;
    ListNode* greaterTail = &greaterDummy;
    for (ListNode* current = head; current != nullptr; current = current->next) {
        if (current->val < partitionValue) {
            lessTail->next = current;
            lessTail = lessTail->next;
        } else {
            greaterTail->next = current;
            greaterTail = greaterTail->next;
        }
    }
    greaterTail->next = nullptr;
    lessTail->next = greaterDummy.next;
    return lessDummy.next;
}

Time: O(n), Space: O(1). A single pass, only relinking existing nodes.

Interview Notes.

  • Setting greaterTail->next = nullptr at the end is mandatory; the last greater node may still point into the original list and create a cycle.
  • Two dummy heads remove all the "is this the first node in the bucket" special cases.
  • Relative order is preserved for free because we append in traversal order.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Linked List›Problem 67 of 150
67

LRU Cache

Medium

Problem. Design a cache with a fixed capacity supporting get(key) and put(key, value), both in O(1) average time. When the cache is full, put evicts the least recently used entry. Any get or put on a key counts as a use, making it most recently used. Example. With capacity 2: put(1,1), put(2,2), get(1) returns 1, put(3,3) evicts key 2 (least recently used), then get(2) returns -1. Brute Force. Store entries in a list of (key, value) pairs and track recency by position. get scans the list for the key; on any access, move the entry to the front; put evicts from the back when full. The scan makes operations linear.

class LRUCache {
    int capacity;
    list<pair<int, int>> entries;
public:
    LRUCache(int cacheCapacity) : capacity(cacheCapacity) {}

    int get(int key) {
        for (auto iterator = entries.begin(); iterator != entries.end(); ++iterator) {
            if (iterator->first == key) {
                pair<int, int> found = *iterator;
                entries.erase(iterator);
                entries.push_front(found);
                return found.second;
            }
        }
        return -1;
    }

    void put(int key, int value) {
        for (auto iterator = entries.begin(); iterator != entries.end(); ++iterator) {
            if (iterator->first == key) {
                entries.erase(iterator);
                break;
            }
        }
        entries.push_front({key, value});
        if ((int)entries.size() > capacity) {
            entries.pop_back();
        }
    }
};

Time: O(n) per operation, Space: O(capacity). Each call scans the whole list to find the key.

Optimized. The classic design is a hash map + doubly linked list. The doubly linked list keeps entries ordered by recency (front = most recent, back = least recent); the hash map maps each key to its node, so we find any entry in O(1). On access we unlink the node and re-attach it at the front; on overflow we drop the back node. C++'s std::list gives O(1) splice and stable iterators, so we store iterators in the map.

class LRUCache {
    int capacity;
    list<pair<int, int>> entries;
    unordered_map<int, list<pair<int, int>>::iterator> keyToNode;
public:
    LRUCache(int cacheCapacity) : capacity(cacheCapacity) {}

    int get(int key) {
        auto found = keyToNode.find(key);
        if (found == keyToNode.end()) {
            return -1;
        }
        entries.splice(entries.begin(), entries, found->second);
        return found->second->second;
    }

    void put(int key, int value) {
        auto found = keyToNode.find(key);
        if (found != keyToNode.end()) {
            found->second->second = value;
            entries.splice(entries.begin(), entries, found->second);
            return;
        }
        if ((int)entries.size() == capacity) {
            int leastRecentKey = entries.back().first;
            keyToNode.erase(leastRecentKey);
            entries.pop_back();
        }
        entries.push_front({key, value});
        keyToNode[key] = entries.begin();
    }
};

Time: O(1) average per operation, Space: O(capacity). The map lookup and the list splice are both constant time.

Interview Notes.

  • entries.splice(entries.begin(), entries, iterator) moves a node to the front in O(1) without invalidating any iterator, which is exactly why std::list is the right container.
  • After splice, the stored iterator stays valid, so the map does not need updating on a get.
  • On eviction you must erase the key from the map before popping the node, using entries.back().first to get that key.
  • Interviewers often want you to hand-roll the doubly linked list (custom node with prev/next) rather than use std::list; mention you can, but std::list is cleaner and equally O(1).
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Binary Tree (General)›Problem 68 of 150
68

Maximum Depth of Binary Tree

Easy

Problem. Return the maximum depth of a binary tree: the number of nodes along the longest path from the root down to the farthest leaf. An empty tree has depth 0; a single node has depth 1. Example. Tree [3,9,20,null,null,15,7] → 3. Path 3 → 20 → 15 (or 3 → 20 → 7) has three nodes, longer than the path 3 → 9. Brute Force. Even the "brute force" here is recursion, since iterating a tree by hand without recursion or a stack is awkward. The cleanest first approach is a breadth-first level count: walk the tree level by level with a queue, incrementing a counter once per level. This uses extra memory for the queue but is intuitive — the depth is simply how many levels exist.

class Solution {
public:
    int maxDepth(TreeNode* root) {
        if (root == nullptr) return 0;
        queue<TreeNode*> nodesAtCurrentLevels;
        nodesAtCurrentLevels.push(root);
        int depth = 0;
        while (!nodesAtCurrentLevels.empty()) {
            int countThisLevel = nodesAtCurrentLevels.size();
            for (int index = 0; index < countThisLevel; ++index) {
                TreeNode* current = nodesAtCurrentLevels.front();
                nodesAtCurrentLevels.pop();
                if (current->left) nodesAtCurrentLevels.push(current->left);
                if (current->right) nodesAtCurrentLevels.push(current->right);
            }
            ++depth;
        }
        return depth;
    }
};

Time: O(n) — every node is enqueued and dequeued once. Space: O(w) where w is the maximum width of the tree; in the worst case (a full bottom level) that is O(n). Optimized. The recursive insight is one line of reasoning: the depth of a tree is 1 (for the root) plus the larger of the depths of its two subtrees. Define the function to return the depth of whatever node it is handed; the base case nullptr returns 0.

class Solution {
public:
    int maxDepth(TreeNode* root) {
        if (root == nullptr) return 0;
        int leftDepth = maxDepth(root->left);
        int rightDepth = maxDepth(root->right);
        return 1 + max(leftDepth, rightDepth);
    }
};

Time: O(n) — each node is visited exactly once. Space: O(h) where h is the height of the tree, for the recursion call stack; this is O(log n) for a balanced tree and O(n) for a degenerate (linked-list-shaped) tree. Interview Notes.

  • This is the canonical first tree problem; internalize the shape "base case for null, recurse on both children, combine."
  • The recursive version's space cost is hidden in the call stack — interviewers like to hear that h can be n in the worst case.
  • BFS is preferred only if you must avoid recursion entirely (e.g. extremely deep trees that would overflow the stack).
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Binary Tree (General)›Problem 69 of 150
69

Same Tree

Easy

Problem. Given the roots of two binary trees, return true if they are structurally identical and every corresponding node holds the same value. Example. p = [1,2,3], q = [1,2,3] → true. p = [1,2], q = [1,null,2] → false (the 2 is a left child in one tree, a right child in the other). Brute Force. Serialize both trees into strings using a traversal that records structure (including nulls), then compare the strings. This works but allocates strings and is clumsier than direct comparison.

class Solution {
public:
    void serialize(TreeNode* node, string& output) {
        if (node == nullptr) { output += "#,"; return; }
        output += to_string(node->val) + ",";
        serialize(node->left, output);
        serialize(node->right, output);
    }
    bool isSameTree(TreeNode* p, TreeNode* q) {
        string firstSerialized, secondSerialized;
        serialize(p, firstSerialized);
        serialize(q, secondSerialized);
        return firstSerialized == secondSerialized;
    }
};

Time: O(n) to walk both trees. Space: O(n) for the two serialized strings plus the recursion stack. Optimized. Compare the two trees in lockstep, node by node. At each step both nodes must be null together (a match), or both non-null with equal values and matching subtrees; any other case is a mismatch.

class Solution {
public:
    bool isSameTree(TreeNode* p, TreeNode* q) {
        if (p == nullptr && q == nullptr) return true;
        if (p == nullptr || q == nullptr) return false;
        if (p->val != q->val) return false;
        return isSameTree(p->left, q->left) && isSameTree(p->right, q->right);
    }
};

Time: O(n) where n is the size of the smaller tree (recursion stops at the first mismatch). Space: O(h) for the call stack. Interview Notes.

  • The order of the three guard clauses matters: check "both null" before "one null," otherwise you would dereference a null pointer.
  • && short-circuits, so a mismatch in the left subtree avoids walking the right — a small but free optimization.
  • This lockstep pattern reappears in Symmetric Tree (problem 71).
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Binary Tree (General)›Problem 70 of 150
70

Invert Binary Tree

Easy

Problem. Invert (mirror) a binary tree: for every node, swap its left and right children. Return the new root. Example. [4,2,7,1,3,6,9] → [4,7,2,9,6,3,1]. The root keeps its value; the whole left subtree and right subtree trade places, recursively. Brute Force. Build a brand-new mirrored tree without modifying the original, allocating fresh nodes. This is wasteful in memory but illustrates the mirroring clearly: a new node's left child is the inversion of the original's right child.

class Solution {
public:
    TreeNode* invertTree(TreeNode* root) {
        if (root == nullptr) return nullptr;
        TreeNode* mirrored = new TreeNode(root->val);
        mirrored->left = invertTree(root->right);
        mirrored->right = invertTree(root->left);
        return mirrored;
    }
};

Time: O(n). Space: O(n) for the newly allocated tree plus O(h) recursion stack. Optimized. Invert in place: at each node, swap the two child pointers, then recurse into both children. No new nodes are created.

class Solution {
public:
    TreeNode* invertTree(TreeNode* root) {
        if (root == nullptr) return nullptr;
        TreeNode* originalLeft = root->left;
        root->left = invertTree(root->right);
        root->right = invertTree(originalLeft);
        return root;
    }
};

Time: O(n) — every node visited once. Space: O(h) for the recursion stack. Interview Notes.

  • Save the original left pointer before overwriting it; if you call invertTree(root->right) and assign it to root->left first, the old root->left is gone unless you stashed it.
  • An iterative version using a queue or stack works identically — pop a node, swap its children, push them.
  • This is the famous "whiteboard the inverter" problem; the in-place three-line solution is what interviewers expect.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Binary Tree (General)›Problem 71 of 150
71

Symmetric Tree

Easy

Problem. Return true if a binary tree is a mirror image of itself around its center — i.e. the left subtree is the mirror reflection of the right subtree. Example. [1,2,2,3,4,4,3] → true. [1,2,2,null,3,null,3] → false (the two 3s sit on the same side, breaking the mirror). Brute Force. Invert the right subtree into a new tree, then check whether the (inverted right) equals the left subtree using the Same-Tree comparison. Correct, but it allocates a whole mirrored copy.

class Solution {
public:
    TreeNode* invert(TreeNode* node) {
        if (node == nullptr) return nullptr;
        TreeNode* copy = new TreeNode(node->val);
        copy->left = invert(node->right);
        copy->right = invert(node->left);
        return copy;
    }
    bool sameTree(TreeNode* first, TreeNode* second) {
        if (first == nullptr && second == nullptr) return true;
        if (first == nullptr || second == nullptr) return false;
        return first->val == second->val
            && sameTree(first->left, second->left)
            && sameTree(first->right, second->right);
    }
    bool isSymmetric(TreeNode* root) {
        if (root == nullptr) return true;
        return sameTree(root->left, invert(root->right));
    }
};

Time: O(n). Space: O(n) for the inverted copy plus O(h) recursion stack. Optimized. Compare the two subtrees as mirrors directly, without building anything: a left node and a right node mirror each other when their values match, the left's left mirrors the right's right, and the left's right mirrors the right's left.

class Solution {
public:
    bool isMirror(TreeNode* leftSubtree, TreeNode* rightSubtree) {
        if (leftSubtree == nullptr && rightSubtree == nullptr) return true;
        if (leftSubtree == nullptr || rightSubtree == nullptr) return false;
        if (leftSubtree->val != rightSubtree->val) return false;
        return isMirror(leftSubtree->left, rightSubtree->right)
            && isMirror(leftSubtree->right, rightSubtree->left);
    }
    bool isSymmetric(TreeNode* root) {
        if (root == nullptr) return true;
        return isMirror(root->left, root->right);
    }
};

Time: O(n). Space: O(h) for the call stack. Interview Notes.

  • The crux is the cross-pairing: left->left with right->right, and left->right with right->left. Mixing these up is the most common bug.
  • An iterative version pushes pairs onto a queue and processes two nodes at a time.
  • Note how this reuses the Same-Tree null-handling pattern, just with mirrored recursion.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Binary Tree (General)›Problem 72 of 150
72

Construct Binary Tree from Preorder and Inorder Traversal

Medium

Problem. Given the preorder and inorder traversal sequences of a binary tree (all values distinct), reconstruct and return the tree. Preorder visits node-left-right; inorder visits left-node-right. Example. preorder = [3,9,20,15,7], inorder = [9,3,15,20,7] → tree [3,9,20,null,null,15,7]. The first preorder element 3 is the root; in inorder, everything left of 3 ([9]) is the left subtree, everything right ([15,20,7]) is the right. Brute Force. At each step, the first remaining preorder value is the current root; find it inside the current inorder range with a linear scan, split into left and right halves, and recurse. The linear search is the inefficiency.

class Solution {
public:
    TreeNode* build(vector<int>& preorder, int preStart, int preEnd,
                    vector<int>& inorder, int inStart, int inEnd) {
        if (preStart > preEnd) return nullptr;
        int rootValue = preorder[preStart];
        TreeNode* root = new TreeNode(rootValue);
        int rootIndexInInorder = inStart;
        while (inorder[rootIndexInInorder] != rootValue) ++rootIndexInInorder;
        int leftSubtreeSize = rootIndexInInorder - inStart;
        root->left = build(preorder, preStart + 1, preStart + leftSubtreeSize,
                           inorder, inStart, rootIndexInInorder - 1);
        root->right = build(preorder, preStart + leftSubtreeSize + 1, preEnd,
                            inorder, rootIndexInInorder + 1, inEnd);
        return root;
    }
    TreeNode* buildTree(vector<int>& preorder, vector<int>& inorder) {
        return build(preorder, 0, preorder.size() - 1,
                     inorder, 0, inorder.size() - 1);
    }
};

Time: O(n²) in the worst case — each of n nodes may trigger an O(n) scan of inorder. Space: O(h) recursion, O(n) worst case. Optimized. The only slow part is finding the root inside inorder. Precompute a hash map from value to its index in inorder, turning each lookup into O(1). The recursion structure is unchanged.

class Solution {
public:
    unordered_map<int, int> inorderIndexOf;
    int nextPreorderIndex = 0;
    TreeNode* build(vector<int>& preorder, int inStart, int inEnd) {
        if (inStart > inEnd) return nullptr;
        int rootValue = preorder[nextPreorderIndex++];
        TreeNode* root = new TreeNode(rootValue);
        int rootIndexInInorder = inorderIndexOf[rootValue];
        root->left = build(preorder, inStart, rootIndexInInorder - 1);
        root->right = build(preorder, rootIndexInInorder + 1, inEnd);
        return root;
    }
    TreeNode* buildTree(vector<int>& preorder, vector<int>& inorder) {
        for (int index = 0; index < (int)inorder.size(); ++index)
            inorderIndexOf[inorder[index]] = index;
        return build(preorder, 0, inorder.size() - 1);
    }
};

Time: O(n) — one map build plus one O(1) lookup per node. Space: O(n) for the map plus O(h) recursion. Optimized version key point. Because preorder is consumed strictly left to right (root before its entire left subtree before its entire right subtree), a single advancing pointer nextPreorderIndex correctly hands out roots in the right order — process the left subtree fully before the right. Interview Notes.

  • The order of the two recursive calls is mandatory: build the left subtree first, because in preorder the left subtree's nodes come immediately after the root and before any right-subtree node.
  • "All values distinct" is what makes the inorder lookup unambiguous; with duplicates the reconstruction is not unique.
  • Be ready to explain why the inorder split point separates left from right subtrees.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Binary Tree (General)›Problem 73 of 150
73

Construct Binary Tree from Inorder and Postorder Traversal

Medium

Problem. Given inorder (left-node-right) and postorder (left-right-node) traversals with distinct values, reconstruct the tree. Postorder visits the root last. Example. inorder = [9,3,15,20,7], postorder = [9,15,7,20,3] → [3,9,20,null,null,15,7]. The last postorder element 3 is the root; inorder splits into left [9] and right [15,20,7]. Brute Force. Symmetric to problem 72 but reading postorder from the back: the last unused postorder value is the current root, located in inorder by a linear scan. Because the root sits at the end, build the right subtree before the left.

class Solution {
public:
    TreeNode* build(vector<int>& inorder, int inStart, int inEnd,
                    vector<int>& postorder, int postStart, int postEnd) {
        if (inStart > inEnd) return nullptr;
        int rootValue = postorder[postEnd];
        TreeNode* root = new TreeNode(rootValue);
        int rootIndexInInorder = inStart;
        while (inorder[rootIndexInInorder] != rootValue) ++rootIndexInInorder;
        int rightSubtreeSize = inEnd - rootIndexInInorder;
        root->right = build(inorder, rootIndexInInorder + 1, inEnd,
                            postorder, postEnd - rightSubtreeSize, postEnd - 1);
        root->left = build(inorder, inStart, rootIndexInInorder - 1,
                           postorder, postStart, postEnd - rightSubtreeSize - 1);
        return root;
    }
    TreeNode* buildTree(vector<int>& inorder, vector<int>& postorder) {
        return build(inorder, 0, inorder.size() - 1,
                     postorder, 0, postorder.size() - 1);
    }
};

Time: O(n²) worst case from the repeated linear scans. Space: O(h) recursion. Optimized. Use a value-to-index map on inorder for O(1) root location, and consume postorder from the back with a single retreating pointer. Crucially, recurse right before left because the postorder root is followed (reading backwards) by the entire right subtree before the left.

class Solution {
public:
    unordered_map<int, int> inorderIndexOf;
    int nextPostorderIndex;
    TreeNode* build(vector<int>& postorder, int inStart, int inEnd) {
        if (inStart > inEnd) return nullptr;
        int rootValue = postorder[nextPostorderIndex--];
        TreeNode* root = new TreeNode(rootValue);
        int rootIndexInInorder = inorderIndexOf[rootValue];
        root->right = build(postorder, rootIndexInInorder + 1, inEnd);
        root->left = build(postorder, inStart, rootIndexInInorder - 1);
        return root;
    }
    TreeNode* buildTree(vector<int>& inorder, vector<int>& postorder) {
        for (int index = 0; index < (int)inorder.size(); ++index)
            inorderIndexOf[inorder[index]] = index;
        nextPostorderIndex = postorder.size() - 1;
        return build(postorder, 0, inorder.size() - 1);
    }
};

Time: O(n). Space: O(n) for the map plus O(h) recursion. Interview Notes.

  • The right-before-left order is the single thing that distinguishes this from problem 72; swapping it produces a silently wrong tree, not a crash.
  • Reading postorder backwards is the mirror image of reading preorder forwards — the same idea seen from the other end.
  • If asked for preorder + postorder, point out it does not uniquely determine the tree in general (ambiguous for nodes with a single child).
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Binary Tree (General)›Problem 74 of 150
74

Populating Next Right Pointers in Each Node II

Medium

Problem. Each node has an extra next pointer (initially null). Set every node's next to the node immediately to its right on the same level, or null if it is the rightmost. The tree is arbitrary (not necessarily perfect). Node type here is Node with left, right, and next. Example. [1,2,3,4,5,null,7]: node 4's next is 5, node 5's next is 7, node 7's next is null, node 2's next is 3. Brute Force. Do a level-order traversal with a queue; within each level, link each dequeued node to the previous one. Simple and uses the next pointers only as the answer, but consumes O(w) queue memory.

class Node {
public:
    int val;
    Node* left;
    Node* right;
    Node* next;
};
class Solution {
public:
    Node* connect(Node* root) {
        if (root == nullptr) return nullptr;
        queue<Node*> levelQueue;
        levelQueue.push(root);
        while (!levelQueue.empty()) {
            int countThisLevel = levelQueue.size();
            Node* previousOnLevel = nullptr;
            for (int index = 0; index < countThisLevel; ++index) {
                Node* current = levelQueue.front();
                levelQueue.pop();
                if (previousOnLevel) previousOnLevel->next = current;
                previousOnLevel = current;
                if (current->left) levelQueue.push(current->left);
                if (current->right) levelQueue.push(current->right);
            }
        }
        return root;
    }
};

Time: O(n). Space: O(w) for the queue, up to O(n). Optimized. Use the next pointers already built on the current level to walk it like a linked list, while stitching together the children into the next level — achieving O(1) extra space. A dummy head marks the start of each new level, and a moving tail appends children as they appear.

class Solution {
public:
    Node* connect(Node* root) {
        Node* currentLevelNode = root;
        while (currentLevelNode != nullptr) {
            Node nextLevelDummy;
            Node* nextLevelTail = &nextLevelDummy;
            while (currentLevelNode != nullptr) {
                if (currentLevelNode->left) {
                    nextLevelTail->next = currentLevelNode->left;
                    nextLevelTail = nextLevelTail->next;
                }
                if (currentLevelNode->right) {
                    nextLevelTail->next = currentLevelNode->right;
                    nextLevelTail = nextLevelTail->next;
                }
                currentLevelNode = currentLevelNode->next;
            }
            currentLevelNode = nextLevelDummy.next;
        }
        return root;
    }
};

Time: O(n) — each node is touched once as a parent and once when appended. Space: O(1) extra (the dummy is a single stack object); the recursion stack is not used since this is iterative. Interview Notes.

  • The dummy-head trick removes the special case of "is this the first child on the level?" — tail->next = child always works.
  • This is the "II" variant; because the tree is not perfect, you cannot assume left and right both exist, so you must guard each child.
  • Solving it in O(1) space is the whole point of the problem — the BFS solution is correct but considered the "easy" answer.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Binary Tree (General)›Problem 75 of 150
75

Flatten Binary Tree to Linked List

Medium

Problem. Flatten the tree into a "linked list" in place using the same TreeNode type: each node's right points to the next node in preorder, and every left is set to null. Example. [1,2,5,3,4,null,6] → 1 → 2 → 3 → 4 → 5 → 6 along right pointers (left all null). The order is exactly the preorder traversal. Brute Force. Record the preorder sequence into a vector, then relink the nodes: each node's left becomes null and right becomes the next node in the list.

class Solution {
public:
    void preorder(TreeNode* node, vector<TreeNode*>& visitedOrder) {
        if (node == nullptr) return;
        visitedOrder.push_back(node);
        preorder(node->left, visitedOrder);
        preorder(node->right, visitedOrder);
    }
    void flatten(TreeNode* root) {
        vector<TreeNode*> visitedOrder;
        preorder(root, visitedOrder);
        for (int index = 0; index + 1 < (int)visitedOrder.size(); ++index) {
            visitedOrder[index]->left = nullptr;
            visitedOrder[index]->right = visitedOrder[index + 1];
        }
        if (!visitedOrder.empty()) {
            visitedOrder.back()->left = nullptr;
            visitedOrder.back()->right = nullptr;
        }
    }
};

Time: O(n). Space: O(n) for the vector plus O(h) recursion. Optimized. The elegant O(1)-extra-space method (Morris-style) walks down the right spine: for each node that has a left child, find that left subtree's rightmost node, attach the current node's right subtree underneath it, then move the entire left subtree to the right and null the left pointer. Continue to the next right node.

class Solution {
public:
    void flatten(TreeNode* root) {
        TreeNode* current = root;
        while (current != nullptr) {
            if (current->left != nullptr) {
                TreeNode* rightmostOfLeft = current->left;
                while (rightmostOfLeft->right != nullptr)
                    rightmostOfLeft = rightmostOfLeft->right;
                rightmostOfLeft->right = current->right;
                current->right = current->left;
                current->left = nullptr;
            }
            current = current->right;
        }
    }
};

Time: O(n) — although it walks down left subtrees to find predecessors, each edge is traversed a constant number of times. Space: O(1) extra. Interview Notes.

  • This is a Morris-traversal-style rewiring: the predecessor (rightmost of the left subtree) gets the orphaned right subtree grafted on, so nothing is lost.
  • A clean recursive alternative flattens right subtree first, then left, prepending each into a member previousNode pointer — that costs O(h) stack.
  • Make sure the rightmost-search loop uses the updated tree only after attaching; here we search before any rewiring, so it is safe.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Binary Tree (General)›Problem 76 of 150
76

Path Sum

Easy

Problem. Given a tree and a target sum, return true if there is a root-to-leaf path whose node values add up exactly to the target. A leaf is a node with no children. Example. [5,4,8,11,null,13,4,7,2,null,null,null,1], target 22 → true via 5 → 4 → 11 → 2. Brute Force. Enumerate every root-to-leaf path explicitly into a list, then check each path's sum. Correct but stores whole paths needlessly.

class Solution {
public:
    void collectPathSums(TreeNode* node, int runningSum, vector<int>& leafSums) {
        if (node == nullptr) return;
        runningSum += node->val;
        if (node->left == nullptr && node->right == nullptr) {
            leafSums.push_back(runningSum);
            return;
        }
        collectPathSums(node->left, runningSum, leafSums);
        collectPathSums(node->right, runningSum, leafSums);
    }
    bool hasPathSum(TreeNode* root, int targetSum) {
        vector<int> leafSums;
        collectPathSums(root, 0, leafSums);
        for (int sum : leafSums) if (sum == targetSum) return true;
        return false;
    }
};

Time: O(n). Space: O(number of leaves) for the list plus O(h) recursion. Optimized. Carry the remaining target down the tree: subtract the current value, and at a leaf check whether the remainder hit zero. No storage of paths; short-circuit as soon as a match is found.

class Solution {
public:
    bool hasPathSum(TreeNode* root, int targetSum) {
        if (root == nullptr) return false;
        int remaining = targetSum - root->val;
        if (root->left == nullptr && root->right == nullptr)
            return remaining == 0;
        return hasPathSum(root->left, remaining)
            || hasPathSum(root->right, remaining);
    }
};

Time: O(n). Space: O(h) recursion stack. Interview Notes.

  • The leaf test left == nullptr && right == nullptr is essential — you may not stop at an internal node even if the running sum already matches.
  • Returning false for a null root handles the empty tree and prevents a single-child internal node from being mistaken for a path end.
  • Values can be negative, so you cannot prune a branch just because the running sum overshot the target.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Binary Tree (General)›Problem 77 of 150
77

Sum Root to Leaf Numbers

Medium

Problem. Each root-to-leaf path spells a number by concatenating digits (each node holds a digit 0–9). Return the sum of all such numbers. Example. [1,2,3] → paths 1→2 = 12 and 1→3 = 13, sum 25. [4,9,0,5,1] → 495 + 491 + 40 = 1026. Brute Force. Collect each root-to-leaf path as its full integer into a list, then sum the list. Same idea as Path Sum's brute force, building each number by running * 10 + digit.

class Solution {
public:
    void collectNumbers(TreeNode* node, int runningNumber, vector<int>& leafNumbers) {
        if (node == nullptr) return;
        runningNumber = runningNumber * 10 + node->val;
        if (node->left == nullptr && node->right == nullptr) {
            leafNumbers.push_back(runningNumber);
            return;
        }
        collectNumbers(node->left, runningNumber, leafNumbers);
        collectNumbers(node->right, runningNumber, leafNumbers);
    }
    int sumNumbers(TreeNode* root) {
        vector<int> leafNumbers;
        collectNumbers(root, 0, leafNumbers);
        int total = 0;
        for (int number : leafNumbers) total += number;
        return total;
    }
};

Time: O(n). Space: O(number of leaves) plus O(h) recursion. Optimized. Fold the addition into the recursion: pass the number built so far down, and have each call return the sum of all leaf-numbers beneath it. At a leaf, return the completed number; otherwise return the sum from both children.

class Solution {
public:
    int sumFrom(TreeNode* node, int runningNumber) {
        if (node == nullptr) return 0;
        runningNumber = runningNumber * 10 + node->val;
        if (node->left == nullptr && node->right == nullptr)
            return runningNumber;
        return sumFrom(node->left, runningNumber)
             + sumFrom(node->right, runningNumber);
    }
    int sumNumbers(TreeNode* root) {
        return sumFrom(root, 0);
    }
};

Time: O(n). Space: O(h) recursion stack. Interview Notes.

  • The trick runningNumber * 10 + digit is the standard way to grow a number digit by digit, left to right.
  • A null child returns 0, so internal nodes with one child correctly forward only the existing branch's contribution.
  • Constraints guarantee the sum fits in a 32-bit int on this problem; mention you would widen to long if depths were larger.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Binary Tree (General)›Problem 78 of 150
78

Binary Tree Maximum Path Sum

Hard

Problem. A path is any sequence of connected nodes (it need not pass through the root and need not reach a leaf), and each node appears at most once. Return the maximum possible sum of node values along any such path. Example. [-10,9,20,null,null,15,7] → 42, the path 15 → 20 → 7. Note it does not include the root. Brute Force. For every node, treat it as the highest point ("turning point") of a path and compute the best downward path on each side by a separate recursive height-style function; combine left + node + right and track the global best. Recomputing the per-side best for every node from scratch is the inefficiency.

class Solution {
public:
    int bestDownward(TreeNode* node) {
        if (node == nullptr) return 0;
        int leftGain = max(0, bestDownward(node->left));
        int rightGain = max(0, bestDownward(node->right));
        return node->val + max(leftGain, rightGain);
    }
    int maxPathSum(TreeNode* root) {
        if (root == nullptr) return 0;
        int throughThisNode = root->val
            + max(0, bestDownward(root->left))
            + max(0, bestDownward(root->right));
        int answer = throughThisNode;
        if (root->left) answer = max(answer, maxPathSum(root->left));
        if (root->right) answer = max(answer, maxPathSum(root->right));
        return answer;
    }
};

Time: O(n²) — for each node we call bestDownward which itself walks a subtree. Space: O(h) recursion. Optimized. A single postorder pass does both jobs. The helper returns the best downward path starting at a node (so it can be extended by the parent), while a member variable records the best complete path seen anywhere (which may turn at the current node, using both sides). Negative downward gains are clamped to zero — better to drop a branch than to subtract from the sum.

class Solution {
public:
    int bestPathSum = INT_MIN;
    int bestDownward(TreeNode* node) {
        if (node == nullptr) return 0;
        int leftGain = max(0, bestDownward(node->left));
        int rightGain = max(0, bestDownward(node->right));
        int pathTurningHere = node->val + leftGain + rightGain;
        bestPathSum = max(bestPathSum, pathTurningHere);
        return node->val + max(leftGain, rightGain);
    }
    int maxPathSum(TreeNode* root) {
        bestDownward(root);
        return bestPathSum;
    }
};

Time: O(n) — one visit per node. Space: O(h) recursion stack. Interview Notes.

  • The key distinction: a node returns a path that goes down only one side (so the parent can hang it below itself), but updates the answer with a path that may use both sides (which cannot be extended upward).
  • Clamp each child's gain with max(0, ...) so a negative subtree is simply excluded.
  • Initialize the global best to INT_MIN, not 0 — the answer can be negative when all values are negative.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Binary Tree (General)›Problem 79 of 150
79

Binary Search Tree Iterator

Medium

Problem. Implement an iterator over a BST that returns values in ascending order. next() returns the next-smallest value; hasNext() reports whether more remain. Aim for average O(1) per next() and O(h) memory. Example. BST [7,3,15,null,null,9,20]: next()→3, next()→7, hasNext()→true, next()→9, next()→15, hasNext()→true, next()→20, hasNext()→false. Brute Force. In the constructor, do a full inorder traversal into a vector (which is automatically sorted for a BST), then next() just walks an index. Simple, but it uses O(n) memory up front and does all the work before the first next().

class BSTIterator {
    vector<int> sortedValues;
    int position = 0;
    void inorder(TreeNode* node) {
        if (node == nullptr) return;
        inorder(node->left);
        sortedValues.push_back(node->val);
        inorder(node->right);
    }
public:
    BSTIterator(TreeNode* root) { inorder(root); }
    int next() { return sortedValues[position++]; }
    bool hasNext() { return position < (int)sortedValues.size(); }
};

Time: O(n) construction, O(1) per next(). Space: O(n) for the stored values. Optimized. Simulate the inorder traversal lazily with an explicit stack holding only the path of left-descendants. Push all left children initially; on next(), pop a node (the current smallest), then push the left spine of its right child. The stack never exceeds the tree height.

class BSTIterator {
    stack<TreeNode*> leftSpine;
    void pushLeftSpine(TreeNode* node) {
        while (node != nullptr) {
            leftSpine.push(node);
            node = node->left;
        }
    }
public:
    BSTIterator(TreeNode* root) { pushLeftSpine(root); }
    int next() {
        TreeNode* smallest = leftSpine.top();
        leftSpine.pop();
        pushLeftSpine(smallest->right);
        return smallest->val;
    }
    bool hasNext() { return !leftSpine.empty(); }
};

Time: O(1) amortized per next() — each node is pushed and popped exactly once across the whole iteration. Space: O(h) for the stack. Interview Notes.

  • "Amortized O(1)" is the precise claim: a single next() might push a long left spine, but those pushes are paid back over later cheap calls.
  • The O(h) stack is the win over the O(n) precomputed vector; mention this directly when asked about memory.
  • This is just an inorder traversal turned inside-out — recognizing that connection is what the interviewer is testing.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Binary Tree (General)›Problem 80 of 150
80

Count Complete Tree Nodes

Easy

Problem. Count the nodes in a complete binary tree (every level full except possibly the last, which is filled left to right). A plain O(n) count works, but the structure allows faster. Example. [1,2,3,4,5,6] → 6. Levels are [1], [2,3], [4,5,6]; the last is left-filled. Brute Force. Ignore the completeness property and count every node with an ordinary traversal.

class Solution {
public:
    int countNodes(TreeNode* root) {
        if (root == nullptr) return 0;
        return 1 + countNodes(root->left) + countNodes(root->right);
    }
};

Time: O(n). Space: O(h) recursion. Optimized. Exploit completeness. Measure the leftmost depth and rightmost depth: if they are equal, the tree is perfect and has exactly 2^depth - 1 nodes — no traversal needed. Otherwise recurse on both children; only the subtrees straddling the incomplete last level do real work.

class Solution {
public:
    int leftHeight(TreeNode* node) {
        int height = 0;
        while (node != nullptr) { ++height; node = node->left; }
        return height;
    }
    int rightHeight(TreeNode* node) {
        int height = 0;
        while (node != nullptr) { ++height; node = node->right; }
        return height;
    }
    int countNodes(TreeNode* root) {
        if (root == nullptr) return 0;
        int leftmost = leftHeight(root);
        int rightmost = rightHeight(root);
        if (leftmost == rightmost) return (1 << leftmost) - 1;
        return 1 + countNodes(root->left) + countNodes(root->right);
    }
};

Time: O(log² n) — at most O(log n) levels of recursion, each doing an O(log n) height measurement. Space: O(log n) recursion (the tree is complete, so its height is logarithmic). Interview Notes.

  • 1 << depth is 2^depth; for a perfect tree of height h the node count is 2^h - 1.
  • The recursion only branches along the single path where the last level is incomplete; all-perfect subtrees are counted by formula in O(log n).
  • If the tree were not guaranteed complete, this optimization would be invalid and you would fall back to the O(n) count.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Binary Tree (General)›Problem 81 of 150
81

Lowest Common Ancestor of a Binary Tree

Medium

Problem. Given two nodes p and q known to exist in the tree, return their lowest common ancestor: the deepest node that has both as descendants (a node can be a descendant of itself). This is a general binary tree, not a BST. Example. [3,5,1,6,2,0,8,null,null,7,4], p=5, q=1 → 3. With p=5, q=4 → 5, since 5 is an ancestor of 4 and of itself. Brute Force. Find the root-to-node path for p and for q as two lists, then walk both lists from the top in parallel; the last node they share is the LCA. Clear but stores two full paths.

class Solution {
public:
    bool findPath(TreeNode* node, TreeNode* target, vector<TreeNode*>& path) {
        if (node == nullptr) return false;
        path.push_back(node);
        if (node == target) return true;
        if (findPath(node->left, target, path)) return true;
        if (findPath(node->right, target, path)) return true;
        path.pop_back();
        return false;
    }
    TreeNode* lowestCommonAncestor(TreeNode* root, TreeNode* p, TreeNode* q) {
        vector<TreeNode*> pathToP, pathToQ;
        findPath(root, p, pathToP);
        findPath(root, q, pathToQ);
        TreeNode* ancestor = nullptr;
        int index = 0;
        while (index < (int)pathToP.size() && index < (int)pathToQ.size()
               && pathToP[index] == pathToQ[index]) {
            ancestor = pathToP[index];
            ++index;
        }
        return ancestor;
    }
};

Time: O(n). Space: O(h) for the two paths plus recursion. Optimized. A single postorder recursion. Ask each subtree "do you contain p or q?" If one of the targets is found in the left subtree and the other in the right, the current node is the LCA. If the current node is itself p or q, it counts as found too.

class Solution {
public:
    TreeNode* lowestCommonAncestor(TreeNode* root, TreeNode* p, TreeNode* q) {
        if (root == nullptr || root == p || root == q) return root;
        TreeNode* foundInLeft = lowestCommonAncestor(root->left, p, q);
        TreeNode* foundInRight = lowestCommonAncestor(root->right, p, q);
        if (foundInLeft != nullptr && foundInRight != nullptr) return root;
        return foundInLeft != nullptr ? foundInLeft : foundInRight;
    }
};

Time: O(n) — each node visited once. Space: O(h) recursion stack. Interview Notes.

  • The return value does double duty: at the LCA it returns the answer; below the LCA it returns "where I found a target," which bubbles up.
  • The base case root == p || root == q handles the ancestor-of-itself case cleanly — if q is below p, the call at p returns p and the other side returns null, so p propagates up as the answer.
  • This works because both nodes are guaranteed present; if they might be absent, you would add a count to confirm both were actually seen before returning.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Binary Tree (BFS)›Problem 82 of 150
82

Binary Tree Right Side View

Medium

Problem. Imagine standing to the right of a binary tree and looking left. Return the values of the nodes you can see, ordered from top to bottom. For each depth level, that visible node is the rightmost node on that level. The tree can be empty (return an empty list).

Example. Tree: root 1 with left child 2 and right child 3; node 2 has a right child 5; node 3 has a right child 4.

    1
   / \
  2   3
   \   \
    5   4

Level 0 rightmost is 1, level 1 rightmost is 3, level 2 rightmost is 4. Output: [1, 3, 4].

Brute Force. Do a depth-first traversal while tracking the current depth. For each level keep the latest value seen, but force the traversal to visit the left subtree before the right subtree so that the right side overwrites the left — the last value written for each depth ends up being the rightmost. Store one entry per depth in a map keyed by depth, then read it out in order.

class Solution {
public:
    void traverse(TreeNode* node, int depth, std::map<int, int>& rightmostByDepth) {
        if (node == nullptr) {
            return;
        }
        rightmostByDepth[depth] = node->val;
        traverse(node->left, depth + 1, rightmostByDepth);
        traverse(node->right, depth + 1, rightmostByDepth);
    }

    std::vector<int> rightSideView(TreeNode* root) {
        std::map<int, int> rightmostByDepth;
        traverse(root, 0, rightmostByDepth);
        std::vector<int> result;
        for (const auto& depthValuePair : rightmostByDepth) {
            result.push_back(depthValuePair.second);
        }
        return result;
    }
};

Time: O(n log n), Space: O(n). Justification: every one of the n nodes is visited once, but each std::map write costs O(log n) because the map is a balanced tree, giving O(n log n). The map plus recursion stack hold up to O(n) entries.

Optimized. Run an ordinary BFS level by level. At each level the last node you pop (the one at index levelSize - 1) is the rightmost, so append its value to the answer. No depth bookkeeping or map is needed — the level-size loop gives you the boundary for free.

class Solution {
public:
    std::vector<int> rightSideView(TreeNode* root) {
        std::vector<int> result;
        if (root == nullptr) {
            return result;
        }
        std::queue<TreeNode*> nodeQueue;
        nodeQueue.push(root);
        while (!nodeQueue.empty()) {
            int levelSize = nodeQueue.size();
            for (int positionInLevel = 0; positionInLevel < levelSize; ++positionInLevel) {
                TreeNode* currentNode = nodeQueue.front();
                nodeQueue.pop();
                if (positionInLevel == levelSize - 1) {
                    result.push_back(currentNode->val);
                }
                if (currentNode->left != nullptr) {
                    nodeQueue.push(currentNode->left);
                }
                if (currentNode->right != nullptr) {
                    nodeQueue.push(currentNode->right);
                }
            }
        }
        return result;
    }
};

Time: O(n), Space: O(n). Justification: each node is enqueued and dequeued exactly once with O(1) work, so O(n) total. The queue holds at most one full level, which can be up to O(n) wide for a near-complete tree.

Interview Notes.

  • The rightmost node is not always a right child — node 5 could be visible if 3 had no right child, so don't shortcut by only following right pointers.
  • Push children left-then-right so the queue stays in left-to-right order within each level; the rightmost is then simply the last popped.
  • A DFS variant that visits right-before-left and records the first value seen per depth also works in O(n); mention it if asked for the recursive form.
  • Empty tree must return [], not crash — guard the null root.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Binary Tree (BFS)›Problem 83 of 150
83

Average of Levels in Binary Tree

Easy

Problem. Given the root of a binary tree, return a list where the i-th element is the average value of all nodes at depth i. The tree is non-empty. Because values can sum large, accumulate the per-level sum in a wide integer type to avoid overflow, then divide as a double.

Example. Tree: root 3, left child 9, right child 20; node 20 has children 15 and 7.

    3
   / \
  9   20
     /  \
    15   7

Level 0 average is 3, level 1 average is (9 + 20) / 2 = 14.5, level 2 average is (15 + 7) / 2 = 11. Output: [3.0, 14.5, 11.0].

Brute Force. DFS while tracking depth, building two parallel maps: one summing the values at each depth and one counting how many nodes that depth has. After the traversal, walk the depths in order and divide sum by count.

class Solution {
public:
    void traverse(TreeNode* node, int depth,
                  std::map<int, long long>& sumByDepth,
                  std::map<int, int>& countByDepth) {
        if (node == nullptr) {
            return;
        }
        sumByDepth[depth] += node->val;
        countByDepth[depth] += 1;
        traverse(node->left, depth + 1, sumByDepth, countByDepth);
        traverse(node->right, depth + 1, sumByDepth, countByDepth);
    }

    std::vector<double> averageOfLevels(TreeNode* root) {
        std::map<int, long long> sumByDepth;
        std::map<int, int> countByDepth;
        traverse(root, 0, sumByDepth, countByDepth);
        std::vector<double> result;
        for (const auto& depthSumPair : sumByDepth) {
            int depth = depthSumPair.first;
            result.push_back(static_cast<double>(depthSumPair.second) / countByDepth[depth]);
        }
        return result;
    }
};

Time: O(n log n), Space: O(n). Justification: n node visits, each doing O(log n) map operations. The two maps plus recursion stack are O(n).

Optimized. BFS with the level-size loop. For each level, sum the values of exactly levelSize popped nodes, then push sum / levelSize as a double. The level size is known up front, so no counting map is needed.

class Solution {
public:
    std::vector<double> averageOfLevels(TreeNode* root) {
        std::vector<double> result;
        if (root == nullptr) {
            return result;
        }
        std::queue<TreeNode*> nodeQueue;
        nodeQueue.push(root);
        while (!nodeQueue.empty()) {
            int levelSize = nodeQueue.size();
            long long levelSum = 0;
            for (int positionInLevel = 0; positionInLevel < levelSize; ++positionInLevel) {
                TreeNode* currentNode = nodeQueue.front();
                nodeQueue.pop();
                levelSum += currentNode->val;
                if (currentNode->left != nullptr) {
                    nodeQueue.push(currentNode->left);
                }
                if (currentNode->right != nullptr) {
                    nodeQueue.push(currentNode->right);
                }
            }
            result.push_back(static_cast<double>(levelSum) / levelSize);
        }
        return result;
    }
};

Time: O(n), Space: O(n). Justification: one enqueue and one dequeue per node with O(1) work each. The queue holds at most a full level, up to O(n).

Interview Notes.

  • Use long long for levelSum: a level full of values near INT_MAX overflows a plain int before you divide.
  • Cast to double before dividing, otherwise integer division throws away the fractional part (e.g. 29 / 2 would give 14, not 14.5).
  • This is the cleanest "BFS template" problem — if you can write this, the rest of the section is the same loop with a different per-level action.
  • levelSize is guaranteed non-zero inside the loop, so the division is always safe.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Binary Tree (BFS)›Problem 84 of 150
84

Binary Tree Level Order Traversal

Medium

Problem. Return the node values grouped by level: a list of lists, where the i-th inner list holds every value at depth i, in left-to-right order. Empty tree returns an empty outer list.

Example. Tree: root 3, left 9, right 20; node 20 has children 15 and 7.

    3
   / \
  9   20
     /  \
    15   7

Output: [[3], [9, 20], [15, 7]].

Brute Force. DFS with depth tracking. Maintain a vector of vectors; whenever the current depth equals the current number of inner lists, append a fresh empty inner list, then push the node's value into the inner list at index depth. Visiting left before right keeps each inner list in left-to-right order.

class Solution {
public:
    void traverse(TreeNode* node, int depth, std::vector<std::vector<int>>& levels) {
        if (node == nullptr) {
            return;
        }
        if (depth == static_cast<int>(levels.size())) {
            levels.push_back(std::vector<int>());
        }
        levels[depth].push_back(node->val);
        traverse(node->left, depth + 1, levels);
        traverse(node->right, depth + 1, levels);
    }

    std::vector<std::vector<int>> levelOrder(TreeNode* root) {
        std::vector<std::vector<int>> levels;
        traverse(root, 0, levels);
        return levels;
    }
};

Time: O(n), Space: O(n). Justification: each node is visited once and appended in amortized O(1). The output stores all n values and the recursion stack is O(h) where h is tree height, both within O(n).

Optimized. BFS with the level-size loop. For each level, create one fresh inner vector, fill it with the levelSize popped values, then attach it to the result. This produces the grouping directly without any depth comparison.

class Solution {
public:
    std::vector<std::vector<int>> levelOrder(TreeNode* root) {
        std::vector<std::vector<int>> result;
        if (root == nullptr) {
            return result;
        }
        std::queue<TreeNode*> nodeQueue;
        nodeQueue.push(root);
        while (!nodeQueue.empty()) {
            int levelSize = nodeQueue.size();
            std::vector<int> currentLevelValues;
            for (int positionInLevel = 0; positionInLevel < levelSize; ++positionInLevel) {
                TreeNode* currentNode = nodeQueue.front();
                nodeQueue.pop();
                currentLevelValues.push_back(currentNode->val);
                if (currentNode->left != nullptr) {
