            return;
        }
        for (int candidate : nums) {
            if (find(path.begin(), path.end(), candidate) != path.end())
                continue;
            path.push_back(candidate);
            backtrack(nums, path, results);
            path.pop_back();
        }
    }
};

Time: O(n! · n²), Space: O(n). There are n! leaves, each path costs O(n) to copy, and the per-candidate linear find adds the extra O(n) factor.

Optimized. Replace the linear membership test with a boolean used array (O(1) lookup), or swap elements in place to avoid the auxiliary structure entirely. The used version below is the most readable and the one interviewers usually want.

class Solution {
public:
    vector<vector<int>> permute(vector<int>& nums) {
        vector<vector<int>> results;
        vector<int> path;
        vector<bool> used(nums.size(), false);
        backtrack(nums, used, path, results);
        return results;
    }
private:
    void backtrack(vector<int>& nums, vector<bool>& used, vector<int>& path,
                   vector<vector<int>>& results) {
        if (path.size() == nums.size()) {
            results.push_back(path);
            return;
        }
        for (int index = 0; index < (int)nums.size(); ++index) {
            if (used[index]) continue;
            used[index] = true;
            path.push_back(nums[index]);
            backtrack(nums, used, path, results);
            path.pop_back();
            used[index] = false;
        }
    }
};

Time: O(n! · n), Space: O(n). Still n! leaves with O(n) copy each, but the used array makes the availability check O(1); recursion depth and the bookkeeping arrays are O(n).

Interview Notes.

  • Note the contrast with Combinations: permutations have no start index because order matters and every unused element is a valid next choice.
  • Follow-up "Permutations II" (input contains duplicates): sort first, then skip nums[index] when it equals the previous value and the previous value is unused — the standard duplicate-suppression idiom.
  • Mention the in-place swap variant as an O(1)-extra-space alternative; some interviewers probe for it.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Backtracking›Problem 104 of 150
104

Combination Sum

Medium

Problem. Given an array of distinct positive integers candidates and a target, return all unique combinations whose elements sum to target. Each candidate may be reused an unlimited number of times. Combinations are unordered, so [2,2,3] and [2,3,2] count once. Example. Input candidates = [2,3,6,7], target = 7 → [[2,2,3],[7]]. Trace: from 2 you can take another 2 (sum 4), another 2 (sum 6) is dead-ended by 3 overshooting, but 3 lands exactly on 7; separately 7 alone hits target. Brute Force. Try every value at every level with no ordering discipline, allowing reuse, and dedupe at the end by sorting each found combination and inserting into a set. This explodes the search tree because [2,3] and [3,2] are both explored before being merged.

class Solution {
public:
    vector<vector<int>> combinationSum(vector<int>& candidates, int target) {
        set<vector<int>> uniqueResults;
        vector<int> path;
        search(candidates, target, path, uniqueResults);
        vector<vector<int>> results(uniqueResults.begin(), uniqueResults.end());
        return results;
    }
private:
    void search(vector<int>& candidates, int remaining, vector<int>& path,
                set<vector<int>>& uniqueResults) {
        if (remaining == 0) {
            vector<int> sorted = path;
            sort(sorted.begin(), sorted.end());
            uniqueResults.insert(sorted);
            return;
        }
        if (remaining < 0) return;
        for (int candidate : candidates) {
            path.push_back(candidate);
            search(candidates, remaining - candidate, path, uniqueResults);
            path.pop_back();
        }
    }
};

Time: exponential, roughly O(branches^depth · k log(results)) with the set overhead, Space: O(target/min · results). Exploring every ordering and deduping afterward is wasteful both in time and in the sort-per-leaf cost.

Optimized. Use a start index so each recursion only considers candidates at or after the current one — this permits reuse (we recurse with the same index, not index+1) while preventing the [2,3]/[3,2] duplication. Prune by passing remaining = target - chosen: stop a branch as soon as remaining < 0. Sorting candidates first lets you break out of the loop the moment a candidate exceeds remaining.

class Solution {
public:
    vector<vector<int>> combinationSum(vector<int>& candidates, int target) {
        sort(candidates.begin(), candidates.end());
        vector<vector<int>> results;
        vector<int> path;
        backtrack(candidates, target, 0, path, results);
        return results;
    }
private:
    void backtrack(vector<int>& candidates, int remaining, int start,
                   vector<int>& path, vector<vector<int>>& results) {
        if (remaining == 0) {
            results.push_back(path);
            return;
        }
        for (int index = start; index < (int)candidates.size(); ++index) {
            if (candidates[index] > remaining) break;
            path.push_back(candidates[index]);
            backtrack(candidates, remaining - candidates[index], index, path, results);
            path.pop_back();
        }
    }
};

Time: O(N^(target/min) ) in the worst case, where N is candidate count and min the smallest candidate (the tree depth is bounded by target/min), Space: O(target/min) recursion depth. The break after sorting and the remaining prune cut the explored tree dramatically.

Interview Notes.

  • The key trick for "reuse allowed" is recursing with index (not index + 1); for "each used once" (Combination Sum II) you'd recurse with index + 1.
  • Combination Sum II adds duplicate candidates: sort and skip candidates[index] == candidates[index-1] when index > start to avoid duplicate combinations.
  • Sorting + early break is the prune interviewers look for; state it explicitly.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Backtracking›Problem 105 of 150
105

N-Queens II

Hard

Problem. Count the number of distinct ways to place n queens on an n × n board so that no two attack each other (no shared row, column, or diagonal). Unlike the full N-Queens, you return only the count, not the boards. Example. Input n = 4 → 2. Trace: only two valid configurations exist for a 4×4 board; both are 180°-rotations/reflections of each other but counted separately as required. Brute Force. Place one queen per row, and at each cell run a full O(n) scan against all previously placed queens to test column and both diagonals before committing. Correct, but the conflict check is recomputed from scratch every time.

class Solution {
public:
    int totalNQueens(int n) {
        vector<int> queenColumn(n, -1);
        return place(0, n, queenColumn);
    }
private:
    bool isSafe(const vector<int>& queenColumn, int row, int column) {
        for (int previousRow = 0; previousRow < row; ++previousRow) {
            int previousColumn = queenColumn[previousRow];
            if (previousColumn == column) return false;
            if (abs(previousColumn - column) == abs(previousRow - row))
                return false;
        }
        return true;
    }
    int place(int row, int n, vector<int>& queenColumn) {
        if (row == n) return 1;
        int count = 0;
        for (int column = 0; column < n; ++column) {
            if (isSafe(queenColumn, row, column)) {
                queenColumn[row] = column;
                count += place(row + 1, n, queenColumn);
                queenColumn[row] = -1;
            }
        }
        return count;
    }
};

Time: O(n!) leaves but with an O(n) safety scan per placement, effectively O(n! · n), Space: O(n). Placing row by row already prunes the row constraint, but rescanning all prior queens is redundant work.

Optimized. Track occupancy with three boolean (or bitmask) sets: one for columns, one for the / diagonals (indexed by row + column), one for the \ diagonals (indexed by row - column + n - 1). Each conflict check becomes O(1). This is the classic constant-time-pruning version.

class Solution {
public:
    int totalNQueens(int n) {
        vector<bool> columnUsed(n, false);
        vector<bool> diagonalUsed(2 * n - 1, false);
        vector<bool> antiDiagonalUsed(2 * n - 1, false);
        return place(0, n, columnUsed, diagonalUsed, antiDiagonalUsed);
    }
private:
    int place(int row, int n, vector<bool>& columnUsed,
              vector<bool>& diagonalUsed, vector<bool>& antiDiagonalUsed) {
        if (row == n) return 1;
        int count = 0;
        for (int column = 0; column < n; ++column) {
            int diagonal = row - column + n - 1;
            int antiDiagonal = row + column;
            if (columnUsed[column] || diagonalUsed[diagonal] ||
                antiDiagonalUsed[antiDiagonal])
                continue;
            columnUsed[column] = true;
            diagonalUsed[diagonal] = true;
            antiDiagonalUsed[antiDiagonal] = true;
            count += place(row + 1, n, columnUsed, diagonalUsed, antiDiagonalUsed);
            columnUsed[column] = false;
            diagonalUsed[diagonal] = false;
            antiDiagonalUsed[antiDiagonal] = false;
        }
        return count;
    }
};

Time: O(n!), Space: O(n). The branching shrinks at each row as occupancy fills up, and the three marker arrays make every conflict test O(1) instead of O(n).

Interview Notes.

  • The diagonal index formulas are the crux: cells on the same \ diagonal share row - column; cells on the same / diagonal share row + column. Shift by n - 1 to keep indices non-negative.
  • Top-tier optimization: replace the boolean vectors with integer bitmasks and use bit tricks (available & -available to grab the lowest free column) — this is the famous one-liner-core N-Queens that impresses interviewers.
  • Follow-up: the full N-Queens (return the actual boards) reuses this skeleton but records a queenColumn array and renders strings at the leaf.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Backtracking›Problem 106 of 150
106

Generate Parentheses

Medium

Problem. Given n pairs of parentheses, generate all combinations of well-formed (balanced) parenthesis strings using exactly n opening and n closing brackets. Example. Input n = 3 → ["((()))","(()())","(())()","()(())","()()()"]. Trace: you may open while opens used < n, and close only while closes used < opens so far, guaranteeing every prefix stays valid. Brute Force. Generate all 2^(2n) strings over {'(', ')'} of length 2n, then keep the balanced ones with a validity check (running counter that never goes negative and ends at zero). Most generated strings are discarded.

class Solution {
public:
    vector<string> generateParenthesis(int n) {
        vector<string> results;
        string current;
        generateAll(current, 2 * n, results);
        return results;
    }
private:
    bool isValid(const string& candidate) {
        int balance = 0;
        for (char bracket : candidate) {
            balance += (bracket == '(') ? 1 : -1;
            if (balance < 0) return false;
        }
        return balance == 0;
    }
    void generateAll(string& current, int totalLength,
                     vector<string>& results) {
        if ((int)current.size() == totalLength) {
            if (isValid(current)) results.push_back(current);
            return;
        }
        current.push_back('(');
        generateAll(current, totalLength, results);
        current.pop_back();
        current.push_back(')');
        generateAll(current, totalLength, results);
        current.pop_back();
    }
};

Time: O(2^(2n) · n), Space: O(n) recursion depth plus output. Every one of the 2^(2n) raw strings is built and validated in O(n), even though only the Catalan-number-many are kept.

Optimized. Prune as you build: never add '(' once you've used n of them, and never add ')' unless the number of closes placed is strictly less than the number of opens placed. These two invariants mean every string you ever construct is already valid, so no post-check is needed.

class Solution {
public:
    vector<string> generateParenthesis(int n) {
        vector<string> results;
        string current;
        backtrack(current, 0, 0, n, results);
        return results;
    }
private:
    void backtrack(string& current, int opensUsed, int closesUsed, int n,
                   vector<string>& results) {
        if ((int)current.size() == 2 * n) {
            results.push_back(current);
            return;
        }
        if (opensUsed < n) {
            current.push_back('(');
            backtrack(current, opensUsed + 1, closesUsed, n, results);
            current.pop_back();
        }
        if (closesUsed < opensUsed) {
            current.push_back(')');
            backtrack(current, opensUsed, closesUsed + 1, n, results);
            current.pop_back();
        }
    }
};

Time: O(4^n / √n · n) ≈ O(Catalan(n) · n), Space: O(n) recursion depth. The number of valid strings is the n-th Catalan number; pruning means the tree visits essentially only nodes that lead to valid leaves.

Interview Notes.

  • The two prune conditions (opensUsed < n to open, closesUsed < opensUsed to close) are the entire problem — be able to justify each in one sentence.
  • The result count being the Catalan number is a classic follow-up; knowing C(n) ≈ 4^n / (n^1.5 √π) signals depth.
  • Follow-up: produce them iteratively, or count valid strings without generating them (a DP over (opens, closes) states).
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Backtracking›Problem 107 of 150
107

Word Search

Medium

Problem. Given an m × n grid of characters and a word, determine whether the word can be formed by a path of adjacent (horizontally or vertically neighboring) cells, where each cell is used at most once per search. Example. Input board = [["A","B","C","E"],["S","F","C","S"],["A","D","E","E"]], word = "ABCCED" → true. Trace: start at A(0,0) → B(0,1) → C(0,2) → C(1,2) → E(2,2) → D(2,1), a connected non-repeating path. Brute Force. Try every cell as a start, and from each explore all four directions recursively, but track visited cells with a separate visited boolean grid and allocate/copy it as you go. Functionally correct; the extra structure and any copying inflate memory.

class Solution {
public:
    bool exist(vector<vector<char>>& board, string word) {
        int rows = board.size(), columns = board[0].size();
        vector<vector<bool>> visited(rows, vector<bool>(columns, false));
        for (int row = 0; row < rows; ++row)
            for (int column = 0; column < columns; ++column)
                if (search(board, word, 0, row, column, visited)) return true;
        return false;
    }
private:
    bool search(vector<vector<char>>& board, const string& word, int matched,
                int row, int column, vector<vector<bool>>& visited) {
        if (matched == (int)word.size()) return true;
        if (row < 0 || row >= (int)board.size() ||
            column < 0 || column >= (int)board[0].size()) return false;
        if (visited[row][column] || board[row][column] != word[matched])
            return false;
        visited[row][column] = true;
        bool found = search(board, word, matched + 1, row + 1, column, visited) ||
                     search(board, word, matched + 1, row - 1, column, visited) ||
                     search(board, word, matched + 1, row, column + 1, visited) ||
                     search(board, word, matched + 1, row, column - 1, visited);
        visited[row][column] = false;
        return found;
    }
};

Time: O(m · n · 4^L) where L is the word length, Space: O(m · n + L). Every cell can start a search, and each step branches up to 4 ways for L levels; the separate visited grid adds O(m·n) memory.

Optimized. Avoid the auxiliary visited grid by marking the current cell in place — temporarily overwrite board[row][column] with a sentinel like '#', recurse, then restore the original character on the way out. This is the same choose/explore/un-choose pattern applied directly to the board and saves the O(m·n) memory.

class Solution {
public:
    bool exist(vector<vector<char>>& board, string word) {
        int rows = board.size(), columns = board[0].size();
        for (int row = 0; row < rows; ++row)
            for (int column = 0; column < columns; ++column)
                if (search(board, word, 0, row, column)) return true;
        return false;
    }
private:
    bool search(vector<vector<char>>& board, const string& word, int matched,
                int row, int column) {
        if (matched == (int)word.size()) return true;
        if (row < 0 || row >= (int)board.size() ||
            column < 0 || column >= (int)board[0].size()) return false;
        if (board[row][column] != word[matched]) return false;
        char original = board[row][column];
        board[row][column] = '#';
        bool found = search(board, word, matched + 1, row + 1, column) ||
                     search(board, word, matched + 1, row - 1, column) ||
                     search(board, word, matched + 1, row, column + 1) ||
                     search(board, word, matched + 1, row, column - 1);
        board[row][column] = original;
        return found;
    }
};

Time: O(m · n · 4^L), Space: O(L) recursion depth only. The in-place sentinel removes the O(m·n) visited grid; the time bound is unchanged because the search tree is the same shape.

Interview Notes.

  • The in-place marking trick ('#' then restore) is the expected optimization — mention it even if you write the visited version first.
  • Order the base-case checks carefully: test bounds before indexing the board, and the character-mismatch check naturally prunes most branches early.
  • Follow-up "Word Search II" (find many words at once) is a classic Hard upgrade: build a Trie of all words and DFS the grid once against the Trie instead of running this search per word.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Divide & Conquer›Problem 108 of 150
108

Convert Sorted Array to Binary Search Tree

Easy

Problem. Given an integer array sorted in ascending order, build a height-balanced binary search tree (BST) from it. Height-balanced means the depths of the two subtrees of every node differ by at most one. Constraints: the array is strictly sorted and may be empty; any valid balanced BST is accepted.

Example. Input [-10, -3, 0, 5, 9] → one valid output is a tree rooted at 0, with left subtree built from [-10, -3] and right subtree from [5, 9]. Trace: middle index 2 holds 0, so 0 becomes the root, then recurse on [-10, -3] and [5, 9].

Brute Force. A naive approach inserts elements one by one into an initially empty BST using ordinary BST insertion. Because the input is already sorted, inserting in order produces a degenerate tree that looks like a linked list (each node hangs off the right of the previous), which violates the balance requirement. To force balance you would need to insert in a deliberately scrambled order, but figuring out that order is essentially the divide-and-conquer solution anyway. The simplest "just make it work but unbalanced" version:

class Solution {
public:
    TreeNode* sortedArrayToBST(vector<int>& sortedNumbers) {
        TreeNode* root = nullptr;
        for (int currentValue : sortedNumbers) {
            root = insertIntoBST(root, currentValue);
        }
        return root;
    }
private:
    TreeNode* insertIntoBST(TreeNode* node, int value) {
        if (node == nullptr) return new TreeNode(value);
        if (value < node->val) node->left = insertIntoBST(node->left, value);
        else node->right = insertIntoBST(node->right, value);
        return node;
    }
};

Time: O(n^2), Space: O(n). Justification: inserting into a degenerate tree of height up to n costs O(n) per element across n elements, giving O(n^2); the tree itself uses O(n) space and the recursion stack can also reach O(n). This also fails the balance constraint, so it is only illustrative.

Optimized. Key insight: the middle element of any sorted range is the perfect root because exactly half the values are smaller and half are larger, guaranteeing balance. Recurse on the left half to build the left subtree and the right half to build the right subtree. Pass index bounds instead of copying subarrays to avoid extra allocation.

class Solution {
public:
    TreeNode* sortedArrayToBST(vector<int>& sortedNumbers) {
        return buildBalanced(sortedNumbers, 0, (int)sortedNumbers.size() - 1);
    }
private:
    TreeNode* buildBalanced(vector<int>& sortedNumbers, int leftIndex, int rightIndex) {
        if (leftIndex > rightIndex) return nullptr;
        int middleIndex = leftIndex + (rightIndex - leftIndex) / 2;
        TreeNode* root = new TreeNode(sortedNumbers[middleIndex]);
        root->left = buildBalanced(sortedNumbers, leftIndex, middleIndex - 1);
        root->right = buildBalanced(sortedNumbers, middleIndex + 1, rightIndex);
        return root;
    }
};

Time: O(n), Space: O(log n). Justification: each element becomes exactly one node and is touched once, so the total work is O(n); the recursion stack depth equals the tree height, which is O(log n) for a balanced tree (ignoring the O(n) output nodes themselves).

Interview Notes.

  • Always pick the middle by index, not by copying subarrays — slicing turns it into O(n log n) time and O(n log n) space from repeated allocations.
  • When the range has an even length there are two valid middles; either works, so prefer the lower-middle formula leftIndex + (rightIndex - leftIndex) / 2 to avoid integer overflow.
  • FAANG follow-up: "What if the input is a sorted linked list instead of an array?" (LeetCode 109's sibling) — you cannot index in O(1), so use slow/fast pointers to find the middle, or convert to an array first.
  • Follow-up: "Prove the tree is balanced." The two halves differ in size by at most one at every level, so subtree heights differ by at most one.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Divide & Conquer›Problem 109 of 150
109

Sort List

Medium

Problem. Given the head of a singly linked list, sort the nodes in ascending order and return the new head. Constraints: the list may have 0 to 5×10^4 nodes; the interviewer typically wants O(n log n) time and, ideally, O(1) extra space beyond recursion.

Example. Input 4 -> 2 -> 1 -> 3 → output 1 -> 2 -> 3 -> 4. Trace: split into 4 -> 2 and 1 -> 3, sort each to 2 -> 4 and 1 -> 3, then merge to 1 -> 2 -> 3 -> 4.

Brute Force. Walk the list once to copy every value into a vector, sort the vector with the standard library, then walk the list again writing the sorted values back into the existing nodes. Simple and reliable, but it allocates an array proportional to the list length.

class Solution {
public:
    ListNode* sortList(ListNode* head) {
        vector<int> values;
        for (ListNode* current = head; current != nullptr; current = current->next) {
            values.push_back(current->val);
        }
        sort(values.begin(), values.end());
        int writeIndex = 0;
        for (ListNode* current = head; current != nullptr; current = current->next) {
            current->val = values[writeIndex++];
        }
        return head;
    }
};

Time: O(n log n), Space: O(n). Justification: sorting the vector is O(n log n); the two list traversals are O(n); the extra vector holding every value is O(n) space.

Optimized. Key insight: merge sort is the natural fit for linked lists because merging two sorted lists needs only pointer rewiring, not random access. Find the middle with slow/fast pointers, cut the list into two halves, recursively sort each half, then merge. This keeps O(n log n) time without copying values into an array.

class Solution {
public:
    ListNode* sortList(ListNode* head) {
        if (head == nullptr || head->next == nullptr) return head;
        ListNode* middle = splitInHalf(head);
        ListNode* leftSorted = sortList(head);
        ListNode* rightSorted = sortList(middle);
        return mergeTwoSorted(leftSorted, rightSorted);
    }
private:
    ListNode* splitInHalf(ListNode* head) {
        ListNode* slow = head;
        ListNode* fast = head->next;
        while (fast != nullptr && fast->next != nullptr) {
            slow = slow->next;
            fast = fast->next->next;
        }
        ListNode* secondHalf = slow->next;
        slow->next = nullptr;
        return secondHalf;
    }
    ListNode* mergeTwoSorted(ListNode* firstList, ListNode* secondList) {
        ListNode placeholder(0);
        ListNode* tail = &placeholder;
        while (firstList != nullptr && secondList != nullptr) {
            if (firstList->val <= secondList->val) {
                tail->next = firstList;
                firstList = firstList->next;
            } else {
                tail->next = secondList;
                secondList = secondList->next;
            }
            tail = tail->next;
        }
        tail->next = (firstList != nullptr) ? firstList : secondList;
        return placeholder.next;
    }
};

Time: O(n log n), Space: O(log n). Justification: merge sort makes log n levels of splitting, and each level does O(n) total work merging, giving O(n log n); the only extra space is the recursion stack of depth O(log n) — no value-copying array.

Interview Notes.

  • Starting fast at head->next (not head) ensures the first half is never larger than the second, which prevents infinite recursion on two-node lists.
  • Cutting the list (slow->next = nullptr) is essential — forget it and the two halves stay connected, corrupting the sort.
  • FAANG follow-up: "Why merge sort over quicksort for lists?" Quicksort needs random access for good pivots and degrades; merge sort merges via pointers cleanly and is stable.
  • Follow-up: "Make it truly O(1) space." Use bottom-up iterative merge sort, which removes the recursion stack entirely.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Divide & Conquer›Problem 110 of 150
110

Construct Quad Tree

Medium

Problem. Given an n×n grid of 0s and 1s (n is a power of two), build a quad tree. Each node has a boolean val and a boolean isLeaf. If a square region is all the same value, it becomes a leaf holding that value. Otherwise it is an internal node split into four equal quadrants — topLeft, topRight, bottomLeft, bottomRight — each built recursively.

Example. Input [[1,1],[1,1]] (all ones) → a single leaf node with isLeaf = true, val = 1. For [[0,1],[1,0]] the four cells differ, so the root is internal (isLeaf = false) with four leaf children holding 0, 1, 1, 0.

Brute Force. For every region, scan all of its cells to check whether they are uniform before deciding to make a leaf or recurse. The check itself is the same as the recursion; the "brute" flavor here is re-scanning the entire region at every level rather than reusing information from children. It still produces the correct tree.

class Solution {
public:
    Node* construct(vector<vector<int>>& grid) {
        return build(grid, 0, 0, (int)grid.size());
    }
private:
    Node* build(vector<vector<int>>& grid, int rowStart, int colStart, int sideLength) {
        if (isUniform(grid, rowStart, colStart, sideLength)) {
            return new Node(grid[rowStart][colStart] == 1, true);
        }
        int half = sideLength / 2;
        Node* result = new Node(true, false);
        result->topLeft = build(grid, rowStart, colStart, half);
        result->topRight = build(grid, rowStart, colStart + half, half);
        result->bottomLeft = build(grid, rowStart + half, colStart, half);
        result->bottomRight = build(grid, rowStart + half, colStart + half, half);
        return result;
    }
    bool isUniform(vector<vector<int>>& grid, int rowStart, int colStart, int sideLength) {
        int firstValue = grid[rowStart][colStart];
        for (int row = rowStart; row < rowStart + sideLength; row++) {
            for (int col = colStart; col < colStart + sideLength; col++) {
                if (grid[row][col] != firstValue) return false;
            }
        }
        return true;
    }
};

Time: O(n^2 log n), Space: O(log n). Justification: at each of the log n levels the uniformity scans cover the whole grid once (O(n^2)), so total scanning is O(n^2 log n); the recursion stack is O(log n) deep (excluding the output tree).

Optimized. Key insight: instead of scanning each region top-down, recurse to the four quadrants first, then combine. If all four children are leaves holding the same value, collapse them into one leaf; otherwise keep them as four children of an internal node. This is true divide and conquer — the combine step reuses the children's answers rather than re-scanning.

class Solution {
public:
    Node* construct(vector<vector<int>>& grid) {
        return build(grid, 0, 0, (int)grid.size());
    }
private:
    Node* build(vector<vector<int>>& grid, int rowStart, int colStart, int sideLength) {
        if (sideLength == 1) {
            return new Node(grid[rowStart][colStart] == 1, true);
        }
        int half = sideLength / 2;
        Node* topLeft = build(grid, rowStart, colStart, half);
        Node* topRight = build(grid, rowStart, colStart + half, half);
        Node* bottomLeft = build(grid, rowStart + half, colStart, half);
        Node* bottomRight = build(grid, rowStart + half, colStart + half, half);
        bool allLeaves = topLeft->isLeaf && topRight->isLeaf
                         && bottomLeft->isLeaf && bottomRight->isLeaf;
        bool sameValue = (topLeft->val == topRight->val)
                         && (topRight->val == bottomLeft->val)
                         && (bottomLeft->val == bottomRight->val);
        if (allLeaves && sameValue) {
            bool collapsedValue = topLeft->val;
            delete topLeft;
            delete topRight;
            delete bottomLeft;
            delete bottomRight;
            return new Node(collapsedValue, true);
        }
        Node* result = new Node(true, false);
        result->topLeft = topLeft;
        result->topRight = topRight;
        result->bottomLeft = bottomLeft;
        result->bottomRight = bottomRight;
        return result;
    }
};

Time: O(n^2), Space: O(log n). Justification: each of the n^2 cells is visited exactly once at the leaf level and the combine work per node is constant, so the total is O(n^2); the recursion stack depth is O(log n).

Interview Notes.

  • The optimized version is faster because each cell is read once at the base case, whereas the brute force re-reads regions at every level.
  • The base case can be sideLength == 1 (one cell, always uniform) — clean and avoids a separate uniformity scan.
  • FAANG follow-up: "What are quad trees used for?" Image compression, spatial indexing (collision detection, geographic point queries), and level-of-detail rendering.
  • Watch the constructor argument order: LeetCode's Node(bool val, bool isLeaf) puts val first — swapping them is a common silent bug.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Divide & Conquer›Problem 111 of 150
111

Merge k Sorted Lists

Hard

Problem. Given an array of k linked lists, each already sorted in ascending order, merge them into one sorted linked list and return its head. Constraints: k can be 0 (return null); total node count across all lists can be large (up to ~10^4), so an O(N log k) solution is expected, where N is the total number of nodes.

Example. Input [[1,4,5], [1,3,4], [2,6]] → output 1 -> 1 -> 2 -> 3 -> 4 -> 4 -> 5 -> 6. Trace: repeatedly take the smallest current head among the three lists.

Brute Force. Merge the lists one at a time: merge list 0 with list 1, merge that result with list 2, and so on. Reuses the two-list merge from problem 109. Correct but slow because early elements get re-traversed during every subsequent merge.

class Solution {
public:
    ListNode* mergeKLists(vector<ListNode*>& lists) {
        ListNode* merged = nullptr;
        for (ListNode* currentList : lists) {
            merged = mergeTwoSorted(merged, currentList);
        }
        return merged;
    }
private:
    ListNode* mergeTwoSorted(ListNode* firstList, ListNode* secondList) {
        ListNode placeholder(0);
        ListNode* tail = &placeholder;
        while (firstList != nullptr && secondList != nullptr) {
            if (firstList->val <= secondList->val) {
                tail->next = firstList;
                firstList = firstList->next;
            } else {
                tail->next = secondList;
                secondList = secondList->next;
            }
            tail = tail->next;
        }
        tail->next = (firstList != nullptr) ? firstList : secondList;
        return placeholder.next;
    }
};

Time: O(k·N), Space: O(1). Justification: the accumulated list grows toward N nodes, and each of the k merges re-walks that growing accumulator, giving roughly k·N work; only pointers are rewired so extra space is O(1).

Optimized. Two clean approaches. (1) Divide and conquer: pair up the lists and merge each pair, halving the number of lists each round, just like the merge step of merge sort — this gives O(N log k). (2) Min-heap: push the head of every list into a priority queue keyed by value, repeatedly pop the smallest and push its successor. Below is the divide-and-conquer version, which needs no extra container.

class Solution {
public:
    ListNode* mergeKLists(vector<ListNode*>& lists) {
        if (lists.empty()) return nullptr;
        return mergeRange(lists, 0, (int)lists.size() - 1);
    }
private:
    ListNode* mergeRange(vector<ListNode*>& lists, int leftIndex, int rightIndex) {
        if (leftIndex == rightIndex) return lists[leftIndex];
        int middleIndex = leftIndex + (rightIndex - leftIndex) / 2;
        ListNode* leftMerged = mergeRange(lists, leftIndex, middleIndex);
        ListNode* rightMerged = mergeRange(lists, middleIndex + 1, rightIndex);
        return mergeTwoSorted(leftMerged, rightMerged);
    }
    ListNode* mergeTwoSorted(ListNode* firstList, ListNode* secondList) {
        ListNode placeholder(0);
        ListNode* tail = &placeholder;
        while (firstList != nullptr && secondList != nullptr) {
            if (firstList->val <= secondList->val) {
                tail->next = firstList;
                firstList = firstList->next;
            } else {
                tail->next = secondList;
                secondList = secondList->next;
            }
            tail = tail->next;
        }
        tail->next = (firstList != nullptr) ? firstList : secondList;
        return placeholder.next;
    }
};

Time: O(N log k), Space: O(log k). Justification: there are log k rounds of pairwise merging, and each round moves all N nodes exactly once, giving O(N log k); the recursion stack is O(log k) deep and merging itself is O(1) extra space.

Interview Notes.

  • The min-heap alternative is also O(N log k): a priority_queue of size at most k, with N pop/push operations each costing O(log k). Use a comparator that orders by node->val.
  • Divide and conquer is often preferred in interviews because it reuses the two-list merge and needs no heap; mention both and let the interviewer choose.
  • FAANG follow-up: "What if the lists arrive as a stream and you cannot store them all?" The min-heap approach generalizes to streaming better than pairwise merging.
  • Edge cases that trip candidates: empty input array, some lists being null, and a single list — make sure each returns correctly.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Kadane's Algorithm›Problem 112 of 150
112

Maximum Subarray

Medium

Problem. Given an integer array nums, find the contiguous subarray (containing at least one number) that has the largest sum, and return that sum. The array can contain negative numbers, so the answer is not simply the sum of all positives. Constraints: 1 <= nums.length <= 10^5, values fit comfortably in a 32-bit int but the running sum should be tracked in a wide enough type.

Example. Input nums = [-2, 1, -3, 4, -1, 2, 1, -5, 4] → Output 6. Trace: the subarray [4, -1, 2, 1] sums to 6, which beats every other contiguous run.

Brute Force. Try every possible subarray. Use an outer loop to fix the start index, an inner loop to extend the end index, and accumulate the sum as the end moves so we do not recompute from scratch. Track the largest sum seen.

class Solution {
public:
    int maxSubArray(vector<int>& nums) {
        int maxSum = nums[0];
        for (int start = 0; start < (int)nums.size(); ++start) {
            int currentSum = 0;
            for (int end = start; end < (int)nums.size(); ++end) {
                currentSum += nums[end];
                maxSum = max(maxSum, currentSum);
            }
        }
        return maxSum;
    }
};

Time: O(n^2), Space: O(1). The two nested loops examine every (start, end) pair, giving roughly n^2/2 subarrays; we only keep a few scalar variables so space is constant.

Optimized. Apply Kadane directly. Keep currentSum, the best sum of a subarray ending at the current index, and maxSum, the best over all indices. At each element, either extend the previous subarray or restart at this element — that is max(currentElement, currentSum + currentElement). Update maxSum after each step. Initializing both to the first element handles the all-negative case cleanly, since the answer is then the single largest (least negative) element.

class Solution {
public:
    int maxSubArray(vector<int>& nums) {
        int currentSum = nums[0];
        int maxSum = nums[0];
        for (int index = 1; index < (int)nums.size(); ++index) {
            currentSum = max(nums[index], currentSum + nums[index]);
            maxSum = max(maxSum, currentSum);
        }
        return maxSum;
    }
};

Time: O(n), Space: O(1). A single pass touches each element once and we store only two scalars regardless of input size.

Interview Notes.

  • All-negative edge case: do not initialize maxSum to 0, or you will wrongly return 0 for an array like [-3, -1, -2]; initialize to nums[0] (or INT_MIN) so the least-negative element wins.
  • Follow-up "return the subarray indices": track a tentative tempStart that you reset whenever you restart currentSum at the current element, and commit bestStart/bestEnd whenever currentSum exceeds maxSum.
  • This is the canonical 1D DP collapse — interviewers love hearing you name the recurrence dp[i] = max(nums[i], dp[i-1] + nums[i]) and then explain why only the previous state matters.
  • A common follow-up is the divide-and-conquer solution (O(n log n)) — know it exists, but Kadane is strictly better and is the expected answer.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Kadane's Algorithm›Problem 113 of 150
113

Maximum Sum Circular Subarray

Medium

Problem. Given a circular integer array nums, find the maximum possible sum of a non-empty subarray, where the subarray may wrap around the end and continue from the beginning. Each element may be used at most once (no element appears twice in the chosen subarray). Constraints: 1 <= nums.length <= 3 * 10^4, values are 32-bit ints.

Example. Input nums = [5, -3, 5] → Output 10. Trace: the best subarray wraps around — take the last 5 and the first 5 (indices 2 then 0), giving 5 + 5 = 10, which beats the non-wrapping best of 5.

Brute Force. The answer is either a normal (non-wrapping) subarray or a wrapping one. Simulate wrapping by checking subarrays that start at each index and extend up to n elements using modular indexing, accumulating the sum and tracking the maximum.

class Solution {
public:
    int maxSubarraySumCircular(vector<int>& nums) {
        int arraySize = nums.size();
        int maxSum = nums[0];
        for (int start = 0; start < arraySize; ++start) {
            int currentSum = 0;
            for (int length = 0; length < arraySize; ++length) {
                currentSum += nums[(start + length) % arraySize];
                maxSum = max(maxSum, currentSum);
            }
        }
        return maxSum;
    }
};

Time: O(n^2), Space: O(1). The outer loop fixes a start, the inner loop extends up to n elements with wraparound, and we keep only scalar accumulators.

Optimized. Key insight: a wrapping subarray is exactly the total array sum minus a contiguous non-wrapping subarray in the middle. So the best wrapping sum is totalSum - minSubarraySum, where minSubarraySum is the smallest contiguous sum found by running Kadane with min instead of max. The overall answer is max(maxStraight, totalSum - minSubarray). The critical edge case: if every element is negative, then totalSum - minSubarray equals 0 (you'd be "removing" the entire array, leaving an empty subarray, which is not allowed), so when maxStraight < 0 you must return maxStraight alone.

class Solution {
public:
    int maxSubarraySumCircular(vector<int>& nums) {
        int totalSum = 0;
        int currentMax = nums[0];
        int bestStraight = nums[0];
        int currentMin = nums[0];
        int bestWrappedComplement = nums[0];
        for (int index = 0; index < (int)nums.size(); ++index) {
            int value = nums[index];
            totalSum += value;
            if (index == 0) continue;
            currentMax = max(value, currentMax + value);
            bestStraight = max(bestStraight, currentMax);
            currentMin = min(value, currentMin + value);
            bestWrappedComplement = min(bestWrappedComplement, currentMin);
        }
        if (bestStraight < 0) {
            return bestStraight;
        }
        return max(bestStraight, totalSum - bestWrappedComplement);
    }
};

Time: O(n), Space: O(1). We run two Kadane passes (max and min) fused into one loop and keep only a handful of scalars.

Interview Notes.

  • All-negative edge case is the trap here: totalSum - minSubarray collapses to an empty subarray, so guard with if (bestStraight < 0) return bestStraight; before considering the wrap.
  • Explain the decomposition out loud: the maximum wrapping subarray = total minus the minimum internal subarray, because keeping the prefix+suffix is the same as discarding the middle.
  • "Return the indices" follow-up is harder when the answer wraps — you track start/end for both the max-Kadane and min-Kadane passes, and for the wrap case the chosen subarray is the complement of the min-subarray (a suffix joined to a prefix).
  • Mention you fold both passes into a single loop for clarity and cache-friendliness; doing two separate scans is also fine and arguably easier to read under pressure.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Binary Search›Problem 114 of 150
114

Search Insert Position

Easy

Problem. Given a sorted array of distinct integers and a target value, return the index where the target is found. If it is not present, return the index where it would be inserted to keep the array sorted. Constraints: the array is sorted ascending, sizes up to 10^4, values fit in int.

Example. nums = [1, 3, 5, 6], target = 5 → output 2 (found at index 2). For target = 2 → output 1 (would slot between 1 and 3). Trace for target 2: window shrinks until low lands on the first element >= 2, which is index 1.

Brute Force. Walk left to right and return the first index whose value is greater than or equal to the target; if none qualifies, the target belongs at the end.

class Solution {
public:
    int searchInsert(vector<int>& nums, int target) {
        for (int index = 0; index < (int)nums.size(); index++) {
            if (nums[index] >= target) {
                return index;
            }
        }
        return nums.size();
    }
};

Time: O(n), Space: O(1). We may scan the entire array once in the worst case, using only a loop counter.

Optimized. This is the classic "lower bound" search: find the leftmost index whose value is >= target. Use the low < high template where high starts at nums.size() (one past the end) so the answer can legitimately be the insertion-at-end position. Whenever nums[mid] >= target, that index is a candidate, so move high to mid; otherwise the answer is strictly to the right, so move low to mid + 1.

class Solution {
public:
    int searchInsert(vector<int>& nums, int target) {
        int low = 0;
        int high = nums.size();
        while (low < high) {
            int mid = low + (high - low) / 2;
            if (nums[mid] >= target) {
                high = mid;
            } else {
                low = mid + 1;
            }
        }
        return low;
    }
};

Time: O(log n), Space: O(1). Each iteration halves the window, and we keep only a few index variables.

Interview Notes.

  • This is the std::lower_bound pattern — mentioning the STL equivalent (lower_bound(nums.begin(), nums.end(), target) - nums.begin()) signals fluency.
  • Note why high = nums.size() not nums.size() - 1: the valid output range is [0, n], and an exclusive upper bound captures the insert-at-end case cleanly.
  • FAANG follow-up: "Now the array has duplicates — return the first occurrence" is the same lower-bound code unchanged.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Binary Search›Problem 115 of 150
115

Search a 2D Matrix

Medium

Problem. Given an m x n matrix where each row is sorted ascending and the first integer of each row is greater than the last integer of the previous row, determine whether a target value exists. Constraints: the whole matrix is effectively one sorted sequence read row by row.

Example. matrix = [[1,3,5,7],[10,11,16,20],[23,30,34,60]], target = 3 → true. Treating it as the flat sorted list [1,3,5,7,10,...], index 1 holds 3.

Brute Force. Inspect every cell and compare against the target.

class Solution {
public:
    bool searchMatrix(vector<vector<int>>& matrix, int target) {
        for (const vector<int>& row : matrix) {
            for (int value : row) {
                if (value == target) {
                    return true;
                }
            }
        }
        return false;
    }
};

Time: O(m·n), Space: O(1). We may touch every one of the m·n cells.

Optimized. The key insight is the strong ordering guarantee: reading the matrix row by row produces one fully sorted sequence, so we can binary-search the virtual flat array of length m·n. Map a flat index back to a 2D cell with row = mid / columnCount and column = mid % columnCount.

class Solution {
public:
    bool searchMatrix(vector<vector<int>>& matrix, int target) {
        int rowCount = matrix.size();
        int columnCount = matrix[0].size();
        int low = 0;
        int high = rowCount * columnCount - 1;
        while (low <= high) {
            int mid = low + (high - low) / 2;
            int value = matrix[mid / columnCount][mid % columnCount];
            if (value == target) {
                return true;
            } else if (value < target) {
                low = mid + 1;
            } else {
                high = mid - 1;
            }
        }
        return false;
    }
};

Time: O(log(m·n)), Space: O(1). One binary search over m·n virtual elements.

Interview Notes.

  • The index mapping mid / columnCount and mid % columnCount is the whole trick — practice it until it is automatic.
  • FAANG follow-up: the related problem "Search a 2D Matrix II" weakens the guarantee (rows and columns sorted, but rows are not chained). There the flat-array trick fails; the answer is staircase search from the top-right corner in O(m + n). Knowing the difference between the two problems is a frequent gotcha.
  • Here low <= high with an inclusive high = m*n - 1 is the natural fit since we want exact-match detection.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Binary Search›Problem 116 of 150
116

Find Peak Element

Medium

Problem. A peak element is one strictly greater than its neighbors. Given an array where nums[i] != nums[i+1] for all valid i, return the index of any peak. Imagine nums[-1] and nums[n] as negative infinity, so the ends can be peaks. Constraints: must run in O(log n).

Example. nums = [1, 2, 3, 1] → 2 (value 3 is greater than both neighbors). For nums = [1, 2, 1, 3, 5, 6, 4], either index 1 or index 5 is a valid answer.

Brute Force. Scan and return the first index larger than its right neighbor (the boundaries are handled by the imagined infinities).

class Solution {
public:
    int findPeakElement(vector<int>& nums) {
        for (int index = 0; index + 1 < (int)nums.size(); index++) {
            if (nums[index] > nums[index + 1]) {
                return index;
            }
        }
        return nums.size() - 1;
    }
};

Time: O(n), Space: O(1). A single linear pass.

Optimized. The monotonic insight is subtle because the array is not sorted: compare nums[mid] with nums[mid + 1]. If nums[mid] < nums[mid + 1], the slope is rising, so a peak must exist to the right — move low to mid + 1. Otherwise the slope is falling (or mid itself is a peak), so a peak exists at mid or to its left — move high to mid. Using low < high guarantees mid + 1 is always a valid index and the window converges to a single peak.

class Solution {
public:
    int findPeakElement(vector<int>& nums) {
        int low = 0;
        int high = nums.size() - 1;
        while (low < high) {
            int mid = low + (high - low) / 2;
            if (nums[mid] < nums[mid + 1]) {
                low = mid + 1;
            } else {
                high = mid;
            }
        }
        return low;
    }
};

Time: O(log n), Space: O(1). We discard half the array each step even without global sorting.

Interview Notes.

  • The eye-opener for interviewers: binary search needs a monotonic decision, not a sorted array. "Which half is guaranteed to contain a peak?" is the predicate here.
  • Why low < high and not <=: with <= and high = mid you risk an infinite loop when low == high == mid. Converging on a single survivor avoids that.
  • FAANG follow-up: "find a peak in a 2D grid" extends the same divide-and-conquer idea along a column/row and is a known hard variant.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Binary Search›Problem 117 of 150
117

Search in Rotated Sorted Array

Medium

Problem. A sorted array of distinct values was rotated at some unknown pivot (e.g. [0,1,2,4,5,6,7] became [4,5,6,7,0,1,2]). Given the rotated array and a target, return its index or -1. Constraints: must run in O(log n).

Example. nums = [4,5,6,7,0,1,2], target = 0 → 4. Trace: at mid = 3 (value 7) the left half [4,5,6,7] is sorted but 0 is not inside it, so search right.

Brute Force. Linear scan for the target.

class Solution {
public:
    int search(vector<int>& nums, int target) {
        for (int index = 0; index < (int)nums.size(); index++) {
            if (nums[index] == target) {
                return index;
            }
        }
        return -1;
    }
};

Time: O(n), Space: O(1). Every element may be visited.

Optimized. The insight: at any mid, at least one of the two halves is properly sorted. Detect which one by comparing nums[low] with nums[mid]. If the left half is sorted and the target lies within its value range, search left; otherwise search right. Symmetrically for a sorted right half. This keeps each step O(log n) despite the rotation.

class Solution {
public:
    int search(vector<int>& nums, int target) {
        int low = 0;
        int high = nums.size() - 1;
        while (low <= high) {
            int mid = low + (high - low) / 2;
            if (nums[mid] == target) {
                return mid;
            }
            if (nums[low] <= nums[mid]) {
                if (nums[low] <= target && target < nums[mid]) {
                    high = mid - 1;
                } else {
                    low = mid + 1;
                }
            } else {
                if (nums[mid] < target && target <= nums[high]) {
                    low = mid + 1;
                } else {
                    high = mid - 1;
                }
            }
        }
        return -1;
    }
};

Time: O(log n), Space: O(1). One half is always discarded.

Interview Notes.

  • The nums[low] <= nums[mid] check (using <=, not <) handles the two-element window correctly — a classic off-by-one trap.
  • FAANG follow-up: "what if duplicates are allowed?" (LeetCode 81) breaks the sorted-half detection when nums[low] == nums[mid] == nums[high]; you fall back to shrinking both ends by one, degrading worst case to O(n).
  • Be ready to explain why exactly one half is sorted — the rotation point can sit in only one of them.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Binary Search›Problem 118 of 150
118

Find First and Last Position of Element in Sorted Array

Medium

Problem. Given a sorted array, return the starting and ending indices of a target value as [first, last]. If the target is absent, return [-1, -1]. Constraints: must run in O(log n); values may repeat.

Example. nums = [5,7,7,8,8,10], target = 8 → [3, 4]. For target = 6 → [-1, -1].

Brute Force. Scan once to record the first match and keep updating the last match.

class Solution {
public:
    vector<int> searchRange(vector<int>& nums, int target) {
        int first = -1;
        int last = -1;
        for (int index = 0; index < (int)nums.size(); index++) {
            if (nums[index] == target) {
                if (first == -1) {
                    first = index;
                }
                last = index;
            }
        }
        return {first, last};
    }
};

Time: O(n), Space: O(1). One full pass; the output vector is constant size.

Optimized. Run two boundary binary searches. The first finds the leftmost index >= target (lower bound); the second finds the leftmost index > target, whose predecessor is the last occurrence. After computing the lower bound, confirm it actually points at the target before trusting it.

class Solution {
public:
    vector<int> searchRange(vector<int>& nums, int target) {
        int first = lowerBound(nums, target);
        if (first == (int)nums.size() || nums[first] != target) {
            return {-1, -1};
        }
        int last = lowerBound(nums, target + 1) - 1;
        return {first, last};
    }
private:
    int lowerBound(vector<int>& nums, int target) {
        int low = 0;
        int high = nums.size();
        while (low < high) {
            int mid = low + (high - low) / 2;
            if (nums[mid] >= target) {
                high = mid;
            } else {
                low = mid + 1;
            }
        }
        return low;
    }
};

Time: O(log n), Space: O(1). Two logarithmic searches, only index variables stored.

Interview Notes.

  • Reusing one lowerBound helper twice (target and target + 1) is cleaner than writing separate first/last searches and avoids subtle boundary bugs.
  • Watch the overflow edge: if target is INT_MAX, target + 1 overflows. Mention guarding it or searching for the upper bound directly in a strict interview.
  • FAANG follow-up: "count occurrences of the target" is just last - first + 1, a one-line extension once you have both boundaries.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Binary Search›Problem 119 of 150
119

Find Minimum in Rotated Sorted Array

Medium

Problem. A sorted array of distinct values was rotated at an unknown pivot. Return the minimum element. Constraints: must run in O(log n).

Example. nums = [3,4,5,1,2] → 1. For nums = [11,13,15,17] (rotated zero times) → 11.

Brute Force. Scan and track the smallest value.

class Solution {
public:
    int findMin(vector<int>& nums) {
        int minimum = nums[0];
        for (int value : nums) {
            if (value < minimum) {
                minimum = value;
            }
        }
        return minimum;
    }
};

Time: O(n), Space: O(1). A linear pass over all values.

Optimized. The minimum is the single "inflection point" where the rotation wraps. Compare nums[mid] with nums[high]: if nums[mid] > nums[high], the minimum must lie strictly to the right of mid, so move low to mid + 1; otherwise the minimum is at mid or to its left, so move high to mid. Comparing against high (not low) is what makes the non-rotated case work correctly.

class Solution {
public:
    int findMin(vector<int>& nums) {
        int low = 0;
        int high = nums.size() - 1;
        while (low < high) {
            int mid = low + (high - low) / 2;
            if (nums[mid] > nums[high]) {
                low = mid + 1;
            } else {
                high = mid;
            }
        }
        return nums[low];
    }
};

Time: O(log n), Space: O(1). Half the window is dropped each iteration.

Interview Notes.

  • A common mistake is comparing nums[mid] to nums[low]; that fails on an already-sorted (zero-rotation) array. Anchor on high instead.
  • The low < high template is deliberate: the loop converges to the single minimum without ever reading an out-of-bounds index.
  • FAANG follow-up: "with duplicates" (LeetCode 154) — when nums[mid] == nums[high] you cannot tell which side holds the min, so decrement high, pushing the worst case to O(n).
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Binary Search›Problem 120 of 150
120

Median of Two Sorted Arrays

Hard

Problem. Given two sorted arrays, return the median of the combined set of all elements, without actually merging them. Constraints: the official requirement is O(log(m + n)) time; total length can be even or odd.

Example. nums1 = [1, 3], nums2 = [2] → 2.0 (merged [1,2,3], middle element). nums1 = [1, 2], nums2 = [3, 4] → 2.5 (average of the two middles of [1,2,3,4]).

Brute Force. Merge the two arrays in sorted order, then pick the middle element(s).

class Solution {
public:
    double findMedianSortedArrays(vector<int>& nums1, vector<int>& nums2) {
        vector<int> merged;
        merged.reserve(nums1.size() + nums2.size());
        int firstIndex = 0;
        int secondIndex = 0;
        while (firstIndex < (int)nums1.size() && secondIndex < (int)nums2.size()) {
            if (nums1[firstIndex] <= nums2[secondIndex]) {
                merged.push_back(nums1[firstIndex++]);
            } else {
                merged.push_back(nums2[secondIndex++]);
            }
        }
        while (firstIndex < (int)nums1.size()) {
            merged.push_back(nums1[firstIndex++]);
        }
        while (secondIndex < (int)nums2.size()) {
            merged.push_back(nums2[secondIndex++]);
        }
        int total = merged.size();
        if (total % 2 == 1) {
            return merged[total / 2];
        }
        return (merged[total / 2 - 1] + merged[total / 2]) / 2.0;
    }
};

Time: O(m + n), Space: O(m + n). We build the full merged array.

Optimized. The O(log(m + n)) approach is partition-based binary search. Choose a cut in the smaller array; the cut in the larger array is then forced so that exactly half of all elements sit on the left side. A partition is valid when every element on the left is <= every element on the right, checked via maxLeftFirst <= minRightSecond and maxLeftSecond <= minRightFirst. Binary-search the cut position in the smaller array, using sentinels (INT_MIN/INT_MAX) for empty sides. Always binary-search the smaller array to keep the cut range valid.

class Solution {
public:
    double findMedianSortedArrays(vector<int>& nums1, vector<int>& nums2) {
        if (nums1.size() > nums2.size()) {
            return findMedianSortedArrays(nums2, nums1);
        }
        int firstSize = nums1.size();
        int secondSize = nums2.size();
        int low = 0;
        int high = firstSize;
        int halfLength = (firstSize + secondSize + 1) / 2;
        while (low <= high) {
            int cutFirst = low + (high - low) / 2;
            int cutSecond = halfLength - cutFirst;
            int maxLeftFirst = (cutFirst == 0) ? INT_MIN : nums1[cutFirst - 1];
            int minRightFirst = (cutFirst == firstSize) ? INT_MAX : nums1[cutFirst];
            int maxLeftSecond = (cutSecond == 0) ? INT_MIN : nums2[cutSecond - 1];
            int minRightSecond = (cutSecond == secondSize) ? INT_MAX : nums2[cutSecond];
            if (maxLeftFirst <= minRightSecond && maxLeftSecond <= minRightFirst) {
                if ((firstSize + secondSize) % 2 == 1) {
                    return max(maxLeftFirst, maxLeftSecond);
                }
                return (max(maxLeftFirst, maxLeftSecond) + min(minRightFirst, minRightSecond)) / 2.0;
            } else if (maxLeftFirst > minRightSecond) {
                high = cutFirst - 1;
            } else {
                low = cutFirst + 1;
            }
        }
        return 0.0;
    }
};

Time: O(log(min(m, n))), Space: O(1). We binary-search only the smaller array's cut positions and store a handful of boundary values.

Interview Notes.

  • This is one of the hardest "Top 150" problems; many interviewers accept the clean O(log(m + n)) merge-walk (advance through (m+n)/2 elements with two pointers) if you cannot finish the partition version under pressure — but the partition method is the gold standard.
  • The forced relationship cutSecond = halfLength - cutFirst is the heart of it; halfLength = (m + n + 1) / 2 makes the odd-length case put the median on the left side automatically.
  • Always recurse to search the smaller array first; otherwise cutSecond can go negative and the sentinels break.
  • FAANG follow-up: "find the k-th smallest of two sorted arrays" generalizes this and is a frequent harder variant.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Heap (Priority Queue)›Problem 121 of 150
121

Kth Largest Element in an Array

Medium

Problem. Given an integer array nums and an integer k, return the k-th largest element in sorted order (not the k-th distinct element). Constraints: 1 <= k <= nums.size() <= 10^5, values can be negative.

Example. nums = [3,2,1,5,6,4], k = 2 → 5. Sorted descending is [6,5,4,3,2,1]; the 2nd element is 5.

Brute Force. Sort the whole array in descending order and index into position k - 1. Simple and correct, but it does more work than needed — we sort all n elements just to read one.

class Solution {
public:
    int findKthLargest(vector<int>& nums, int k) {
        sort(nums.begin(), nums.end(), greater<int>());
        return nums[k - 1];
    }
};

Time: O(n log n), Space: O(1). Sorting dominates; the comparison-based sort visits all n elements log n times. No extra storage beyond the sort's internal stack.

Optimized. Maintain a min-heap of size k. Push elements in; whenever the heap exceeds size k, pop the smallest. After processing everything, the heap holds the k largest elements and its top (the smallest of those) is exactly the k-th largest. We never store more than k items, and each push/pop is O(log k).

class Solution {
public:
    int findKthLargest(vector<int>& nums, int k) {
        priority_queue<int, vector<int>, greater<int>> minHeap;
        for (int currentNumber : nums) {
            minHeap.push(currentNumber);
            if (minHeap.size() > static_cast<size_t>(k)) {
                minHeap.pop();
            }
        }
        return minHeap.top();
    }
};

Time: O(n log k), Space: O(k). Each of the n elements triggers an O(log k) heap operation; the heap is capped at k elements.

Interview Notes.

  • The optimal-on-average solution is Quickselect (partition like quicksort but recurse into only one side): O(n) average time, O(n^2) worst case, O(1) extra space. Interviewers often want you to mention it.
  • If asked to guarantee worst-case O(n), reference the median-of-medians pivot strategy.
  • Clarify whether duplicates count separately — here they do (k-th largest by position, not distinct value).
  • The heap solution is preferred when data streams in and you can't hold all of it; Quickselect needs the full array in memory.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Heap (Priority Queue)›Problem 122 of 150
122

IPO

Hard

Problem. You can complete at most k projects. Each project i has a profits[i] and a capital[i] requirement. You start with w capital; finishing a project adds its profit to your capital. You can only start a project if your current capital is at least its requirement, and each project is done at most once. Maximize final capital. Constraints: up to 10^5 projects.

Example. k = 2, w = 0, profits = [1,2,3], capital = [0,1,1] → 4. With capital 0 only project 0 is affordable (profit 1, capital → 1). Now projects 1 and 2 are affordable; pick the bigger profit 3 (capital → 4). Done with 2 projects.

Brute Force. Repeat k times: scan every not-yet-done project, find the affordable one with the highest profit, take it, add its profit. Each of the k rounds rescans all n projects.

class Solution {
public:
    int findMaximizedCapital(int k, int w, vector<int>& profits, vector<int>& capital) {
        int projectCount = profits.size();
        vector<bool> alreadyDone(projectCount, false);
        for (int round = 0; round < k; ++round) {
            int bestIndex = -1;
            for (int projectIndex = 0; projectIndex < projectCount; ++projectIndex) {
                if (!alreadyDone[projectIndex] && capital[projectIndex] <= w) {
                    if (bestIndex == -1 || profits[projectIndex] > profits[bestIndex]) {
                        bestIndex = projectIndex;
                    }
                }
            }
            if (bestIndex == -1) break;
            w += profits[bestIndex];
            alreadyDone[bestIndex] = true;
        }
        return w;
    }
};

Time: O(k·n), Space: O(n). Each round is a full O(n) scan; the boolean array tracks completed projects.

Optimized. This is a greedy problem: at every step take the affordable project with the largest profit. Sort projects by capital requirement so we can cheaply unlock them as our capital grows. Keep a max-heap of profits of all currently affordable projects. Each round, move every newly-affordable project (capital ≤ w) into the heap, then pop the heap's top — the biggest reachable profit.

class Solution {
public:
    int findMaximizedCapital(int k, int w, vector<int>& profits, vector<int>& capital) {
        int projectCount = profits.size();
        vector<pair<int, int>> projectsByCapital;
        for (int projectIndex = 0; projectIndex < projectCount; ++projectIndex) {
            projectsByCapital.push_back({capital[projectIndex], profits[projectIndex]});
        }
        sort(projectsByCapital.begin(), projectsByCapital.end());

        priority_queue<int> affordableProfits;
        int nextProject = 0;
        for (int round = 0; round < k; ++round) {
            while (nextProject < projectCount && projectsByCapital[nextProject].first <= w) {
                affordableProfits.push(projectsByCapital[nextProject].second);
                ++nextProject;
            }
            if (affordableProfits.empty()) break;
            w += affordableProfits.top();
            affordableProfits.pop();
        }
        return w;
    }
};

Time: O(n log n), Space: O(n). Sorting is O(n log n); each project enters and leaves the heap at most once, costing O(n log n) total over all rounds.

Interview Notes.

  • The key realization is that doing a project never reduces capital, so the set of affordable projects only grows — that is what makes pure greedy correct here.
  • Sorting by capital lets the nextProject pointer advance monotonically; you never re-scan unlocked projects.
  • A common follow-up: what if profits could be negative? Then greedy breaks and you'd need DP — interviewers like probing whether you understand why greedy works.
  • Watch the early exit: if no project is affordable, no future project will be either, so break immediately.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Heap (Priority Queue)›Problem 123 of 150
123

Find K Pairs with Smallest Sums

Medium

Problem. Given two sorted integer arrays nums1 and nums2 and an integer k, find the k pairs (u, v) with u from nums1 and v from nums2 that have the smallest sums. Return them as a list of pairs. Constraints: arrays can each be up to 10^5 long, so the total pair count m·n can be huge.

Example. nums1 = [1,7,11], nums2 = [2,4,6], k = 3 → [[1,2],[1,4],[1,6]]. Their sums 3, 5, 7 are the three smallest among all nine pairs.

Brute Force. Generate all m·n pairs, sort them by sum, take the first k. Correct but builds and sorts a potentially enormous list.

class Solution {
public:
    vector<vector<int>> kSmallestPairs(vector<int>& nums1, vector<int>& nums2, int k) {
        vector<vector<int>> allPairs;
        for (int firstValue : nums1) {
            for (int secondValue : nums2) {
                allPairs.push_back({firstValue, secondValue});
            }
        }
        sort(allPairs.begin(), allPairs.end(), [](const vector<int>& leftPair, const vector<int>& rightPair) {
            return leftPair[0] + leftPair[1] < rightPair[0] + rightPair[1];
        });
        vector<vector<int>> result;
        for (int pairIndex = 0; pairIndex < k && pairIndex < static_cast<int>(allPairs.size()); ++pairIndex) {
            result.push_back(allPairs[pairIndex]);
        }
        return result;
    }
};

Time: O(m·n log(m·n)), Space: O(m·n). We materialize every pair and sort the lot.

Optimized. Use a min-heap of candidate pairs ordered by sum. Because both arrays are sorted, the smallest pair must be (nums1[0], nums2[0]). Seed the heap with pairs (nums1[i], nums2[0]) for each i. Each time we pop the smallest pair (i, j), we add its sum to the result and push the next candidate in that row, (i, j+1). This explores only the frontier of promising pairs rather than all of them.

class Solution {
public:
    vector<vector<int>> kSmallestPairs(vector<int>& nums1, vector<int>& nums2, int k) {
        vector<vector<int>> result;
        if (nums1.empty() || nums2.empty()) return result;

        priority_queue<tuple<int, int, int>, vector<tuple<int, int, int>>, greater<tuple<int, int, int>>> minHeap;
        for (int firstIndex = 0; firstIndex < static_cast<int>(nums1.size()) && firstIndex < k; ++firstIndex) {
            minHeap.push({nums1[firstIndex] + nums2[0], firstIndex, 0});
        }

        while (!minHeap.empty() && static_cast<int>(result.size()) < k) {
            auto [currentSum, firstIndex, secondIndex] = minHeap.top();
            minHeap.pop();
            result.push_back({nums1[firstIndex], nums2[secondIndex]});
            if (secondIndex + 1 < static_cast<int>(nums2.size())) {
                minHeap.push({nums1[firstIndex] + nums2[secondIndex + 1], firstIndex, secondIndex + 1});
            }
        }
        return result;
    }
};

Time: O(k log k), Space: O(k). The heap holds at most about k entries, and we pop k times, each pop/push costing O(log k).

Interview Notes.

  • Storing the sum as the first element of the tuple lets the default tuple comparison sort by sum automatically — a clean trick worth explaining.
  • We seed at most k rows (firstIndex < k) because we can never need more than k pairs, which keeps the heap small even when nums1 is enormous.
  • This is the same pattern as "merge k sorted lists" — each row i of nums1 paired with nums2 is a sorted sequence, and we are merging them.
  • Edge cases interviewers check: empty arrays, and k larger than m·n (return all pairs).
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Heap (Priority Queue)›Problem 124 of 150
124

Find Median from Data Stream

Hard

Problem. Design a data structure that supports addNum(int num) to ingest numbers one at a time from a stream, and findMedian() to return the median of all numbers seen so far. The median is the middle value when the count is odd, or the average of the two middle values when even. Constraints: up to 5·10^4 calls, so each operation must be fast.

Example. Add 1, add 2 → median 1.5 (average of 1 and 2). Add 3 → median 2 (middle of 1, 2, 3).

Brute Force. Keep all numbers in a vector. On each findMedian, sort the vector and read the middle. Easy to reason about but every query re-sorts.

class MedianFinder {
public:
    vector<int> allNumbers;

    MedianFinder() {}

    void addNum(int num) {
        allNumbers.push_back(num);
    }

    double findMedian() {
        sort(allNumbers.begin(), allNumbers.end());
        int totalCount = allNumbers.size();
        if (totalCount % 2 == 1) {
            return allNumbers[totalCount / 2];
        }
        return (allNumbers[totalCount / 2 - 1] + allNumbers[totalCount / 2]) / 2.0;
    }
};

Time: O(1) add, O(n log n) per findMedian, Space: O(n). Each query pays a full sort.

Optimized. Use the two-heap technique. Keep a max-heap (lowerHalf) for the smaller half of the numbers and a min-heap (upperHalf) for the larger half. Maintain the invariant that lowerHalf has the same number of elements as upperHalf or exactly one more. The median is then either the top of lowerHalf (odd total) or the average of the two tops (even total). On each insert, push into lowerHalf, hand its top over to upperHalf, and rebalance if upperHalf grew larger — this keeps both halves correctly partitioned.

class MedianFinder {
public:
    priority_queue<int> lowerHalf;
    priority_queue<int, vector<int>, greater<int>> upperHalf;

    MedianFinder() {}

    void addNum(int num) {
        lowerHalf.push(num);
        upperHalf.push(lowerHalf.top());
        lowerHalf.pop();
        if (upperHalf.size() > lowerHalf.size()) {
            lowerHalf.push(upperHalf.top());
            upperHalf.pop();
        }
    }

    double findMedian() {
        if (lowerHalf.size() > upperHalf.size()) {
            return lowerHalf.top();
        }
        return (lowerHalf.top() + upperHalf.top()) / 2.0;
    }
};

Time: O(log n) per addNum, O(1) per findMedian, Space: O(n). Inserts do a constant number of O(log n) heap operations; the median is read directly from the tops.

Interview Notes.

  • The "push to lowerHalf, move its max to upperHalf, rebalance" dance is the cleanest way to keep both halves sorted relative to each other without comparing values yourself.
  • The invariant lowerHalf.size() ∈ {upperHalf.size(), upperHalf.size() + 1} is what makes findMedian O(1) — state it explicitly in an interview.
  • Common follow-up: if all numbers are in a small fixed range (e.g., 0–100), a counting/bucket approach gives O(1) add; if 99% of values fall in a range, mention a hybrid of buckets plus heaps for the tails.
  • Another follow-up is a sliding-window median, which needs removals — heaps don't delete arbitrary elements cheaply, so you'd reach for a balanced BST or an ordered multiset (std::multiset) instead.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Bit Manipulation›Problem 125 of 150
125

Add Binary

Easy

Problem. Given two non-empty binary strings a and b, return their sum as a binary string. Inputs contain only '0' and '1', have no leading zeros (except the string "0" itself), and can be up to 10^4 characters long — far longer than any built-in integer can hold, so you must add them as strings. Example. a = "11", b = "1" → "100". Trace from the right: 1+1 = 10 (write 0, carry 1); next 1+0+carry 1 = 10 (write 0, carry 1); no digits left but carry 1 (write 1). Reading the written bits bottom-to-top gives 100. Brute Force. Convert both strings to integers, add, convert back. This is conceptually simple but breaks immediately: a 10^4-bit number overflows long long (which holds ~63 bits), so this approach is wrong for the stated constraints, not merely slow. We include it only to show why string addition is necessary.

class Solution {
public:
    string addBinary(string a, string b) {
        unsigned long long left = stoull(a, nullptr, 2);
        unsigned long long right = stoull(b, nullptr, 2);
        unsigned long long total = left + right;
        if (total == 0) return "0";
        string result;
        while (total > 0) {
            result.push_back(char('0' + (total & 1)));
            total >>= 1;
        }
        reverse(result.begin(), result.end());
        return result;
    }
};

Time: O(n), Space: O(n) — but only valid when both numbers fit in 64 bits; for the real constraints it produces a wrong answer. Optimized. Walk both strings from the least-significant end (the rightmost character), summing matching digits plus a running carry. At each position the column sum is 0..3; the output bit is sum & 1 (sum modulo 2) and the next carry is sum >> 1 (sum divided by 2). Continue while either string has digits left or a carry remains, then reverse the collected bits.

class Solution {
public:
    string addBinary(string a, string b) {
        string result;
        int i = a.size() - 1;
        int j = b.size() - 1;
        int carry = 0;
        while (i >= 0 || j >= 0 || carry) {
            int sum = carry;
            if (i >= 0) sum += a[i--] - '0';
            if (j >= 0) sum += b[j--] - '0';
            result.push_back(char('0' + (sum & 1)));
            carry = sum >> 1;
        }
        reverse(result.begin(), result.end());
        return result;
    }
};

Time: O(max(n, m)), Space: O(max(n, m)) — one pass over the longer string; the result string is the only extra storage and its length is at most one more than the longer input. Interview Notes.

  • The whole problem is just elementary-school column addition in base 2; the only bit tricks are sum & 1 for the digit and sum >> 1 for the carry.
  • Building the result back-to-front and reversing once at the end is O(n); repeatedly prepending to the front would be O(n^2).
  • The same skeleton solves "Add Strings" (base 10) — swap & 1/>> 1 for % 10// 10.
  • Mention that you deliberately avoided integer conversion because of overflow; interviewers like hearing you anticipate the long-input constraint.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Bit Manipulation›Problem 126 of 150
126

Reverse Bits

Easy

Problem. Given a 32-bit unsigned integer, return the integer formed by reversing the order of its 32 bits (bit 0 swaps with bit 31, bit 1 with bit 30, and so on). The input is always treated as exactly 32 bits wide. Example. n = 43261596 = 00000010100101000001111010011100. Reversing the 32-bit string gives 00111001011110000010100101000000 = 964176192. Brute Force. Read the 32 bits into a string from the input, reverse the string, parse it back to an integer. It works but allocates a heap string and does character parsing for a pure-arithmetic task.

class Solution {
public:
    uint32_t reverseBits(uint32_t n) {
        string bits;
        for (int i = 0; i < 32; ++i) {
            bits.push_back(char('0' + (n & 1)));
            n >>= 1;
        }
        return static_cast<uint32_t>(stoul(bits, nullptr, 2));
    }
};

Time: O(32) = O(1), Space: O(32) = O(1) — fixed 32 iterations and a fixed-length string; constant, but with unnecessary allocation. Optimized. Build the answer bit by bit without any string. For each of the 32 positions, shift result left to open a new low slot, drop in the current lowest bit of n via n & 1, then shift n right to expose the next bit. Pulling bits off the low end of n while pushing them onto the low end of a left-shifting result reverses their order naturally.

class Solution {
public:
    uint32_t reverseBits(uint32_t n) {
        uint32_t result = 0;
        for (int i = 0; i < 32; ++i) {
            result = (result << 1) | (n & 1);
            n >>= 1;
        }
        return result;
    }
};

Time: O(1), Space: O(1) — exactly 32 iterations of constant work and a single integer of storage. Interview Notes.

  • The type is uint32_t for a reason: right-shifting a signed negative int is implementation-defined and may sign-extend (copy the sign bit in), corrupting the reversal. Always reverse on an unsigned type.
  • Follow-up: "If this is called millions of times, how do you speed it up?" Precompute a 256-entry lookup table that reverses one byte, then reverse the four bytes and reorder them — O(1) with a tiny constant.
  • A divide-and-conquer variant swaps halves, then quarters, then bytes, then nibbles, then pairs with five masked shifts; mention it as the lookup-free fast path.
How well did you know it?
Patterns›Bit Manipulation›Problem 127 of 150
