int smallest = minHeap.top();

You can also order by a custom rule using a comparator; this is common for heaps of pairs.

Key <algorithm> functions

sort — sorts a range in O(n log n). Ascending by default; pass a comparator lambda to change the order.

sort(numbers.begin(), numbers.end());
sort(numbers.begin(), numbers.end(), [](int left, int right) {
    return left > right;
});

reverse — flips a range in place, O(n).

reverse(numbers.begin(), numbers.end());

min / max — the smaller or larger of two values, O(1). min_element / max_element — an iterator to the smallest/largest in a range, O(n); dereference with * to get the value.

int larger = max(firstNumber, secondNumber);
int smallestInRange = *min_element(numbers.begin(), numbers.end());

lower_bound / upper_bound — binary search on a sorted range, O(log n). lower_bound returns an iterator to the first element not less than the target; upper_bound to the first element strictly greater.

sort(numbers.begin(), numbers.end());
auto position = lower_bound(numbers.begin(), numbers.end(), 5);
int indexFound = position - numbers.begin();

accumulate — sums (or folds) a range, O(n); lives in <numeric>. Give it a starting value, and make that value 0LL (a long long) when the total might overflow an int.

#include <numeric>
long long total = accumulate(numbers.begin(), numbers.end(), 0LL);

swap — exchanges two values, O(1).

swap(firstNumber, secondNumber);

__builtin_popcount — a fast built-in (GCC/Clang) that counts the number of 1 bits in an integer, O(1). Useful in bit-manipulation problems; use __builtin_popcountll for a long long.

int oneBits = __builtin_popcount(13);

A note on <climits> constants

The header <climits> defines the limits of the integer types. The two you will reach for constantly are INT_MAX (largest int, about 2.1 billion) and INT_MIN (most negative int). They make perfect sentinel starting values: initialize a running minimum to INT_MAX and a running maximum to INT_MIN, then update as you scan. For 64-bit limits there are LLONG_MAX and LLONG_MIN.

#include <climits>
int runningMax = INT_MIN;
for (int number : numbers) runningMax = max(runningMax, number);

You now have the full toolkit. Don't worry about memorizing every cost — flip back here whenever a solution uses something unfamiliar. Onward to the problems!

A. Pattern Cheat Sheet — "When You See X, Reach For Y"

This is the single highest-leverage page in the book for revision. In an interview you rarely invent a technique from scratch — you recognize a signal and pull the matching tool off the shelf. Drill this table until the right column fires automatically the moment you read the left column.

Signal / Clue in the Problem Likely Technique Example Problems (from this book)
Sorted array, find a pair / triple summing to a target Two pointers (one from each end) Two Sum II, 3Sum, Container With Most Water
Contiguous subarray / substring satisfying a constraint (longest, shortest, at-most-k) Sliding window (expand right, shrink left) Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters, Minimum Size Subarray Sum, Minimum Window Substring
"Top k", "k smallest/largest", "k closest", or a running stream Heap (priority queue), size-k Kth Largest Element, Top K Frequent, Find Median from Data Stream
Shortest path / fewest steps in an unweighted graph or grid BFS (queue, level by level) Word Ladder, Snakes and Ladders, Rotting Oranges
Shortest path with weighted edges (non-negative) Dijkstra (min-heap) Network Delay, Path With Minimum Effort
All combinations / permutations / subsets / "generate every…" Backtracking (choose → recurse → un-choose) Subsets, Permutations, Combination Sum, N-Queens
Overlapping subproblems, "number of ways", min/max over a sequence of choices Dynamic programming (memo or tabulation) Climbing Stairs, Coin Change, Longest Common Subsequence, Edit Distance
Range-sum queries, "subarray summing to k", running totals Prefix sum (+ hash map of seen sums) Range Sum Query, Subarray Sum Equals K, Product of Array Except Self
Need O(1) lookup / dedup / "have I seen this before?" Hash map / hash set Two Sum, Contains Duplicate, Group Anagrams, Longest Consecutive Sequence
Sorted (or monotonic) input + "find" / "first position" / O(log n) demanded Binary search (incl. search on the answer space) Search in Rotated Sorted Array, Find Peak Element, Koko Eating Bananas
Intervals that may overlap (merge, insert, count rooms) Sort by start (or end) then sweep Merge Intervals, Insert Interval, Meeting Rooms II
Tree, process level by level / "shortest depth" / right-side view BFS with a queue (track level size) Binary Tree Level Order, Right Side View, Minimum Depth
Tree path, sum, diameter, or any bottom-up aggregate DFS recursion (return info to parent) Path Sum, Diameter of Binary Tree, Lowest Common Ancestor
Many words share prefixes; autocomplete / prefix matching Trie (prefix tree) Implement Trie, Word Search II, Add and Search Word
Detect a cycle, find the middle, or find the nth-from-end of a linked list Fast & slow pointers (Floyd) Linked List Cycle, Middle of Linked List, Find the Duplicate Number
Pairs that cancel, single odd-one-out, counting set bits, no extra space Bit manipulation (XOR, masks, shifts) Single Number, Number of 1 Bits, Sum of Two Integers
Array values are in range [1..n] and you need O(1) extra space to mark seen In-place array as hash (negate / index marking) Find All Numbers Disappeared, First Missing Positive
"Next greater / next smaller element", spans, valid parentheses Monotonic stack Daily Temperatures, Largest Rectangle in Histogram, Valid Parentheses
Connected components, "is A reachable from B", grouping merges Union-Find (DSU) or DFS/BFS flood Number of Islands, Number of Provinces, Accounts Merge
Ordering with dependencies / "can you finish?" / build order Topological sort (Kahn's BFS or DFS) Course Schedule I & II, Alien Dictionary
Repeated max/min over a sliding range Monotonic deque Sliding Window Maximum
Reservoir / pick uniformly from a stream of unknown length Reservoir sampling Linked List Random Node

Tie-breaker heuristics — choosing between similar techniques:

  • Two pointers vs. sliding window: both crawl an array, but a window tracks a contiguous range with running state (sum, char counts) and is for "best subarray under a constraint"; two pointers converge from the ends and is for sorted-array pair/triple problems. If you need to shrink from the left while a condition breaks, it's a window.
  • Hash map vs. sorting: a hash map gives O(n) average but uses O(n) space and no ordering; sorting gives O(n log n) but unlocks two pointers and removes the need for extra space. If the problem also needs order or you're space-constrained, sort first.
  • BFS vs. DFS on graphs/grids: use BFS when you need the shortest path or fewest steps in an unweighted graph; use DFS for "does a path exist", connectivity, counting components, or anything naturally recursive. BFS uses a queue and more memory; DFS uses the call stack.
  • DP vs. greedy: greedy commits to the locally best choice and never reconsiders — only valid when a local optimum provably leads to a global one. If a greedy choice can be "undone" by a better future arrangement, you need DP. When unsure, write DP; it's harder to get wrong.
  • DP vs. backtracking: if the question asks for a count or an optimal value, lean DP; if it asks to enumerate / return every valid configuration, it's backtracking. Backtracking + memo on overlapping states often is top-down DP.
  • Heap vs. sorting for "top k": sorting is O(n log n); a size-k heap is O(n log k) and works on a stream where you can't see all data at once. Pick the heap when k ≪ n or data arrives incrementally.
  • Binary search vs. two pointers on a sorted array: two pointers when you compare two ends against a target sum; binary search when you locate a single value, a boundary, or you're searching over an answer range ("smallest capacity such that…").
  • Recursion vs. iteration: recursion is cleaner for trees and divide-and-conquer; convert to an explicit stack/queue only when recursion depth risks a stack overflow (very deep/skewed inputs) or the interviewer asks.
  • Stack vs. recursion for trees: they're equivalent; an explicit stack just makes the recursion visible. Reach for it when you want iterative traversal or fine control over order.

B. How to Crack the Coding Interview (Live Problem Strategy)

A 45-minute FAANG coding round is graded on how you think, not just whether you land the answer. The candidate who narrates a clean brute force, then optimizes, often beats the one who silently types a perfect solution. Treat the interviewer as a teammate, not an examiner.

Before you write a single line of code

  • Restate the problem in your own words. Confirm you and the interviewer mean the same thing. This catches misreads before they cost you 20 minutes.
  • Ask clarifying questions — this is expected and rewarded, not a sign of weakness:
  • Input size / range — is n up to 10, 10⁵, or 10⁹? This decides your target complexity.
  • Value ranges — can numbers be negative? zero? larger than 32-bit (overflow risk)?
  • Duplicates allowed? Is the input sorted? Already unique?
  • What about empty input, a single element, or null?
  • What exactly should I return — the value, the index, all answers, or just a boolean? In what order?
  • State your assumptions out loud ("I'll assume the array fits in memory and values are 32-bit signed") so the interviewer can correct you early.
  • Walk one small concrete example by hand. It cements your understanding and often reveals the pattern.

The UMPIRE structured approach

Lean on a checklist so nerves don't make you skip a step:

  • Understand — restate, ask the clarifying questions above, and write down inputs/outputs/constraints.
  • Match — map the problem to a known pattern using the cheat sheet in Part A ("sorted + find pair → two pointers").
  • Plan — sketch the approach in plain pseudocode or bullets before coding; get the interviewer to nod at it.
  • Implement — translate the plan to clean C++, narrating as you go.
  • Review / test — dry-run your code on the example and edge cases, fixing bugs you spot.
  • Evaluate — state final time and space complexity and whether it meets the constraints.

Think out loud — brute force first

  • Say the obvious brute force immediately, even if it's O(n²) or O(2ⁿ): "The naive approach checks every pair, that's O(n²) time, O(1) space."
  • Then explicitly look for the bottleneck and improve it: "The repeated lookup is the cost — a hash map makes each lookup O(1), bringing us to O(n)."
  • This shows range, gives you a working fallback if you run out of time, and lets the interviewer steer you. Silence is your enemy; a wrong-but-narrated idea is far better than a long pause.

Coding cleanly

  • Choose descriptive names (left, right, seen, windowSum) over single letters — readable code reads as competence.
  • Handle edge cases inline (empty container, size 1, all-equal elements) rather than hoping they don't come up.
  • Don't micro-optimize before it's correct. Get a working solution, then tighten. Premature optimization wastes your limited clock.
  • Keep helper logic in small functions if it clarifies intent; the interviewer should be able to follow top to bottom.

Testing

  • First, dry-run the provided example line by line and confirm the output matches.
  • Then deliberately throw edge cases at it: empty input, a single element, all elements identical, negative numbers, and values that could overflow (sums of large ints — consider long long).
  • Trace at least one case out loud with real values; "I'll trace nums = [2,7,11,15], target = 9…". Catching your own bug scores higher than the interviewer catching it.

When you're stuck

  • Shrink the problem: solve it for n = 2 or 3 by hand and look for the pattern that generalizes.
  • Fall back to brute force, get it working, then optimize the bottleneck — partial credit is real credit.
  • Ask: "what data structure gives me the operation I need cheaply?" Need O(1) membership → hash set. Need the running min → heap or monotonic stack. Need ordered range queries → sorted structure / binary search.
  • Re-read the constraints — the input size is a giant hint about the intended complexity (n ≤ 20 screams backtracking/bitmask; n up to 10⁵ wants O(n log n) or better).
  • Ask for a hint gracefully: "I'm weighing a heap against sorting here — do you have a preference on the trade-off?" Asking well signals collaboration, not defeat.

Complexity — always close with it

End every problem by stating both time and space complexity, and a one-line justification ("O(n) time since we pass once, O(n) space for the hash map"). Volunteering this without being asked is a hallmark of a strong candidate.

Behavioral basics

FAANG loops almost always include a behavioral round. Use the STAR method: describe the Situation and Task briefly, spend most of your time on the Action you personally took, and end with a measurable Result. Prepare 4–5 concrete stories (a conflict, a failure, a leadership moment, a tight deadline) ahead of time so you're not inventing under pressure.

C. 8-Week Study Plan & Spaced-Repetition Tracker

On a 1–3 month runway, consistency beats intensity. The plan below front-loads foundational topics, layers harder material in the middle, and reserves the final weeks for mixed mock interviews — all while continuously revisiting earlier topics so nothing decays.

Week Focus Topics Goal
1 Arrays & Strings, Two Pointers, Hashing Build fundamentals; solve 3–4 easy problems/day; get fluent with vector, unordered_map, string.
2 Sliding Window, Prefix Sum, Binary Search Master the window template and binary-search boundaries; revisit Week 1 problems once.
3 Stacks & Queues, Linked Lists, Fast/Slow Pointers Pointer manipulation without bugs; redo 5 hardest Week 1–2 problems from memory.
4 Trees (BFS & DFS), Binary Search Trees, Heaps Recursion and level-order fluency; start one timed mock per week; review flagged earlier problems.
5 Graphs (BFS/DFS, Union-Find, Topological Sort) Model grids and dependencies as graphs; re-solve 5 tree problems cold to keep them warm.
6 Backtracking, Recursion, Bit Manipulation Comfortable enumerating subsets/permutations; mix in earlier-week problems daily for revision.
7 Dynamic Programming (1D → 2D), Intervals, Greedy Recognize and set up DP states; two timed mocks this week; revisit graphs and trees.
8 Mixed review, Tries, hard problems, full mock loops Simulate real interviews end-to-end; re-solve your lowest-confidence problems; polish behavioral STAR stories.

If you have closer to 12 weeks, stretch each week into 1.5 weeks and add a dedicated "company-tagged problems" block before the final week.

Spaced repetition — why and how

Your brain forgets on a curve. Reviewing a problem just as you're about to forget it resets that curve and pushes the memory into long-term storage — far more efficient than re-grinding everything constantly. The trick is spacing reviews at expanding intervals.

A simple, proven cadence for any problem you've solved:

  • +1 day — re-solve from a blank editor (not re-read).
  • +3 days — re-solve again; if it flows, lengthen the next gap.
  • +1 week — re-solve; flag it if you stumbled.
  • +2 weeks — final review. If you solve it cleanly twice in a row at this spacing, consider it mastered and retire it from active rotation.

Rule of thumb: if you can't re-derive the approach within ~2 minutes, the interval was too long — shorten it and review again sooner.

Mastery Tracker (photocopy / reprint this page)

Keep one row per problem. Bump Confidence up only when you solve it cleanly without hints, and let it decay your sense of when to review next. Reprint or photocopy this page so you always have blank rows — you'll fill several sheets over 8 weeks.

Problem First Solved (date) Confidence (1–5) Last Reviewed Next Review
Two Sum 2026-06-22 4 2026-06-25 2026-06-29
Course Schedule II 2026-06-23 2 2026-06-24 2026-06-26
_____ ____ ___ ____ ____
_____ ____ ___ ____ ____
_____ ____ ___ ____ ____

Mindset for the 1–3 month grind

  • Consistency over cramming. Two focused hours daily beats a 14-hour weekend binge — spaced practice is how memory sticks.
  • Quality over quantity. Deeply understanding 150 problems and their patterns beats half-solving 500. Always ask "what pattern was this, and what was the signal?"
  • Re-solve, don't re-read. Reading a solution feels productive but builds nothing. If you can't type it from a blank screen, you don't know it yet.
  • Track and confront weakness. Your low-confidence rows in the tracker are exactly where your interview will go wrong — give them disproportionate attention.
  • Simulate pressure early. Do timed mocks (with someone, or out loud to a rubber duck) starting Week 4. The skill of talking while coding is separate from solving and must be rehearsed.
  • Sleep before you grind. Memory consolidation happens during sleep; an extra hour of practice at the cost of sleep is a net loss the day before an interview.
  • A plateau is normal. Around Week 4–5 progress feels flat — that's the patterns reorganizing in your head, not you failing. Keep showing up.
  • Rejection is data, not a verdict. Most strong engineers fail loops before they pass one. Debrief each one, patch the gap, and re-apply.
